hyperedges.label: Problem-First Validation Pipeline
hyperedges.relation: All converge on starting with real customer problems (not solutions), validating through direct conversation, and understanding the job the customer is hiring for.
All advocate starting with a narrow, deep market (few users who desperately want the product) before expanding — the fundamental shape of successful startup go-to-market.

All involve deliberately shaping how the market perceives a new solution — from creating entire new categories (Play Bigger), to positioning within existing ones (Dunford), to building complete solutions for pragmatists (Moore), to noticing gaps from the future (Graham).

hyperedges.label: PMF Convergence Zone
hyperedges.description: Andreessen's PMF definition, Seibel's overwhelmed-with-demand signals, Sean Ellis's 40% rule, and Superhuman's PMF engine converge on the same core concept: PMF is measurable (40%+ very disappointed), experiential (constraint is capacity not demand), and optimizable (segment, double down, iterate). These four frameworks represent the evolution of PMF from concept to operationalization.

hyperedges.label: Go-to-Market System (Bansal Synthesis)
hyperedges.description: Product-Market-Sales Fit integrates the sandwich strategy (bottoms-up + tops-down), a 3-phase sales learning curve, a 2/3–1/3 engineering allocation, 10–15% services sweet spot, and stage-specific pricing. Together these form a complete system where product features, sales motion, engineering investment, pricing, and services align.

hyperedges.label: Seibel Unified Early-Stage Framework
hyperedges.description: Seibel's Order of Operations, Pre-Launch Sequence, User Conversation Method, and Screwdriver Analogy form a coherent system: 10 steps from problem discovery to validation, 4 pre-launch goals, a structured conversation method, and the screwdriver metaphor for iteration. All anchor on the same principle: hold problem/customer tight, solution loose.

nodes.label: Organic Startup Ideas
nodes.rationale: Startup ideas that grow naturally out of the founders' own experiences rather than being deliberately brainstormed. The most successful startups almost all begin this way.

nodes.label: Live in the Future, Then Build What's Missing
nodes.rationale: Combines Paul Buchheit's observation that people at the leading edge of rapidly changing fields 'live in the future' with Robert Pirsig's advice: become the sort of person who notices gaps naturally.
nodes.norm_label: live in the future, then build what's missing

nodes.label: Made-Up / Sitcom Startup Ideas
nodes.rationale: Bad startup ideas that sound plausible enough to fool founders into working on them. They arise from deliberately trying to think of startup ideas rather than noticing real problems. Characterized by broad, shallow demand.

nodes.label: Well vs. Hole Metaphor
nodes.rationale: Good startup ideas are like wells (narrow and deep) — a small number of people want them a large amount. Bad ideas are like broad shallow holes — many people want them a small amount. Nearly all good startup ideas are wells.

nodes.label: Notice Ideas, Don't Think Them Up
nodes.rationale: The verb for startup ideas is 'notice' not 'think up'. Successful founders' ideas come from external stimuli hitting a prepared mind, not from brainstorming sessions.
nodes.norm_label: notice ideas, don't think them up

nodes.label: Clash of Domains
nodes.rationale: Particularly fruitful source of startup ideas. When you know programming and learn another field, you see problems software could solve that domain insiders take for granted.

nodes.label: Toys Become Great Startups
nodes.rationale: Projects dismissed as 'toys' often contain everything a good idea needs except importance. Microcomputers, BackRub (Google), and Facebook all seemed like toys initially. YC treats 'toy' labeling as positive evidence an idea is good.

nodes.label: The Unsexy Filter
nodes.rationale: Founders avoid working on problems they find unsexy or boring despite those problems being valuable. Unlike the schlep filter, the unsexy filter has some correlation with real value as founders gain experience.

nodes.label: The Schlep Filter
nodes.rationale: Founders unconsciously avoid ideas that involve tedious, unpleasant work (schleps). Turning it off is more important than the unsexy filter because the schlep filter is more likely to be an illusion.

nodes.label: Schlep Blindness
nodes.rationale: Unconscious avoidance of startup ideas that involve painful, tedious work. The unconscious mind literally prevents you from seeing these opportunities. Stripe is the canonical example: thousands of programmers knew payment processing was broken but nobody tried to fix it.

nodes.label: Do Things That Don't Scale
nodes.rationale: Founders must manually push startups into motion — recruiting users one at a time, delighting early customers with extreme attentiveness. Startups don't take off by themselves; founders make them take off through unscalable effort.

nodes.label: Collison Installation
nodes.rationale: Aggressive user acquisition technique invented by Stripe's founders: when someone agreed to try Stripe, instead of sending a link, they'd say 'give me your laptop' and set them up on the spot.

nodes.label: Contained Fire Strategy
nodes.rationale: Focus on a deliberately narrow market first to get it really hot (like containing a fire) before adding more logs. Facebook starting with just Harvard is the canonical example.

nodes.label: Insanely Great User Experience
nodes.rationale: Steve Jobs' phrase applied to early-stage startups: the experience of being your user should be insanely great, not just the product. For larval startups, attentiveness compensates for incomplete products.

nodes.label: Startup Ideas as Vectors
nodes.rationale: Startup ideas should be thought of as pairs: what you're going to build PLUS the unscalable thing(s) you'll do to get the company going. Both components contribute to the company's DNA.

nodes.label: Startups in 13 Sentences
nodes.rationale: Paul Graham's distillation of startup advice into 13 principles: pick good cofounders, launch fast, let your idea evolve, understand your users, make few users love you, offer surprisingly good customer service, you make what you measure, spend little, get ramen profitable, avoid distractions, don't get demoralized, don't give up, deals fall through.

nodes.label: Ramen Profitable
nodes.rationale: A startup makes just enough to pay the founders' living expenses. Changes relationship with investors and is great for morale. Not rapid prototyping for business models but a way of hacking the investment process.

nodes.label: Better to Make a Few Users Love You Than Many Ambivalent
nodes.rationale: Initially choose between satisfying all needs of a subset or a subset of needs of all users. Take the first. Easier to expand userwise than satisfaction-wise. Harder to lie to yourself about user count vs product quality. Learned from Paul Buchheit.
nodes.norm_label: better to make a few users love you than many ambivalent

nodes.label: Playing House
nodes.rationale: Young founders go through the motions of starting a startup (raise money, rent office, hire people) without making something people want. Caused by training in gaming systems (school, college admissions) that stops working in startups.

nodes.label: Gaming the System Stops Working
nodes.rationale: Startups are impersonal as physics — there is no boss to trick, only users who care whether the product does what they want. Fundraising tricks don't substitute for making something people want. A core counterintuitive truth about startups.

nodes.label: Just Learn
nodes.rationale: The ultimate advice for young would-be founders boiled down to two words. Instead of studying entrepreneurship, learn powerful things driven by genuine curiosity. Domain expertise is what matters, not startup mechanics.

nodes.label: Startups Are Counterintuitive
nodes.rationale: Like skiing, startups require suppressing natural instincts. Trusting your gut leads to mistakes. YC partners' function is to tell founders things they will ignore because the advice contradicts intuitions.

nodes.label: 10 Key Questions to Evaluate Startup Ideas
nodes.rationale: Jared Friedman's structured checklist: founder-market fit, market size, problem acuity, competition presence, personal want, recent possibility/necessity, proxy existence, long-term interest, scalability, good idea space. A strong idea doesn't need to pass all ten.
nodes.norm_label: 10 key questions to evaluate startup ideas

nodes.label: Founder-Market Fit
nodes.rationale: The most important criterion for evaluating an idea: are you and your team the right people for this specific idea? Reframe from 'is this a good idea?' to 'is this a good idea for MY team?' Exemplified by PlanGrid: Tracy had construction expertise, Ralph had developer skills.

nodes.label: Tarpit Ideas
nodes.rationale: Recurring startup concepts that look attractive but have hidden structural reasons why they never succeed. Canonical example: app for friends to plan meetups — submitted to YC for 20+ years with no success. The fix: Google past attempts, talk to those founders, name the specific structural barrier.

nodes.label: SISP — Solution In Search of a Problem
nodes.rationale: Most common mistake: working backward from a technology to find a problem instead of starting with a real problem. The fix: fall in love with a real problem first. Good starting problems are specific and tractable.
nodes.norm_label: sisp — solution in search of a problem

nodes.label: Idea Space
nodes.rationale: One level of abstraction out from a particular startup idea — a class of closely related ideas (e.g., fintech infrastructure, vertical SaaS). Different idea spaces have wildly different hit rates. A fertile space lets you drift into adjacent ideas without starting over if your initial idea fails. Exemplified by Fivetran.

nodes.label: Boring Ideas Have Higher Hit Rate
nodes.rationale: Boring ideas (tax accounting, payroll, compliance) have higher success rates than exciting ones (restaurant discovery, music recommendation) because they are systematically under-explored. The day-to-day reality of building a startup is largely the same regardless of how glamorous the idea sounds.
nodes.norm_label: boring ideas have higher hit rate

nodes.label: Seven Recipes for Generating Startup Ideas
nodes.rationale: Ordered by likelihood of producing a good idea: (1) Start with team expertise, (2) Start with personally encountered problem, (3) Think of things you wish existed, (4) Look for recent changes, (5) Find new variants of successful companies, (6) Talk to people about their problems, (7) Look for broken big industries.
nodes.norm_label: seven recipes for generating startup ideas

nodes.label: Three Counterintuitive Signs of a Good Idea
nodes.rationale: Ideas that are hard to get started (schlep blindness protects from competition), ideas in boring spaces (systematically under-explored), ideas with existing competitors (competition proves the problem is real and the market exists).
nodes.norm_label: three counterintuitive signs of a good idea

nodes.label: Startup Idea as Growth Hypothesis
nodes.rationale: A startup idea is a hypothesis about why a company could grow quickly. Decomposes into three parts: Problem (initial conditions), Solution (the experiment), Insight (the explanation — your unfair advantage).

nodes.label: Five Unfair Advantages
nodes.rationale: Founder advantage (top 10 in world for this), Market Growth (20%+ YoY), Product Superiority (10X better), Acquisition Advantage (free growth via word of mouth), Monopoly/Network Effects (stronger as you grow). Need at least one.

nodes.label: Threshold Belief vs. Miracle Belief
nodes.rationale: Threshold belief: minimal assumption for any chance of success (usually 'can they build it?'). Miracle belief: the ambitious assumption that if true means breakout success (usually sales, distribution, or behavior change). YC partners spend most diligence on the miracle belief.

nodes.label: 10X Better Product
nodes.rationale: Product superiority must be an order of magnitude better — 10X faster, cheaper, or simpler. A 2X or 3X improvement is not a slam dunk. Someone should look at it and immediately think it's dramatically better than alternatives.

nodes.label: Six Problem Quality Characteristics
nodes.rationale: Good problems are: Popular (millions of users), Growing (20%+ YoY), Urgent (must solve now), Expensive (billion-dollar TAM), Mandatory (regulation/compliance), Frequent (multiple times per day). Aim for at least one.

nodes.label: The Perfect Idea Trap
nodes.rationale: Founders get stuck trying to find the perfect idea before committing. The perfect idea doesn't exist in the abstract — you can only figure out what to work on by making contact with reality and getting feedback from customers. Two failure modes: waiting for perfect idea, and questioning whether you're the perfect founder.

nodes.label: Burn the Other Boats
nodes.rationale: Explicitly foreclose other startup options. Change company name, emails, website, internal narrative. Going deep should feel like 'wearing a new skin'. Exemplified by GovDash which pivoted 5 times, changing identity each time.

nodes.label: Could You Run Your Customer's Business?
nodes.rationale: The litmus test for going deep: if dropped into the customer's business tomorrow, would you know how to run it? Another framing: could you teach a class on the problem? Are you one of the most informed people in the world on the subject?
nodes.norm_label: could you run your customer's business?

nodes.label: Verticalize — Sell Outcomes, Not Software
nodes.rationale: In the AI era with software cost approaching zero, value shifts to customer trust, licenses, regulatory permission, and outcome ownership. Don't build software for insurers — be the insurer. Exemplified by Corgi Insurance acquiring an insurance carrier during YC batch.

nodes.label: Sit at the Edge of What Models Can Do
nodes.rationale: Product should barely work on today's frontier models but will clearly improve as they get better. Understand bottlenecks intimately — solving one might become the company. A version of Paul Graham's 'live in the future, build what's missing'.
nodes.norm_label: sit at the edge of what models can do

nodes.label: Walk Fast in One Direction
nodes.rationale: In the early idea fog, commit to one direction and walk fast rather than taking cautious steps in every direction. Generates more information per unit of time. The worst failure mode is not being wrong — it's not making a decision.

nodes.label: The Idea Maze
nodes.rationale: Good startup ideas are well-developed, multi-year plans contemplating many possible paths based on how the world changes. A good founder anticipates which turns lead to treasure and which to death. Coined the term, attributed to Balaji Srinivasan. Four sources: history, analogy, theories, direct experience.

nodes.label: History Beats Stealth Mode
nodes.rationale: Staying in stealth mode prevents learning from prior attempts. The benefits of shared knowledge about the maze far outweigh the risks of having your idea stolen. Almost all good ideas have been tried before.

nodes.label: Competition Is a Distraction
nodes.rationale: Competition from other startups is usually just a distraction — they won't take the same path and their presence means you might be onto something. The real enemy is the years you could waste going down the wrong path in the maze.

nodes.label: YC Requests for Startups (RFS)
nodes.rationale: YC tradition where partners publicly share startup ideas. Each RFS argues a specific enabling condition just flipped (technology, cost curve, regulatory shift), creating a window that wasn't open before. Summer 2026 framing: AI has stopped being a feature and started being the foundation.

nodes.label: Company Brain
nodes.rationale: A living map of how a company works — extracts knowledge from fragmented sources, structures it, keeps it current, and turns it into executable skills files for AI agents. The missing layer between raw company data and reliable AI automation.

nodes.label: AI-Native SaaS Challengers
nodes.rationale: AI has collapsed the cost of producing software by 10-100x. The moat protecting legacy SaaS (millions of lines of code built over decades) is gone. Next generation built by replacing legacy SaaS with AI-native software.

nodes.label: Software for Agents / Make Something Agents Want
nodes.rationale: The next trillion internet users will be AI agents. They need machine-readable interfaces (APIs, MCPs, CLIs) instead of visual UIs. Every major software category needs to be rebuilt for agents as first-class citizens.
nodes.norm_label: software for agents / make something agents want

nodes.label: AI-Native Service Companies
nodes.rationale: AI companies that don't sell software — they sell the service. Instead of giving you a tool, they just do the work. Total spend on services is many times larger than software spend. Targets: insurance brokerage, accounting, compliance, healthcare admin.

nodes.label: Positioning as Context Setting
nodes.source_file: sources/April Dunford - Obviously awesome _bhow to nail product positioning so customers get it, buy it, love it (2019, Ambient Press) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Positioning is deliberately defining how you are the best at something that a defined market cares a lot about. Context enables people to figure out what's important — like the opening scene of a movie. Joshua Bell subway experiment demonstrates that even world-class products fail when poorly positioned.

nodes.label: 10-Step Positioning Process
nodes.source_file: sources/April Dunford - Obviously awesome _bhow to nail product positioning so customers get it, buy it, love it (2019, Ambient Press) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Systematic methodology: (1) Understand customers who love you, (2) Form positioning team, (3) Align vocabulary, (4) List competitive alternatives, (5) Isolate unique attributes, (6) Map attributes to value themes, (7) Determine who cares a lot, (8) Find market frame of reference, (9) Layer on a trend, (10) Capture positioning for sharing. The only book offering a clear process rather than just theory.

nodes.label: Five (Plus One) Components of Effective Positioning
nodes.source_file: sources/April Dunford - Obviously awesome _bhow to nail product positioning so customers get it, buy it, love it (2019, Ambient Press) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Components that positioning must account for: customer's point of view on the problem, competitive alternatives, unique differentiators, characteristics of best-fit customers, best market context. Plus one: trends layered on top.
nodes.norm_label: five (plus one) components of effective positioning

nodes.label: Two Positioning Traps
nodes.source_file: sources/April Dunford - Obviously awesome _bhow to nail product positioning so customers get it, buy it, love it (2019, Ambient Press) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Trap 1: Product has evolved beyond what you intended to build but you're still positioning it as the original thing (cake became muffins). Trap 2: The market has changed around your product but you haven't adjusted positioning (diet muffin when market shifted to paleo). Both solved by deliberate rather than default positioning.

nodes.label: Critique of Traditional Positioning Statements
nodes.source_file: sources/April Dunford - Obviously awesome _bhow to nail product positioning so customers get it, buy it, love it (2019, Ambient Press) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: The fill-in-the-blank positioning statement template (For X, Y is a Z that provides A unlike B which provides C) is ineffective and potentially dangerous. It confuses writing down positioning with actually doing the work of positioning. Widely taught in business schools but doesn't work.

nodes.label: Repositioning: Database to Data Warehouse
nodes.source_file: sources/April Dunford - Obviously awesome _bhow to nail product positioning so customers get it, buy it, love it (2019, Ambient Press) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Case study: a specialized database repositioned as a data warehouse. As a database it was compared unfavorably to incumbents; as a data warehouse it blew away competitors. Changing context changed how customers evaluated the product and transformed the business.

nodes.label: The Mom Test
nodes.source_file: sources/Mom Test_ How to talk to customers & learn if yeveryone is lying to you, The - Rob Fitzpatrick.pdf
nodes.rationale: A set of rules for crafting customer interview questions that even your mom can't lie to you about. Three rules: (1) Talk about their life instead of your idea, (2) Ask about specifics in the past instead of generics or opinions about the future, (3) Talk less and listen more. Customer conversations are bad by default — it's your job to fix them.

nodes.label: Three Types of Bad Data in Customer Conversations
nodes.source_file: sources/Mom Test_ How to talk to customers & learn if yeveryone is lying to you, The - Rob Fitzpatrick.pdf
nodes.rationale: Compliments (vacuous, worthless), Fluff (generics, hypotheticals, future-tense promises like 'I would definitely buy that'), and Ideas/Feature Requests (need digging to understand underlying motivations). Compliments are the fool's gold of customer learning.
nodes.norm_label: three types of bad data in customer conversations

nodes.label: Deflect Compliments
nodes.source_file: sources/Mom Test_ How to talk to customers & learn if yeveryone is lying to you, The - Rob Fitzpatrick.pdf
nodes.rationale: When customers give compliments, deflect them and pivot back to factual questions about their current behavior. The best way to escape misinformation is to avoid mentioning your idea entirely. Symptoms: saying 'thanks', 'I'm glad you like it', or back at office reporting 'that meeting went really well'.

nodes.label: Anchor Fluff to Specifics
nodes.source_file: sources/Mom Test_ How to talk to customers & learn if yeveryone is lying to you, The - Rob Fitzpatrick.pdf
nodes.rationale: When someone gives generic claims ('I always', 'I never'), future promises ('I would', 'I will'), or hypotheticals ('I might'), anchor them back to specifics in the past. Ask when it last happened, talk me through it, how did you solve it. The world's most deadly fluff: 'I would definitely buy that.'

nodes.label: Dig Beneath Feature Requests and Emotional Signals
nodes.source_file: sources/Mom Test_ How to talk to customers & learn if yeveryone is lying to you, The - Rob Fitzpatrick.pdf
nodes.rationale: When customers offer ideas or feature requests, dig to understand motivations rather than adding to the todo list. Questions: 'Why do you want that?', 'What would that let you do?', 'How are you coping without it?'. Same for emotional signals — strong emotion is worth exploring.
nodes.norm_label: dig beneath feature requests and emotional signals

nodes.label: Commitment and Advancement
nodes.source_file: sources/Mom Test_ How to talk to customers & learn if yeveryone is lying to you, The - Rob Fitzpatrick.pdf
nodes.rationale: The real validation comes from commitment, not compliments. If you're far enough along, ask for money (deposit, pre-order). Real commitments (time, reputation risk, money) separate genuine interest from polite enthusiasm. Framework for advancing conversations toward concrete outcomes.

nodes.label: They Own the Problem, You Own the Solution
nodes.source_file: sources/Mom Test_ How to talk to customers & learn if yeveryone is lying to you, The - Rob Fitzpatrick.pdf
nodes.rationale: Customers aren't allowed to tell you what to build, and you aren't allowed to tell them what their problem is. Deciding what to build is your job. The questions to ask are about their lives: problems, cares, constraints, goals — then take your own visionary leap.
nodes.norm_label: they own the problem, you own the solution

nodes.label: Jobs to Be Done Theory
nodes.source_file: sources/Christensen, Clayton M._Dillon, Karen_Duncan, David S._Hall, Tad - Competing Against Luck_ The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice (2016, HarperCollins_ HarperBusiness) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Customers 'hire' products to get specific jobs done in their lives. If the product does the job well, they hire it again; if not, they 'fire' it. Focuses on the causal mechanism behind purchase decisions rather than correlation. Innovation is about progress, not products. The core question: 'What job did you hire that product to do?'

nodes.label: The Milk Shake Dilemma
nodes.source_file: sources/Christensen, Clayton M._Dillon, Karen_Duncan, David S._Hall, Tad - Competing Against Luck_ The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice (2016, HarperCollins_ HarperBusiness) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Foundational JTBD case study: morning commuters hired milk shakes to make long boring commutes interesting and stave off mid-morning hunger (competing with bananas, bagels, donuts). Afternoon parents hired the same milk shakes to placate children and feel like a good parent (competing with toy store trips). Same product, two completely different jobs, different competitors, different improvement criteria.

nodes.label: Progress, Not Products
nodes.source_file: sources/Christensen, Clayton M._Dillon, Karen_Duncan, David S._Hall, Tad - Competing Against Luck_ The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice (2016, HarperCollins_ HarperBusiness) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: The core reframe of innovation: customers don't buy products — they pull solutions into their lives to make progress. Innovation success comes from understanding the struggle for progress, not from improving product attributes. Data about customer demographics doesn't reveal causality.

nodes.label: Competing Against Luck
nodes.source_file: sources/Christensen, Clayton M._Dillon, Karen_Duncan, David S._Hall, Tad - Competing Against Luck_ The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice (2016, HarperCollins_ HarperBusiness) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Central thesis: innovation can be predictable and profitable when you understand the causal mechanism of customer choice (Jobs to Be Done). Companies that build on this foundation compete against luck; everyone else relies on it. The book provides the practical methodology to apply JTBD to innovation.

nodes.label: Disruptive Innovation Theory
nodes.source_file: sources/Christensen, Clayton M._Dillon, Karen_Duncan, David S._Hall, Tad - Competing Against Luck_ The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice (2016, HarperCollins_ HarperBusiness) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Clayton Christensen's earlier theory: innovation transforms markets by introducing simplicity, convenience, accessibility, and affordability where complexity and high cost are status quo. Explains competitive response but does NOT tell you where to look for new opportunities — that gap is filled by Jobs to Be Done.

nodes.label: Technology Adoption Life Cycle
nodes.source_file: sources/Crossing the Chasm, 3rd Edition - Geoffrey A.] Geoffrey A. Moore [Moore & cj5.pdf
nodes.rationale: Bell-curve model describing market penetration of new technology products through five psychographic segments: Innovators, Early Adopters, Early Majority, Late Majority, Laggards. Each group has distinct characteristics and responds differently to discontinuous innovations. Originated from research on seed potato adoption among American farmers.

nodes.label: The Chasm
nodes.source_file: sources/Crossing the Chasm, 3rd Edition - Geoffrey A.] Geoffrey A. Moore [Moore & cj5.pdf
nodes.rationale: The gap between the early market (innovators and early adopters) and the mainstream market (early majority and beyond). The point of greatest peril in high-tech market development. Early adopters buy based on vision; the early majority demands references from peers. Crossing this chasm requires fundamentally different marketing strategies.

nodes.label: D-Day Analogy for Chasm Crossing
nodes.source_file: sources/Crossing the Chasm, 3rd Edition - Geoffrey A.] Geoffrey A. Moore [Moore & cj5.pdf
nodes.rationale: Crossing the chasm is like the D-Day invasion: target a specific point of attack (beachhead segment), assemble an invasion force (whole product), define the battle (positioning against competition), and launch the invasion (distribution and pricing). Requires unusual company unity.

nodes.label: Whole Product Model
nodes.source_file: sources/Crossing the Chasm, 3rd Edition - Geoffrey A.] Geoffrey A. Moore [Moore & cj5.pdf
nodes.rationale: To satisfy the pragmatic early majority, a product must be a completely configured solution — the generic product plus everything else needed for the customer to achieve their compelling reason to buy. No single vendor can provide it alone; requires partnerships and ecosystem.

nodes.label: Beachhead Segment
nodes.source_file: sources/Crossing the Chasm, 3rd Edition - Geoffrey A.] Geoffrey A. Moore [Moore & cj5.pdf
nodes.rationale: A specific niche market segment targeted first when crossing the chasm. Must be winnable, provide a base for expansion, and be large enough to matter. The pragmatic early majority in one niche can serve as reference for adjacent niches.

nodes.label: Category Kings
nodes.rationale: Companies that create, develop, and dominate entirely new categories of business. They don't just sell better products — they sell different. Category kings typically capture 70-80% of their category's profits and market value. Examples: Google, Facebook, Uber, Salesforce, IKEA, Elvis Presley (rock and roll). Once established, almost impossible to dislodge.

nodes.label: Category Design
nodes.rationale: The emerging discipline of creating a great product, a great company, and a great category at the same time. Involves: defining a point of view (POV), mobilizing the ecosystem, conditioning the market (lightning strike marketing). Contrasts with product design (1980s) and experience design (2000s).

nodes.label: Point of View (POV)
nodes.rationale: Part of category design strategy: articulating a unique perspective on the problem and how the world should be different. Replaces the customer's current point of view with a new one. Makes what came before seem outdated or painful. Not just better — different.

nodes.label: Lightning Strike Marketing
nodes.rationale: Category design tactic: concentrated, high-impact marketing that conditions the market to accept a new category. Makes customers aware they have a problem and that a new solution exists. Part of the mobilization phase of category design.

nodes.label: Thunder Lizards
nodes.rationale: Mike Maples' term for category kings: game-changing companies that massively outperform the industry. Of 10,000 angel-funded startups in a year, only ~12 become thunder lizards. Rare but capture almost all the economics.

nodes.label: Category King Economics
nodes.rationale: Winner-take-all dynamics where the category king captures 70-80% of profits and market cap. Driven by networks, cloud distribution, social media word-of-mouth. Flywheel effects: best data, best talent, best partners, best investors — gap widens over time. Apple captured 93% of smartphone industry profits as category king.

nodes.label: Creation Wins, Disruption Is a By-Product
nodes.rationale: Legendary companies don't set out to disrupt — they set out to create. Disruption is the by-product of creation, not the goal. Elvis didn't set out to disrupt jazz; he created rock and roll. The goal is creation, not attacking incumbents.
nodes.norm_label: creation wins, disruption is a by-product

nodes.label: Category King Flywheel
nodes.rationale: Once a company becomes category king, a self-reinforcing cycle begins: category leadership attracts best data → better insights → better products → more customers → more data. Also attracts best talent, partners, investors, and acquisition targets. Gap between king and followers widens continuously.

nodes.label: Paul Graham
nodes.rationale: Co-founder of Y Combinator, Viaweb. Prolific essayist on startups. Originated concepts: organic startup ideas, schlep blindness, do things that don't scale, ramen profitable, maker's schedule, and many other foundational startup frameworks.

nodes.label: Jared Friedman
nodes.rationale: YC Group Partner. Created the 10 Key Questions framework, identified tarpit ideas and idea spaces, developed 7 recipes for generating startup ideas. Draws from quantitative analysis of YC top 100 companies.

nodes.label: Kevin Hale
nodes.rationale: YC Partner, co-founder of Wufoo. Created the startup idea hypothesis framework (problem/solution/insight), five unfair advantages, and threshold vs miracle belief distinction.

nodes.label: Jon Xu
nodes.rationale: YC General Partner. Authored 'How To Pick A Startup Idea' covering the perfect idea trap, burn the boats, going deep with the litmus test, and three qualities of good AI-era ideas. Also authored YC RFS on AI-Native Discovery Engines.

nodes.label: Chris Dixon
nodes.rationale: A16Z General Partner. Coined 'The Idea Maze' concept in 2013, shifting the conversation from 'ideas vs execution' to 'well-developed ideas vs shallow ideas'. One of the most influential mental models in startup circles.

nodes.label: April Dunford
nodes.source_file: sources/April Dunford - Obviously awesome _bhow to nail product positioning so customers get it, buy it, love it (2019, Ambient Press) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Positioning expert, author of 'Obviously Awesome'. Developed the 10-Step Positioning Process and the 5+1 components framework. Argues positioning is context-setting and most products fail because they never deliberately position. Draws from repositioning 16 products as an executive.

nodes.label: Rob Fitzpatrick
nodes.source_file: sources/Mom Test_ How to talk to customers & learn if yeveryone is lying to you, The - Rob Fitzpatrick.pdf
nodes.rationale: Author of 'The Mom Test', a practical handbook on customer conversations. Created rules for asking questions that even your mom can't lie about. Self-described introverted techie who learned sales from scratch. Co-founded Habit and foundercentric.com.

nodes.label: Clayton Christensen
nodes.source_file: sources/Christensen, Clayton M._Dillon, Karen_Duncan, David S._Hall, Tad - Competing Against Luck_ The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice (2016, HarperCollins_ HarperBusiness) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Harvard Business School professor. Originated Disruptive Innovation Theory and Jobs to Be Done Theory. Author of 'The Innovator's Dilemma' and 'Competing Against Luck'. Argues that correlation-based data analysis misses the causal mechanism of customer choice.

nodes.label: Geoffrey Moore
nodes.source_file: sources/Crossing the Chasm, 3rd Edition - Geoffrey A.] Geoffrey A. Moore [Moore & cj5.pdf
nodes.rationale: Author of 'Crossing the Chasm', the foundational text on high-tech market development. Former English professor turned marketing consultant at Regis McKenna Inc. Originated the Technology Adoption Life Cycle application to high-tech, the chasm concept, whole product model, and D-Day analogy for market entry.

nodes.label: Al Ramadan, Christopher Lochhead, Dave Peterson, Kevin Maney
nodes.rationale: Authors of 'Play Bigger' and founders of advisory firm Play Bigger. Originated the concepts of category design, category kings, POV, and lightning strike. Each brought complementary superpowers: Christopher (creative frontman), Al (analytical), Dave (execution). Kevin Maney (journalist/author) helped shape and research the ideas.
nodes.norm_label: al ramadan, christopher lochhead, dave peterson, kevin maney

nodes.label: Balaji Srinivasan
nodes.rationale: Attributed with the original 'idea maze' concept as cited by Chris Dixon. Described good founders as capable of anticipating which turns lead to treasure and which to certain death in the maze.

nodes.label: Paul Buchheit
nodes.rationale: Creator of Gmail, YC partner. Coined 'live in the future' observation. Originated the principle 'better to make a few people really happy than a lot of people semi-happy'. Advocated selling bad products ASAP to discover real ideas.

nodes.label: Bob Moesta
nodes.source_file: sources/Christensen, Clayton M._Dillon, Karen_Duncan, David S._Hall, Tad - Competing Against Luck_ The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice (2016, HarperCollins_ HarperBusiness) - libgen.li.pdf
nodes.rationale: Consultant and collaborator with Clayton Christensen on Jobs to Be Done. Conducted the original milk shake research that became the foundational JTBD case study. His consulting work now focuses exclusively on Jobs Theory.

nodes.label: Mike Maples
nodes.rationale: Silicon Valley legendary investor who coined 'thunder lizards' as his term for category kings. Teaches at Stanford University. Investment philosophy seeks game-changing companies that massively outperform the industry.

nodes.label: Peter Thiel
nodes.rationale: Super-investor, author of 'Zero to One'. Celebrated monopolies as unique problem-solving companies. Argued all happy companies earn a monopoly by solving a unique problem; all failed companies are the same — they failed to escape competition. His description maps directly to category kings.

nodes.label: Steve Blank
nodes.source_file: sources/Steve Blank - The Startup Owner's Manual.pdf

nodes.label: Bob Dorf
nodes.source_file: sources/Steve Blank - The Startup Owner's Manual.pdf

nodes.label: Kim Christian Hvidkjaer
nodes.source_file: sources/How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf

nodes.label: Product-Market Fit (PMF)
nodes.rationale: Marc Andreessen defined PMF as being in a good market with a product that can satisfy that market. The concept was originally developed and named by Andy Rachleff based on analysis of Don Valentine's Sequoia investing style. It is the central organizing principle for early-stage startups: before PMF, founders should obsessively focus on achieving it; after PMF, they can scale.

nodes.label: Rachleff's Law of Startup Success
nodes.rationale: When a great team meets a lousy market, market wins. When a lousy team meets a great market, market wins. When a great team meets a great market, something special happens. Markets matter more than teams or products.

nodes.label: Market Primacy Principle
nodes.rationale: In a great market, the market pulls product out of the startup. The product doesn't need to be great; it just has to basically work. Conversely, in a terrible market, the best product and team fail. This is the foundational argument of Andreessen's essay: market matters most.

nodes.label: Before/After Product-Market Fit (BPMF/APMF)
nodes.rationale: Andreessen divides startup life into two phases: BPMF (obsessively focus on achieving PMF, do whatever is required ignoring everything else) and APMF (optimize, hire specialists, build moats). Hiring before PMF slows you down; hiring after PMF speeds you up.

nodes.label: Overwhelmed with Demand (True PMF Signal)
nodes.rationale: Seibel argues that real PMF is when you are overwhelmed with usage—the constraint on growth is your capacity to deliver, not demand. The founding team should look slightly exhausted, making trade-offs between shipping features and keeping the lights on. If you have the luxury of spending weeks on a redesign, you probably don't have PMF.

nodes.label: Concrete Signals of True PMF
nodes.rationale: Seibel's five concrete signals: (1) users would be genuinely upset if you shut down, (2) waiting list grows faster than you can serve it, (3) exponential growth without massive marketing spend, (4) strong retention with users returning daily, (5) inbound requests arrive faster than capacity.

nodes.label: The 40% Rule (Sean Ellis PMF Survey)
nodes.rationale: Sean Ellis discovered that if 40% or more of users answer 'Very disappointed' to 'How would you feel if you could no longer use the product?', the company has PMF. Companies struggling to grow had less than 40%; those with strong traction exceeded it. This is a leading indicator, not a lagging one.

nodes.label: Superhuman PMF Engine
nodes.rationale: Rahul Vohra's 4-step systematic PMF engine: (1) Segment to find supporters—identify high-expectation customers (HXCs), (2) Analyze feedback to convert on-the-fence users, (3) Split roadmap: half doubling down on what users love, half addressing what holds others back, (4) Repeat quarterly, tracking PMF score. Superhuman went from 22% to 58% 'very disappointed' using this method.

nodes.label: Founder-Market Fit
nodes.rationale: The prerequisite for sustainable PMF: the founder brings an authentic, non-replicable advantage to the problem—deep domain expertise, personal frustration, or unique insight. Three dimensions: personal experience, skill alignment, and obsession. Without founder-market fit, no amount of product iteration will save you.

nodes.label: Premature Scaling
nodes.rationale: Coined by Steve Blank, premature scaling means spending significant money on growth before PMF is achieved. Startup Genome found 70% of startups scale prematurely and that startups need 2-3x longer to validate their market than founders expect. It is one of the most lethal startup mistakes.

nodes.label: One Order of Operations (Seibel)
nodes.rationale: A 10-step sequential framework for first-time founders to go from 'no idea' to launched MVP with users. Each step builds on the previous one; skipping steps is the most common cause of early-stage failure. Key steps: find a problem (not an idea), brainstorm with smart friends, validate before building, MVP in a week, launch immediately, get users manually, talk to users extensively, fall in love with problem.

nodes.label: The Screwdriver Analogy
nodes.rationale: Seibel's core metaphor for pre-launch: hold the problem tightly, hold the customer tightly, hold the solution loosely. If your screwdriver doesn't help the mechanic, don't shop it around to different problems or customers—fix the screwdriver. The customer (mechanic) and problem (turning screws) are constants; the solution is disposable.

nodes.label: Fall in Love with the Problem, Not the Product
nodes.rationale: Core Seibel principle: founders who love their product become defensive when it fails. Founders who love the problem treat the product as an instrument that can be discarded. The fastest way to avoid product-love is to release quickly—sunk-cost psychology takes over after months of investment.
nodes.norm_label: fall in love with the problem, not the product

nodes.label: Launch Fast / Launch Now
nodes.rationale: YC's most fundamental advice: launch a mediocre product as soon as possible, then talk to customers and iterate. The product must contain a 'quantum of utility'—enough genuine value that overwhelms rough edges. The market teaches you what to build; imagination alone cannot substitute.

nodes.label: Quantum of Utility
nodes.rationale: YC concept: the minimum threshold of genuine value a product must deliver to customers so that the utility overwhelms whatever problems rough edges present. This is the bar for launching, not perfection.

nodes.label: Do Things That Don't Scale
nodes.rationale: Paul Graham's principle: at early stages, founders should do manual, labor-intensive work to get first customers and learn what to build. Airbnb's founders photographing apartments themselves is the canonical example. Scaling prematurely wastes time and often leads to startup death.

nodes.label: 90/10 Solution
nodes.rationale: Paul Buchheit's heuristic: find a way to accomplish 90% of what you want with only 10% of the work. A 90% solution available right away beats a 100% solution that takes ages. Speed of delivery compounds.

nodes.label: Write Code and Talk to Users
nodes.rationale: YC's core operational mantra: the two most important tasks for an early-stage company are writing code (or building product) and talking directly to users. Everything else Sam Altman calls 'fake work'—activities that feel productive but don't move the company forward.
nodes.norm_label: write code and talk to users

nodes.label: Fake Work
nodes.rationale: Sam Altman's term for activities that feel productive but don't move the company forward. Includes conferences, press, meetings with VCs/corporate development, and other distractions. Founders gravitate toward fake work because real work—writing code, sales calls, hearing that your product isn't good enough—is hard.

nodes.label: Customer Development Model
nodes.source_file: sources/Steve Blank - The Startup Owner's Manual.pdf
nodes.rationale: Steve Blank's 4-step methodology: (1) Customer Discovery—turn vision into hypotheses tested with customers, (2) Customer Validation—prove repeatable/scalable business model, (3) Customer Creation—drive end-user demand, (4) Company Building—transition to formal departments. Underpinned by the 14-point Customer Development Manifesto and the principle 'Get Out of the Building'.

nodes.label: Customer Development Manifesto
nodes.source_file: sources/Steve Blank - The Startup Owner's Manual.pdf
nodes.rationale: 14 guiding principles for deploying the Customer Development process, emphasizing that a startup is not a small version of a big company, and that founders must conduct experiments and test hypotheses with real customers.

nodes.label: Business Model Canvas (Hypotheses)
nodes.source_file: sources/Steve Blank - The Startup Owner's Manual.pdf
nodes.rationale: The starting point for Customer Discovery: founders' vision is converted into a business model canvas, then into a series of hypotheses. These hypotheses are turned into experiments and tested with customers to validate problem-solution fit.

nodes.label: Physical vs. Web/Mobile Channels (Parallel Tracks)
nodes.source_file: sources/Steve Blank - The Startup Owner's Manual.pdf
nodes.rationale: Blank and Dorf recognize Customer Development operates at different speeds for web/mobile versus physical distribution channels. The book provides parallel tracks: physical goods/channels and web/mobile products/channels, with distinct MVP and marketing approaches for each.

nodes.label: The Lean Startup
nodes.rationale: Eric Ries's methodology: apply the scientific method to startups—start with a hypothesis, test it, prove it, and iterate. Based on the Build-Measure-Learn feedback loop. Fundamentally a methodology for systematically searching for PMF before scaling.

nodes.label: Value Hypothesis
nodes.rationale: Andy Rachleff's concept: the key assumption underlying why a customer is likely to use a product. Defines what you build, who is the audience, and the business model. Finding a compelling value hypothesis IS finding PMF. Only once proven do you move on to the growth hypothesis.

nodes.label: Net Promoter Score (NPS) for PMF
nodes.rationale: Andy Rachleff suggests NPS as a proxy for customer love—ideally 40 or higher to know you're on the right track. However, NPS is not as accurate as actual market feedback in the form of purchases.

nodes.label: Three-Legged Stool (Markets, People, Products)
nodes.rationale: Don Valentine's framework: building a valuable business requires markets, people, and innovative products. All three legs are needed, but Valentine emphasized market primacy: 'Give me a giant market—always.' Different VCs weight these elements differently at different times.

nodes.label: First to PMF Wins (Not First to Market)
nodes.rationale: Andy Rachleff's principle: being first to market seldom matters. The first company to achieve PMF is almost always the long-term winner. Once PMF is achieved, it is extremely difficult to dislodge even with a better or cheaper product.
nodes.norm_label: first to pmf wins (not first to market)

nodes.label: PMF Myths (Ben Horowitz)
nodes.rationale: Ben Horowitz's four PMF myths: (1) PMF is always a discrete, big bang event, (2) it's patently obvious when you have PMF, (3) once you achieve PMF you can't lose it, (4) once you have PMF you don't have to sweat the competition. Reality: PMF is continuous and requires constant adaptation.

nodes.label: Serendipity Meets Consistent Process
nodes.rationale: Andy Rachleff's teaching: you often stumble into PMF, but the process to get to serendipity is incredibly consistent. He teaches this process at Stanford in 'Aligning Startups with their Markets.' PMF emerges from experiments, not a single Eureka moment.

nodes.label: Pre-Chasm Guideposts (5 Guideposts)
nodes.rationale: Martin Casado's 5 guideposts for founders going to market when no market exists: (1) Innovation-Friend Zone—companies pay for PoCs to learn, not to buy, (2) customers are sold but still can't buy due to organizational barriers, (3) biggest competition is the status quo, not other products, (4) paradox of pricing in non-existent markets, (5) ecosystem partners can't sell pre-chasm products—create a pull-based market first.

nodes.label: Innovation-Friend Zone
nodes.rationale: Casado's concept: in pre-chasm markets, large companies pay startups for PoCs and pilots simply to learn, not because they intend to buy. The tell: lots of positive meetings but no clarity on when anything goes to production. Large companies keep startups engaged for years dangling seven-figure deals.

nodes.label: Status Quo as Biggest Competitor
nodes.rationale: Casado's insight: in pre-chasm markets, the customer may not even be aware of the problem. The real competition is 'how things have always been done.' If there's truly no competition (status quo or otherwise), the market and problem don't exist. Founders must explain the problem, not just the solution.

nodes.label: Pre-Chasm Market
nodes.rationale: A market where there may be early adopters who get it but a deep chasm exists between them and mainstream users. Most startups fall into this chasm. Adapted from Geoffrey Moore's technology adoption lifecycle. Going to market early changes everything about building a company.

nodes.label: Product-Market-Sales Fit (PMSF)
nodes.rationale: Jyoti Bansal's extension: PMF alone is misleading. Founders need the right market, right product, AND right sales/go-to-market strategy working together as one system. Product features must inherit part of the go-to-market. Achieved at $10M ARR during Phase 2 ($1M-$10M).

nodes.label: Three-Phase Revenue Framework
nodes.rationale: Bansal's revenue milestone framework: Phase 1 ($0-$1M ARR)—find PMF, identify target market, validate technology; Phase 2 ($1M-$10M ARR)—find PMSF, iterate GTM; Phase 3 ($10M-$100M+)—scale. Skills needed change at each phase; don't overthink ahead.

nodes.label: The Sandwich Strategy
nodes.rationale: AppDynamics' go-to-market combining bottoms-up (free developer adoption) and tops-down (enterprise air cover). Land small deals ($20K-$50K) via inside sales, expand to large deals ($500K-$2M+) via field sales. Requires both inside and field sales capabilities.

nodes.label: 10-15% Services Sweet Spot
nodes.rationale: Bansal's counterintuitive finding: 10-15% services revenue relative to software spend dramatically improves adoption speed, reduces shelfware, and improves renewals. On a $1M deal, $100K-$150K in services. Lower first-year margin leads to higher adoption, higher renewal rates, higher lifetime margins.

nodes.label: 2/3 - 1/3 Engineering Investment Rule
nodes.rationale: Bansal's engineering allocation: 2/3 on core TAM (improving existing product, serving current customers), 1/3 on TAM expansion (new use cases, adjacent markets). Applied continuously without disrupting existing go-to-market motion.

nodes.label: Sales Learning Curve (Three Phases)
nodes.rationale: Three-phase sales evolution: (1) Initiation (first 25 customers)—founders/PMs sell evangelically, (2) Transition (25-100)—trained salesforce can take over, (3) Execution (100+)—mature sales org with references, Magic Quadrant presence. New products must restart this curve (startup within a startup).

nodes.label: Startup Within a Startup
nodes.rationale: Bansal's approach for selling new products: create a separate evangelical sales team (SWAT team with PM, engineers, best sales engineers) rather than giving new products to mature salesforce. Salespeople sell what's easiest; new products need their own team with aligned incentives.

nodes.label: Bansal's 3-Rule Pricing Framework
nodes.rationale: (1) Pricing must be describable in half a sentence, (2) must be measurable pre- and post-sale, (3) price based on business value/ROI, not cost-plus. Be creative with product, don't be creative with pricing. If your product is truly superior, charge more.

nodes.label: 'How Would You Make the Business Case to Your Boss?'
nodes.rationale: Bansal's key question to reveal true PMF. When customers can articulate the business case (e.g., 'reduces outage triage from 6 engineers for 5 hours to 1 engineer for 15 minutes'), they understand the business value. PMF definition: understanding the business value of your product.
nodes.norm_label: 'how would you make the business case to your boss?'

nodes.label: Three Competing Product Priorities
nodes.rationale: Once you have customers: (1) Win revenue today—features to close deals and beat competitors, (2) Keep customers happy—adopt, renew, expand, (3) Win revenue two years from now—TAM expansion. Maps to 2/3 (priorities 1+2) and 1/3 (priority 3) engineering rule.

nodes.label: Lean B2B (Rapid Validation Framework)
nodes.rationale: Étienne Garbugli's methodology for B2B startups: systematically find and validate business opportunities in mid-to-large organizations. Covers finding early adopters, conducting problem interviews, building MVPs, solution interviews, and PMF assessment. Addresses unique B2B challenges: long sales cycles, stakeholder complexity, domain credibility, gatekeepers, and ROI estimation.

nodes.label: Small Giants
nodes.rationale: Bo Burlingham's term for privately-owned companies that choose to be great instead of big—turning down growth opportunities because they have larger ambitions beyond revenue. Key characteristics: remain privately owned, focus on excellence, great workplaces, community contribution, human scale (where the CEO can still meet new hires). Named by Jay Goltz. Counters the axiom that businesses must grow or die.

nodes.label: How to F*ck Up Your Startup
nodes.source_file: sources/How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Kim Hvidkjaer's taxonomy of startup failures: 90% of startups fail. Six categories of 'f*ckups': (1) Business Model—poor business plan, problem-less solution, co-dependency collapse, terrible timing, (2) Market Research—analysis paralysis, MVP mistakes, (3) Funding—death by overfunding, cap-table capsize, (4) Product Development—big bang release, overcorrecting, (5) Organizational—flawed founding team, missing co-founder, hiring mistakes, (6) Sales—flawed PMF, pricing predicaments. Also covers Growth f*ckups: growing too fast, giving up too soon, not knowing when to quit.
nodes.norm_label: how to f*ck up your startup

nodes.label: Hair on Fire Customers (Sequoia/Seibel)
nodes.rationale: Sequoia's metaphor extended by Seibel: find customers whose problem is so urgent that they'll try any half-baked, v1, imperfect solution. If their hair is on fire, they'll even grab a brick and try to hit themselves in the head if it might help. The litmus test for a real market need.

nodes.label: Handpick Initial Users / Filter First Customers
nodes.rationale: Seibel's principle: the best startups heavily filter who can use the initial product. The goal is not growth but learning whether the solution works for a specific group. A small number of deeply engaged, carefully chosen users teaches far more than many casual ones.
nodes.norm_label: handpick initial users / filter first customers

nodes.label: Keep a Problem Book (Not an Idea Book)
nodes.rationale: Seibel recommends keeping a problem book instead of a startup idea notebook. Write down problems encountered in daily life—personal, friends', family's, work frustrations. This builds an inventory of real problems to draw from, far more useful than a list of half-baked product concepts.
nodes.norm_label: keep a problem book (not an idea book)

nodes.label: Unique Qualification / Founder's Secret
nodes.rationale: Seibel's filter: 'Why am I uniquely qualified to work on this problem?' Not résumé experience, but an angle or perspective that others don't have. Connects to Peter Thiel's 'great companies have secrets' thesis—specific reasons for success others don't see.

nodes.label: MVP Within One Week
nodes.rationale: Seibel's aspirational target for MVPs: build and launch within one week. A forcing function that prevents adding non-essential features and forces focus on absolute core value proposition.

nodes.label: Launch Something Embarrassing
nodes.rationale: Seibel's rule: the first version of your product should be embarrassing. If you're not a little ashamed of it, you waited too long to launch. Embarrassment signals that you shipped early enough to learn something.

nodes.label: The First 10 Customers
nodes.rationale: Seibel's playbook: recruit first 10 users from people who already know and trust you. They must have genuine acute need, must be willing to pay or give brutally honest feedback. Strategy: list specific people, reach out individually with personal messages, frame it as a favor, schedule calls within 48 hours.

nodes.label: Manual Delivery / Be the Software First
nodes.rationale: In earliest phase, deliver value manually instead of automatically. Be the software before you build the software. Reveals whether the problem is real, teaches the solution through doing, builds relationships, and is fast. Examples: Airbnb taking photos, Stripe manually onboarding developers, Zappos buying shoes from stores.
nodes.norm_label: manual delivery / be the software first

nodes.label: Three Kinds of Unique Insight
nodes.rationale: Seibel's taxonomy: (1) Market insight—you see demand others don't, (2) Technology insight—you can build what wasn't possible before, (3) Behavioral insight—you understand user behavior leading to fundamentally different product experience.

nodes.label: Seibel's User Conversation Method
nodes.rationale: Five rules for founder-user conversations: (1) Don't pitch—your goal is to learn, (2) Ask about behavior not opinions—past behavior doesn't lie, (3) Shut up—80% airtime to user, (4) Don't defend—suppress instinct to explain, say 'tell me more,' (5) End with the disappointment question adapted from Sean Ellis. Talk to at least 10 users before writing code.

nodes.label: Real Validation Signals (Seibel)
nodes.rationale: Signals that matter: very-disappointed test, growing waiting lists, strong retention, organic word-of-mouth, users hacking your product, actual revenue. Signals that don't matter: press, conferences, investor interest without traction, vanity metrics, friends saying 'great idea,' competitor news.

nodes.label: Fire Your Customers
nodes.rationale: YC principle: a small group of customers who love you is better than a large group who kind of like you. Some customers cost more than they provide in revenue or learning. Justin.tv/Twitch succeeded by firing non-gaming users and focusing on video game broadcasters.

nodes.label: Startups Die of Suicide, Not Murder
nodes.rationale: YC principle: startups almost always fail because of their own decisions, not competitors. Competition matters eventually but is unlikely to be the cause of death in the first year or two. Founders should ignore competitors and focus on their own execution.
nodes.norm_label: startups die of suicide, not murder

nodes.label: YC Pocket Guide of Essential Advice
nodes.rationale: 14 distilled principles: launch now, build something people want, do things that don't scale, find 90/10 solution, find 10-100 customers who love you, all startups are broken, write code talk to users, it's not your money, growth follows great product, valuation != success, avoid big company deals, avoid conferences, founder relationships matter, ignore competitors, be nice.
nodes.norm_label: yc pocket guide of essential advice

nodes.label: Pre-Launch Sequence (4 Goals)
nodes.rationale: Seibel's 4-goal pre-launch framework: (1) Launch something minimal before you feel ready, (2) Get initial customers by any means, (3) Talk to those users—don't dismiss feedback because product isn't finished, (4) Iterate on the solution—don't abandon the customer or problem. Core principle: reduce time between idea and discovery of worth.

nodes.label: Validate Problem Before Building
nodes.rationale: Seibel's Step 3: talk to potential users before writing code. The litmus test: do people's eyes light up when you describe the problem? Do they tell stories? Or nod politely? The former signals a real problem. If people aren't actively working around the problem with some solution, it may not be real.

nodes.label: Founders Fall Out (Common Failure)
nodes.rationale: YC identifies founder conflicts as a primary reason most companies fail fast. Open honest communication between cofounders prevents future debacles. Founder relationships matter more than founders think.

nodes.label: All Startups Are Broken (Continuously Righting a Capsized Ship)
nodes.rationale: YC principle: nearly every startup has deep fundamental issues. Success is not determined by whether you are broken at the beginning, but by what founders do about the problems. The founder's job is continuously righting a capsized ship.
nodes.norm_label: all startups are broken (continuously righting a capsized ship)

nodes.label: Depth Over Breadth (Few Happy Users > Many Indifferent)
nodes.rationale: Seibel's Step 9: make a few users really happy, not many semi-happy. A user who loves your product tells others, forgives bugs, gives detailed feedback, sticks around. Aligns with Paul Graham's maxim: better to have 100 users who love you than 10,000 who sort of like you.
nodes.norm_label: depth over breadth (few happy users > many indifferent)

nodes.label: Validate First, Scale Later
nodes.rationale: Seibel's Step 10: fundraising, hiring, and growth come after you've built something people want. Two failure paths: (1) pitching investors before building—investors invest in execution not ideas, (2) outsourcing the build—without technical co-founders, need 10x traction to get investment.

nodes.label: Start Small, Validate Early, Iterate Fast (Seibel synthesis)
nodes.rationale: The synthetic thread across all Seibel's frameworks: every day without discovery is risk accumulation, not progress. The answer to almost every early-stage startup question is 'talk to users more.' Not 'raise more money,' not 'add more features,' not 'hire more people.'
nodes.norm_label: start small, validate early, iterate fast (seibel synthesis)

nodes.label: Build-Measure-Learn Loop
nodes.rationale: The Lean Startup's core feedback loop: build a minimum viable product, measure how customers respond, learn whether to pivot or persevere. PMF emerges from continuous iterations of this loop.

nodes.label: Arthur Rock vs. Don Valentine VC Philosophies
nodes.rationale: Two contrasting VC philosophies: Arthur Rock bets on great entrepreneurs ('you find a great entrepreneur and back him'), Don Valentine bets on great markets ('you find a great market and build multiple companies in that market'). Valentine: gave us a technical problem and a big market, we invested in Apple with young Steve Jobs.
nodes.norm_label: arthur rock vs. don valentine vc philosophies

nodes.label: Justin.tv to Twitch Pivot
nodes.rationale: Key case study across Seibel's writings: Justin.tv started as lifecasting platform, founders noticed disproportionate gaming engagement, pivoted to Twitch, sold to Amazon for ~$1B. Example of loving the problem (live streaming platform) not the initial solution (reality TV), and firing wrong customers to focus on gamers.

nodes.label: Airbnb Origin (Conference Airbeds)
nodes.rationale: Frequently cited by Seibel: Airbnb started from the problem of paying rent during a conference when hotels were sold out. Airbed-and-breakfast for conferences. 200M+ nights booked in 2017, almost none fitting the original purpose. Example of starting with a trivial-sounding problem that grew into a billion-dollar company.

nodes.label: Superhuman PMF Optimization Case
nodes.rationale: Rahul Vohra applied Ellis survey to Superhuman (email client): started at 22% 'very disappointed,' segmented by high-expectation customers (HXCs), raised to 33%, then 58% over 3 quarters using the 4-step PMF engine. Most detailed public case study of operationalizing Andreessen's PMF concept.

nodes.label: Slack PMF Survey (51% Very Disappointed)
nodes.rationale: Hiten Shah's 2015 survey of 731 Slack users: 51% would be very disappointed without Slack, confirming PMF at ~500K paying users. Demonstrates how hard the 40% benchmark is—even a generational success like Slack barely exceeds it.

nodes.label: AppDynamics Scaling Case Study
nodes.rationale: AppDynamics grew from $0 to acquired by Cisco for $3.7B. Key lessons: started with Java monitoring, expanded TAM via adjacent markets, systematically applied 2/3-1/3 engineering rule for 7-8 years, used sandwich strategy, established SWAT teams for new products, maintained simple pricing despite complex monitoring.

nodes.label: Nicira Pre-Chasm Case Study
nodes.rationale: Martin Casado's own company: software-defined networking pioneer. First 2 years yielded almost no progress despite positive meetings and paid PoCs. Eventually figured out pre-chasm dynamics, inverted sales approach (qualified better, said no often, told prospects what could go wrong), built a large business acquired by VMware for $1.26B.

nodes.label: Services Margin Math (PMSF)
nodes.rationale: Peter Levine's economic framing: services that lead to a $1M license deal produce far higher blended margins than a $20K no-services deal, even though services unit economics look worse. Low first-year margin → higher adoption → higher renewal rates → higher lifetime margins.

nodes.label: Engineers Talk Directly to Customers
nodes.rationale: AppDynamics cultural principle: break the barrier between engineers and customers. If selling to engineers, it makes no sense to isolate engineers from customers. Engineers and PMs went on sales calls to understand who buys, how much they pay, and what to build.

nodes.label: Keep List Price High, Discount for Early Traction
nodes.rationale: Casado's pricing rule for pre-chasm markets: keep list price high to preserve value perception, discount for early customers but limit by duration, scale, geography, or use case. Matching existing comp prices automatically underprices an innovative product.
nodes.norm_label: keep list price high, discount for early traction

nodes.label: Don't Discuss Pricing Until Value Is Shown
nodes.rationale: Casado's complementary pricing rule: demonstrate value through PoC and pilot using the customer's systems/data before any pricing discussion. Once the account reaches 'technical close' and the customer is aware of value, then engage in value-based pricing.
nodes.norm_label: don't discuss pricing until value is shown

nodes.label: Pull-Based Market (Pre-Chasm)
nodes.rationale: Casado's principle: pre-chasm companies must create a pull-based market, not a push-based one, before partner channels become effective. Ecosystem partners (VARs, resellers, OEMs) only become effective sellers in stage two after a big market is demonstrated.

nodes.label: Product Marketing Over Field Marketing (Pre-Chasm)
nodes.rationale: Casado's marketing recommendation: in pre-chasm, education draws the market. Field/event marketing is optimized for post-chasm growth. Pre-chasm marketing must be targeted, educational, and grounded in deep product understanding—harder to outsource.

nodes.label: Founder-Talking Ratio: 1:5 (80% User Airtime)
nodes.rationale: Seibel's rule for user conversations: founder should talk at most 20% of the time. If you're talking more than the user, you're not learning. Listen more than you talk.

nodes.label: Viddy: Premature Scaling Cautionary Tale
nodes.rationale: Viddy used Facebook OpenGraph to grow to millions of users before achieving PMF. This premature scaling led to the company being sold for very little. Other examples: Friendster, Orkut, Digg. Groupon scaled prematurely but managed to pivot and survive.

nodes.label: Cisco Board Replacing Founders (Market > Team)
nodes.rationale: Example supporting market primacy: Cisco's board replaced the founding husband-and-wife team because the market was too valuable. Illustrates Don Valentine's philosophy that people can be changed, but markets can't.

nodes.label: Eric Schmidt at Google (Team Augmentation)
nodes.rationale: Example of augmenting rather than replacing the founding team: Eric Schmidt brought in as CEO of Google to supply management skills to founders Larry Page and Sergey Brin.

nodes.label: Human Scale (Small Giants)
nodes.rationale: Burlingham's principle: companies should operate at a size where it's still possible for an individual to be acquainted with everyone, for the CEO to meet new hires, for employees to feel closely connected. This is often limited to a few hundred employees. Scale plays an important role in their business approach.

nodes.label: Zingerman's Community of Businesses
nodes.rationale: Burlingham's original inspiration: Zingerman's Delicatessen in Ann Arbor walked away from national expansion to stay local and launch other food businesses. Became ZCoB (Zingerman's Community of Businesses). Exemplifies choosing great over big.

nodes.label: Grow or Die Myth
nodes.rationale: Burlingham challenges the conventional wisdom that businesses must grow or die. True for public companies and venture-backed startups, but thousands of private companies don't grow much and remain quite healthy. This axiom applies only to certain enterprise types.

nodes.label: Shareholder Value in Private Companies
nodes.rationale: Burlingham's insight: what's in shareholders' interest depends on who the shareholders are. Small giant owners have nonfinancial priorities alongside financial objectives: being great at what they do, great workplaces, great service, great supplier relationships, great community contributions, great ways to lead their lives. Wealth is a byproduct.

nodes.label: Jack Welch's #1 or #2 Market Share Mantra
nodes.rationale: Burlingham references Jack Welch's dictum—don't own a business unless it's #1 or #2 in its niche—and questions whether it makes sense for private non-public companies. A business axiom that doesn't apply to most companies.
nodes.norm_label: jack welch's #1 or #2 market share mantra

nodes.label: The Problem-Less Solution (Hvidkjaer)
nodes.source_file: sources/How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Hvidkjaer's term for building a solution without validating the underlying problem. One of the Business Model f*ckups. Parallels Seibel's warning against falling in love with product instead of problem.

nodes.label: Death by Overfunding (Hvidkjaer)
nodes.source_file: sources/How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Hvidkjaer identifies excessive funding as a failure mode: too much capital enables wasteful spending patterns, dilutes urgency, and can lead to premature scaling. Contrasts with Lean Startup philosophy of staying frugal before PMF.

nodes.label: The Passion Pinch (Hvidkjaer)
nodes.source_file: sources/How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: One of Hvidkjaer's Attitude f*ckups: being so passionate about the idea that you ignore warning signs, market signals, and contrary feedback. Passion becomes a liability when it blinds the founder.

nodes.label: Don't Overthink Too Far Ahead
nodes.rationale: Bansal's closing advice: focus on the skills you need to master for the current milestone. Someone at $0-$1M ARR optimizing for $75M problems is wasting time. Skills needed at each phase are fundamentally different.

nodes.label: Intuit, Apple, Google: Not First to Market, First to PMF
nodes.rationale: Rachleff's examples proving first-mover advantage is overrated: Intuit, Apple, Google, and Facebook were not first to market but were first to achieve PMF in their categories.
nodes.norm_label: intuit, apple, google: not first to market, first to pmf

nodes.label: The Dance Between Product and Market (Mike Maples Jr.)
nodes.rationale: Mike Maples Jr.'s description of PMF as creating a dance between product and market—a dynamic, iterative process rather than a static achievement.
nodes.norm_label: the dance between product and market (mike maples jr.)

nodes.label: B2B Problem Interviews (Garbugli)
nodes.rationale: Garbugli's structured approach to B2B customer discovery: code of conduct for interviews, meeting structure for problem qualification, scoring problems to prioritize, and drawing a line to determine which problems matter. Specifically adapted for B2B where stakeholder complexity and purchase processes differ from consumer.

nodes.label: B2B Early Adopter Framework (Garbugli)
nodes.rationale: Garbugli's methodology for finding B2B early adopters: leveraging domain credibility and visibility, becoming 'one of them,' the consultant vs. inventor archetypes, and structured outreach with success ratios.

nodes.label: B2B Solution Interviews (Garbugli)
nodes.rationale: Garbugli's solution validation process: telling a compelling story, solution exploration and demo, closing for the first purchase. Covers preparing the pitch with the jury, the offer, the pilot, and collateral—all B2B-specific.

nodes.label: High-Expectation Customers (HXCs)
nodes.rationale: Concept adapted by Rahul Vohra from brand strategist Julie Supan: the specific customer persona that has the highest expectations and will derive the most value from the product. Superhuman's PMF engine segments users to identify and serve HXCs better than anyone else.

nodes.label: Slack at 51% PMF Score
nodes.rationale: Hiten Shah's 2015 open research: surveyed 731 Slack users using Ellis's question. 51% said 'very disappointed' if Slack disappeared, confirming PMF at ~500K paying users. Benchmarks how difficult the 40% threshold is in practice.

nodes.label: Small Giants
nodes.rationale: Companies that choose to be great instead of big—turning down growth opportunities because they have larger ambitions. These are extraordinary, privately owned companies that forgo revenue or geographic growth to achieve other remarkable ends: being great at what they do, creating a great place to work, providing great service to customers, having great relationships with suppliers, making great contributions to their communities, and finding great ways to lead their lives. They remain privately owned with majority stock in the hands of one person, a small group, or employees.
quotes: I've made much more money by choosing the right things to say no to than by choosing things to say yes to. —Danny Meyer
Wealth, though substantial, was a byproduct of success in these other areas. —Bo Burlingham

nodes.label: Mojo
nodes.rationale: The elusive, mysterious, and difficult-to-define quality that great small companies exude—their corporate charisma, buzz, and soul. Coined by Gary Erickson after a trade show encounter where a marketer said a competitor 'lost their mojo.' Erickson defined it as 'something about the brand, product, and way of being in the world that was different.' Mojo is a quality that must be tended carefully; companies can lose it through negligence, losing creativity, authenticity, emotional connection, or by getting 'too big too fast.' It is the business equivalent of personal charisma—something that makes people want to be associated with the company.
quotes: Whatever mojo was, some smart people evidently thought that it was important, and that Clif Bar had it. —Bo Burlingham
I realized that mojo was an elusive quality and needed to be tended carefully. —Gary Erickson
To me mojo means, 'You got that engine running baby and the sky is the limit!' —Clif Bar employee
A business without soul is not something I'm interested in working at. —Danny Meyer

nodes.label: Human Scale
nodes.rationale: A size at which it is still possible for an individual to be acquainted with everyone else in the organization, still possible for the CEO to meet with new hires, still possible for employees to feel closely connected to the rest of the company. Scale is measured primarily by number of employees rather than revenue. The principle holds that once a company exceeds human scale, it becomes extremely difficult to maintain the intimacy, personal connections, and culture that define a small giant. Fritz Maytag believed 50 employees was about all he could handle; O.C. Tanner demonstrated it was possible with 1,700+ employees, though with significant effort.
quotes: I've always thought it was more fun and satisfying to have all chiefs and no Indians. —Fritz Maytag
Fifty is about all I can handle. —Fritz Maytag on employee count
We stay small because I want to know everyone. When you don't, you're too big. —Bill Butler

nodes.label: Grow or Die Myth
nodes.rationale: The conventional wisdom that businesses must grow or die—a notion that is true for most public companies and venture-backed startups but demonstrably untrue for thousands of private companies that don't grow much yet remain quite healthy. Burlingham argues this myth, along with Jack Welch's 'first or second in market share' dictum and the concept of 'getting to the next level,' are business axioms that actually apply only to large public companies and venture-backed tech startups, not to the vast majority of private enterprises.
quotes: There are thousands of private companies that don't grow much, if at all, and they don't die either. On the contrary, they're often quite healthy. —Bo Burlingham
It occurred to me that you could have a small, prestigious, profitable business, and it would be all right. —Fritz Maytag

nodes.label: Shareholder Value vs. Private Company Values
nodes.rationale: Burlingham argues that the concept of shareholder value has a specific meaning for public companies (legally and morally obligated to produce best financial results) but does not apply the same way to private companies. What is in the interest of shareholders depends on who the shareholders are. Small giant owners have nonfinancial priorities alongside financial objectives: being great at what they do, creating a great workplace, providing great service, building great supplier relationships, contributing to communities, and finding great ways to lead their lives. Milton Friedman's argument that 'the business of business is business' only applies to publicly owned corporations—private owners spend their own money, not someone else's.
quotes: What's in the interest of shareholders depends on who the shareholders are. —Bo Burlingham
If he acts to reduce the returns of his enterprise in order to exercise his 'social responsibility,' he is spending his own money, not someone else's. —Milton Friedman (quoted in book)

nodes.label: Selection Criteria for Small Giants
nodes.rationale: 1. Companies started/owned by people who faced a growth decision and made a conscious choice to limit growth. 2. Companies admired and emulated in their own industries (peer and competitor respect). 3. Companies singled out for extraordinary achievements by independent third-party observers. Excluded: lifestyle businesses (no real choice), franchisees/franchisors, boutique businesses targeting elite markets (staying small is strategy, not choice), traditional mom-and-pop companies.
