QuickBooks offered 'half the functionality at twice the price' of professional accounting software but nailed the job: 'Help me manage my small business finances without having to learn accounting.' This insight came from recognizing the difference between a task and a genuine struggle. Competitors were not other accounting software but hiring a bookkeeper, working late, using spreadsheets, or the shoebox of receipts.

nodes.label: V8 Juice Case Study
nodes.rationale: Campbell's V8 brand team discovered a job: busy people who promised their mothers they'd eat vegetables but found cooking them too time-consuming. V8 could compete against vegetables (peeling carrots, boiling spinach) rather than against other beverages. When they reframed competition this way, sales quadrupled in less than a year. But after the team members moved on, the job was lost. V8 reverted to product-line competition, spawning variants (Spicy Hot, Lemon, Picante, Roasted Chicken, Low-sodium, Organic, Splash, V-Fusion + Tea) that created confusion instead of clarity. This illustrates the fragility of jobs focus — it can be lost when key people leave.

nodes.label: Deseret News Case Study
nodes.rationale: Traditional newspaper facing disruption. Clark Gilbert's team used Jobs Theory to identify a segment of 'like-minded believers' (~56% of American news consumers) who felt massively underserved. Their job: 'Help me be well-informed, feel more confident in my knowledge, and still be true to my beliefs so I can make a difference in my home and community.' They were reading the NYT (admired rigor but felt values disconnect) and watching Sean Hannity (heard their values but felt it was polemic). Deseret News focused on PICA (Perspective, Insight, Context, Analysis) + faith/family. Built social communities around the job rather than the publication — reaching 100M+ social followers through FamilyShare Network. 'The worst insult you could get was that a story looked like the Sacramento Bee.'

nodes.label: Medtronic Pacemakers in India Case Study
nodes.rationale: Medtronic couldn't sell pacemakers in India despite heart disease being the #1 killer. Traditional research (asking doctors about product features) failed to reveal why. Jobs-based research identified four barriers: lack of patient awareness, lack of proper diagnostics, inability to navigate the care pathway, and affordability. The biggest competitor was nonconsumption. Medtronic created free heart-health screening clinics, a loan program (bridge loans, no asset mortgage, death-expiring loans), and 'Sherpa' patient counselors to navigate hospital bureaucracy. Partnered with 100+ hospitals in 30 cities. ~167,000 patients screened, ~15,000 treated.

nodes.label: FranklinCovey Case Study
nodes.rationale: FranklinCovey transformed from selling training courses to solving a deeper job for HR/learning professionals: 'Help me be seen as a vital, contributing member to the company's long-term goals, not just a cost center.' They shifted from selling individual courses to selling subscriptions with unlimited access, plus full processes with measurable business outcomes (e.g., stationing a full-time coach for sales contracts above $500M). Revenue grew from $130M to $220M at 9.2% CAGR over six years, with profit increasing tenfold. Two-thirds of R&D budget now spent on process innovation rather than product innovation.

nodes.label: P&G Pampers in China Case Study
nodes.rationale: P&G initially failed selling disposable diapers in China by focusing on making a cheaper 'ten-cent diaper.' The breakthrough came when a focus group revealed the emotional and social dimensions: a mother's highlight of the week was 'renewed intimacy with her husband' because the baby slept through the night. The real job wasn't containment — it was better sleep for baby and parents. P&G partnered with Beijing Children's Hospital Sleep Research Center, showing that Pampers babies fell asleep 30% faster and slept 30 extra minutes — linked to improved cognitive development. By 2013, Pampers had ~30% market share in China with $1.6B in sales.

nodes.label: Kimberly-Clark Depend Silhouette Case Study
nodes.rationale: Despite having huge market share in adult incontinence, Kimberly-Clark discovered vast nonconsumption: people would rather stay home and disengage socially than buy adult diapers. They suffered for ~2 years before buying any product, using workarounds like feminine hygiene pads. The job: 'Help me reclaim my life without the stigma.' Depend Silhouette Briefs for women and Real Fit Briefs for men were designed to look and feel like normal underwear, with packaging that allowed customers to see the product. Generated $60M in first-year sales with 30% growth in year two — a Nielsen Breakthrough Innovation winner in a 'mature' category.

nodes.label: Detroit Condo Sales / Moving Lives Case Study
nodes.rationale: Bob Moesta was hired to help a Detroit-area builder sell condos to downsizers. Traditional improvements (bay windows, granite counters, more customization options) had no effect. Through Jobs interviews, Moesta discovered the real obstacle: the dining room table. The table represented family history — birthdays, Christmas, homework, forts. Buyers couldn't move until they resolved what to do with it. 'I went in thinking we were in the business of new home construction. But I realized we were instead in the business of moving lives.' Solutions: reduced second bedroom 20% to accommodate dining tables, provided moving services, two years of storage, and a sorting room. Eliminated the 30-page customization checklist (which caused anxiety) in favor of three pre-finished options. Raised prices $3,500 profitably. In 2007, when industry sales dropped 49%, this builder grew 25%.

nodes.label: Uber Case Study
nodes.rationale: Uber succeeded by nailing an unsatisfactorily filled job of urban transportation. The experience — knowing exactly when the driver will arrive, not needing cash, having a record of the trip, driver ratings — solved anxieties that taxis and car services didn't address. Uber competes not just with taxis but with taking the subway, calling a friend, or not going at all. The job involves functional, social, and emotional dimensions that had been poorly served by existing options.

nodes.label: Mayo Clinic Case Study
nodes.rationale: Christensen's personal experience as a patient. Unlike traditional hospitals where a personal-care physician ad hoc coordinates care, Mayo Clinic puts a process person in charge who schedules all specialists, ensures tests are done before appointments, and eliminates anxiety before it forms. Specialists keep openings for real-time needs. The organizing principle is a process to get the right things in the right sequence — not the specialties of doctors. The experience is seamless from the patient's perspective, demonstrating how processes aligned with the job (diagnose my complex condition efficiently) create competitive advantage.

nodes.label: Mattress Purchase Interview (Brian Walker)
nodes.rationale: A detailed Jobs to Be Done interview transcript illustrating the methodology. Brian Walker appeared to be an impulse buyer at Costco but had actually been struggling for a year with a failing $2,000 mattress. Key revelations: he tried sleeping without pillows, flipping the mattress, adjusting positions — all workarounds. His wife also began complaining. The mattress store experience gave him 'the heebie-jeebies' (germaphobe, sales pressure). He nearly bought through Groupon but feared the return process. The Costco purchase was triggered by: wife's blessing in the moment, Costco's no-questions return policy, and the ability to touch a sample. His real competitors were not other mattress brands but Advil and Red Bull (his compensating behaviors). The job: 'Help me get a good night's sleep so I can be productive as a businessman, dad, and husband.'

nodes.label: CVS MinuteClinic / QuickMedx Case Study
nodes.rationale: Founded after Rick Krieger spent frustrating hours in an ER for his son's strep throat test. The job: 'I don't want to see the doctor' — a negative job. Parents know their child has strep but must navigate appointments, waiting rooms, and pharmacy pickups. MinuteClinics provide walk-in nurse practitioners who can prescribe for routine ailments. Now 1,000+ locations in 33 states. Illustrates the power of negative jobs as innovation opportunities.

nodes.label: ING Direct Case Study
nodes.rationale: Traditional banks treated low-net-worth individuals as undesirable (minimum balances, fees). ING Direct saw a job: help people save money toward goals without friction. Simple offering: a few savings accounts, CDs, mutual funds — no minimums, open with $1. Online-only model meant fraction of brick-and-mortar costs. Opened physical 'cafés' (no transactions, just brand presence) to reduce anxiety about a virtual bank. Sold to Capital One for $9B in 2012. Illustrates competing with nonconsumption and overcoming anxiety obstacles.

nodes.label: Mercer Pension Risk Exchange Case Study
nodes.rationale: Mercer's retirement business faced decline as companies shifted from defined-benefit to defined-contribution plans. Jobs lens revealed the CFO/HR professional's job: navigate the enormously stressful process of pension buyouts without damaging their reputation. Traditional process took 6 months with market fluctuations — high anxiety. Mercer created the Pension Risk Exchange (like a stock exchange for pension buyouts), with modeling tools for 'anxiety-reducing practice runs.' Launched in US, UK, and Canada. Key insight: the emotional dimension (career anxiety, reputation) dominated even in a B2B financial context.

nodes.label: Intermountain Healthcare Case Study
nodes.rationale: Innovation director Todd Dunn became a patient and realized the exam room was designed purely for function (crinkly paper, sterile lighting) — creating anxiety rather than reducing it. The doctor chose to sketch on paper rather than use state-of-the-art software because he wanted to maintain eye contact and reassure the patient. 'We'd designed everything in that room from a functional perspective, but we had completely overlooked the emotional score.' Intermountain instituted 'Design for People' — a jobs-based framework requiring observation of functional, emotional, and social components before designing innovation briefs.

nodes.label: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) Case Study
nodes.rationale: Ethan Bernstein consciously designed the CFPB's org structure around consumers' Job to Be Done ('know before they owe') rather than traditional DC silos. Research, markets, and regulations were in one division; supervision, enforcement, and fair lending in another — breaking the typical separation that had contributed to the financial crisis. The policy committee met weekly for two hours, run like a Socratic law school class focused on the job. 'Solving a job was our unifying cause. Our reason for being. It was easy to rally around that. And we got action, rather than typical DC paralysis.'

nodes.label: Unilever Lifebuoy Soap Case Study
nodes.rationale: Unilever transformed its oldest health soap brand by nesting a job under the mission of helping children in emerging markets live to age five. The specific circumstance: in India, ~400,000 children under five die annually from diarrheal disease. Most people wash hands for ~7 seconds; it takes 30 seconds with soap to kill germs. Unilever created color-changing soap that shows children when they've washed for 10 seconds (all that's needed with their special formula), making it fun for kids to wash long enough. The broad mission provided inspiration; the specific job made innovation actionable.

nodes.label: Toyota Production System / Quality Movement Case Study
nodes.rationale: Toyota proved that despite inherent complexity, reliable quality is possible when you focus on the causal mechanism of defects. Every defect was treated as an anomaly to understand, not just fix. Toyota developed processes where each activity is an if-then statement: 'If we do this, then that will be the result.' This created an environment of repeated scientific experimentation. Toyota freely opened its plants to competitors because they knew the secret wasn't visible on the factory floor — it was in the processes. 'They recognized we were asking the wrong questions. We didn't understand this bigger picture.' This illustrates how processes (not products or resources) create competitive advantage.

nodes.label: Pixar Case Study
nodes.rationale: Pixar has openly shared its creative process (Ed Catmull literally wrote the book). Fixed processes exist for how movie ideas are generated, critiqued, improved, and perfected. Yet competitors haven't matched Pixar's success. This demonstrates that processes — not products or resources — are the source of durable competitive advantage. Processes are intangible, belong to the company, and emerge from hundreds of small decisions.

nodes.label: Amazon Case Study
nodes.rationale: Amazon is organized around three customer-job metrics: vast selection, low prices, and fast delivery — monitored minute-by-minute. Bezos calls delivery delays 'defects' to be eradicated. The 'customer backward' innovation process starts with a mock press release. Amazon built a shopping robot that automatically matches competitors' prices twice daily. Its online reviews help customers hire the right product for their specific job by finding reviewers who share the same job. 'We always start with the customers and look at all the metrics that matter for the customer.' Processes are designed as modular 'subroutines' that can be reused across the organization.

nodes.label: Volvo Case Study
nodes.rationale: Volvo was a purpose brand for safety and reliability since 1927. After Ford purchased it in 1999, it veered into flashier luxury cars, losing its purpose brand status. Sales declined; by 2005 it was unprofitable. Ford sold Volvo at a loss to Geely in 2010. 'We lost our way. We gotta go back to our roots.' Illustrates the danger of losing focus on the job that made a purpose brand successful.

nodes.label: Genuine Insight Is Experienced as True on Conception
nodes.rationale: A principle articulated by neuromarketing expert Gerald Zaltman: a genuine insight is a thought that is experienced as true on conception. When you have an insight, you don't have to convince yourself that it's important or powerful. You just know. This is why breakthrough insights might seem obvious in hindsight but are fundamentally contrarian — you see something that others have missed.

nodes.label: Perspective Is Worth 80 IQ Points
nodes.rationale: A Jeff Bezos maxim quoted in the book: looking at familiar things with fresh eyes (a new perspective) can reveal insights that were hiding in plain sight. Thomas Kuhn's 'paradigm shift' concept: 'Scientists see new and different things when looking with familiar instruments in places they have looked before.' The problem in uncovering jobs isn't the tools but what you're looking for and how you piece observations together.
quotes: Perspective is worth 80 I.Q. points. — Jeff Bezos
Scientists see new and different things when looking with familiar instruments in places they have looked before. — Thomas Kuhn

nodes.label: Products Become Services
nodes.rationale: When you are solving a customer's job, your products essentially become services. What matters is not the bundle of product attributes, but the experiences you enable to help customers make the progress they want to make. 'Products that succeed in solving customers' jobs essentially perform services in that customer's life.' This shifts the focus from features and functionality to experiences and obstacle removal.

nodes.label: Cheaper and Crappier Is Better Than Nothing
nodes.rationale: When you have a job to be done and there isn't a good solution, 'cheaper and crappier' is better than nothing. This describes how disruptive innovations often start — they don't need to be perfect, they just need to solve the job better than the existing alternatives (including nonconsumption). Sal Khan's initial YouTube math videos were 'cheap and dirty' compared to others but solved the job better. Airbnb started with air mattresses on floors.

nodes.label: One-Size-Fits-None
nodes.rationale: When companies try to satisfy multiple distinct jobs with a single average solution, they create a product that does none of the jobs well. The milkshake case demonstrated this: if the fast-food chain averaged commuter and dad feedback, it would create a one-size-fits-none product. The same applies to organizational processes that treat all customers as an 'average' that doesn't exist — like the US Air Force cockpits designed for the 'average pilot' that fit no actual pilot.

nodes.label: Don't Overshoot What Customers Will Pay For
nodes.rationale: A clear view of customers' jobs means an organization should never overshoot what customers are actually willing to pay for. Many innovations fail because they add features and improvements on dimensions irrelevant to the job. When you nail the job, customers are often willing to pay more — because the full cost of a product that fails to do the job (wasted time, frustration, poor solutions) is already significant.

nodes.label: Organizational Immune System
nodes.rationale: When an organization's processes are not aligned with a customer job, the 'immune system' rejects innovations that would require changing established processes, resource allocations, and priorities. The fast-food chain that discovered the milkshake jobs didn't implement the recommended changes because local managers deemed the process changes too difficult — the idea 'died a quiet death.' This is why jobs-based innovation requires not just insight but organizational integration.

nodes.label: Signal Who Should NOT Hire Your Product
nodes.rationale: In the age of online reviews, it's critical to make clear who should NOT hire your product. A customer who hires your product for a job it's not designed to do will be disappointed and write a negative review. Airbnb advises hosts to imagine an 'invisible report card on the forehead of potential guests.' Overselling leads to poor reviews. Restaurants have been hurt by great Yelp reviews that attracted foodies to a decent local place that wasn't designed for them.

nodes.label: Data Is Not the Phenomenon
nodes.rationale: Data represents the phenomenon but not very well. It's always an abstraction created by people who decide what to include and what to ignore. All data is man-made and embeds human bias and judgment. Quantitative data hides its subjectivity behind superficial precision. The key is to figure out what data should be created in the first place. Stories are rich in data; numbers distilled from stories offer insights that are shallow but broad.
quotes: Data is not the phenomenon. It represents the phenomenon, but not very well.
Deity does not create data and then bestow it upon mankind. All data is man-made.
The healthiest mindset for innovation is that nearly all data is built upon human bias and judgment.

nodes.label: Christensen's Definition of Theory
nodes.rationale: Good theory helps us understand 'how' and 'why.' It helps us make sense of how the world works and predict the consequences of decisions and actions. Theory has a voice but no agenda — it doesn't change its mind and doesn't apply to some companies but not others. Theories are not right or wrong; they provide accurate predictions given the circumstances. Good theories need anomalies — things they cannot explain — in order to improve. Theories are propositions in if-then form. Jobs Theory was built inductively (observing phenomena and developing propositions) rather than deductively (starting with a proposition and testing it).
quotes: Theory has a voice, but no agenda. A theory doesn't change its mind: it doesn't apply to some companies or people and not to others.
Good theory helps us understand 'how' and 'why.' It helps us make sense of how the world works and predict the consequences of our decisions and our actions.
Good theories actually need anomalies — things that the theory cannot explain — in order to improve.

nodes.label: Job as a Construct
nodes.rationale: In theory-building, a 'construct' is an abstraction — often a visualization — that helps observers see how phenomena interact and change over time. Constructs are rarely directly observable. The term 'job' is a construct. Terms like 'hire' and 'fire' are not just cute words — they help visualize how the processes of buying and selling actually work. Other famous constructs: Auguste Laurent's visualizations of chemical compounds, Adam Smith's 'invisible hand,' trajectories of technological progress in disruption theory.

nodes.label: Correlation Is Not Causation
nodes.rationale: The central problem with most innovation data: companies have masses of correlational data (this customer looks like that one, 68% prefer version A) but none of it reveals why customers make the choices they do. 'Ice cream sales and forest fires are correlated because both occur more often in the summer heat. But there is no causation; you don't light a patch of the Montana brush on fire when you buy a pint of Häagen-Dazs.' — Nate Silver. Jobs Theory provides the causal mechanism behind customer choice.

nodes.label: Connection to Disruptive Innovation Theory
nodes.rationale: The theory of disruptive innovation explains competitive response — what incumbents do when faced with disruption. But it doesn't tell you where to look for new opportunities or specifically what products/services to create. Jobs Theory fills this gap: it provides the offensive roadmap — where and how to innovate. The two theories are complementary. Jobs Theory helps you understand what causes customers to pull products into their lives; disruption theory explains how incumbents respond. Together they provide both offensive and defensive innovation strategy.

nodes.label: Lemon Problem Analogy for Innovation
nodes.rationale: US auto manufacturing in the 1980s treated defects as inherent randomness — adding inspectors and rework stations rather than finding root causes. Japanese manufacturers (inspired by Deming and Juran) treated every defect as an opportunity to understand causation and improve the process. Similarly, innovation today exists in a 'pre-quality revolution' state — managers accept failure as inevitable rather than understanding root causes. Jobs Theory is to innovation what the quality movement was to manufacturing.
quotes: Innovation, in a very real sense, exists in a 'pre-quality revolution' state. Managers accept flaws, missteps, and failure as an inevitable part of the process of innovation.
They have become so accustomed to putting Band-Aids on their uneven innovation success that too often they give no real thought to what's causing it in the first place.

nodes.label: Every Process Is Perfectly Designed to Deliver the Results It Gets
nodes.rationale: Attributed to W. Edwards Deming: if we believe innovation is messy and unknowable, we build processes that operationalize those beliefs. Many companies have 'unwittingly designed innovation processes that perfectly churn out mediocrity.' The corollary: to change results, you must change the underlying processes and the beliefs they embody.

nodes.label: Negative Jobs
nodes.rationale: Jobs that people actively want to avoid doing — the things they don't want to do. 'I think I have as many jobs of not wanting to do something as ones that I want positively to do. In my experience, negative jobs are often the best innovation opportunities.' Examples: 'I don't want to see the doctor' (CVS MinuteClinic), 'I don't want to do my taxes' (TurboTax), 'I don't want to cook vegetables' (V8). Negative jobs are often the most fertile ground for innovation because the emotional component is so powerful.

nodes.label: Beginner's Mind
nodes.rationale: A mindset essential for uncovering jobs: approaching customer interactions without preexisting assumptions that could prematurely filter out critical information. You have to hear what customers can't say by maintaining fresh eyes. The mattress interview demonstrated this — the interviewer didn't ask about mattress features but built a timeline of the customer's struggle, revealing that the real competitors were Advil and Red Bull, not other mattress brands.

nodes.label: Boundaries of Jobs Theory
nodes.rationale: Jobs Theory is not useful: (1) when there is no real struggle or existing solutions are good enough; (2) when decisions rely almost entirely on mathematical analysis (commodities trading); (3) when cost or efficiency is the sole factor. A well-defined Job to Be Done must be expressed in verbs and nouns, not adjectives and adverbs. If only products within the same class can solve the problem, it's not a job — you need to go up a level of abstraction. Jobs Theory does not tell you whether you should offer a solution (e.g., cigarettes solve jobs but are harmful).

nodes.label: Innovation North Star
nodes.rationale: The Job to Be Done serves as an enduring innovation North Star — a clear compass heading that guides innovation efforts over the long term. It provides not just a one-off improvement idea but a trajectory for continuous improvement. FranklinCovey's Bob Whitman: 'Jobs gives you a very clear innovation trajectory. I can see how we need to improve for the next ten years. It's less product oriented now than process oriented.' The North Star helps bridge the gap between senior management expectations and what employees instinctively know to do.

nodes.label: Jagged Profile / No Average Customer
nodes.rationale: From Todd Rose's The End of Average: when the US Air Force measured 4,000+ pilots across ~12 dimensions of size, zero pilots fit the 'average.' Every pilot had a 'jagged profile' — long legs but short arms, tall but narrow chest, etc. Cockpits designed for the average pilot fit no one. The same is true of customers: there is no such thing as an 'average' customer. Innovating toward average is doomed to fail. Jobs-based segmentation recognizes that people are jagged — they share jobs across demographic lines.

nodes.label: What Gets Measured Gets Done
nodes.rationale: Measurement drives behavior. Jobs Theory shifts performance criteria from internal financial metrics to externally relevant customer-benefit metrics. SNHU tracks response time in minutes, not weeks. Amazon tracks when orders are delivered, not shipped. Intuit measures customer labor hours saved. Having the right metrics institutionalizes the process and ensures employees know they're making the right choices. But measuring the right things is hard — the right metrics don't fall out of standard systems.

nodes.label: Discovery-Driven Planning
nodes.rationale: A tool developed by Ian MacMillan and Rita McGrath to test whether a strategy will be fruitful before sinking too many resources. Forces articulation of assumptions that need to be proved true for the strategy to succeed. 'What has to prove true for this to work?' Companies often approve projects based on incorrect guesses rather than testing assumptions early. Most project failures trace to mistakes in critical assumptions that weren't tested until too late.

nodes.label: "If you do not know how to ask the right question, you discover nothing."
Marketing Myopia (Theodore Levitt)
nodes.rationale: Levitt's 1960 HBR article that argued the railroad industry declined because they assumed they were in the railroad business rather than the transportation business. This is the classic example of defining your business by product rather than job. The concept of 'marketing myopia' is the failure to see the broader job customers are hiring you to do.

nodes.label: Loss Aversion (Kahneman & Tversky)
nodes.rationale: Nobel laureate Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky demonstrated that loss aversion — people's tendency to want to avoid loss — is twice as powerful psychologically as the allure of gains. This explains why the forces opposing change (habits of the present, anxiety about the new) are so powerful. Customers weigh potential losses more heavily than potential gains when deciding whether to fire an existing solution and hire something new.

nodes.label: The Signal and the Noise (Nate Silver)
nodes.rationale: Referenced for the insight that 'ice cream sales and forest fires are correlated because both occur more often in the summer heat. But there is no causation.' Also: 'The most calamitous failures of prediction usually have a lot in common. We focus on those signals that tell a story about the world as we would like it to be, not how it really is.'

nodes.label: Paradigm Shift (Thomas Kuhn)
nodes.rationale: From The Structure of Scientific Revolutions: breakthrough moments represent 'paradigm shifts' where 'Scientists see new and different things when looking with familiar instruments in places they have looked before.' Jobs Theory enables this kind of paradigm shift in how companies see their markets.

nodes.label: Drucker on What Customers Buy
nodes.rationale: Peter Drucker: 'The customer rarely buys what the company thinks it sells him.' Along with Levitt's quarter-inch hole insight, this is described as one of the two most important marketing insights of the last century — and one that most marketers' actions contradict daily.

nodes.label: Bounded Rationality (Simon & March)
nodes.rationale: Nobel laureates Herbert A. Simon and James G. March: in decision making, rationality is limited by the information available, cognitive limitations, and finite time. This explains why managers gravitate toward active data (loud, structured, easy to process) over passive data (quiet, messy, context-rich). Managers respond to the information their situation creates rather than seeking out the information they actually need.

nodes.label: The Checklist Manifesto (Atul Gawande)
nodes.rationale: Referenced as evidence that creating and following a process (checklists) dramatically improves patient safety. Aligns with the Jobs Theory principle that processes integrated around the job create superior outcomes and are hard for competitors to copy.

nodes.label: Organizational Culture and Leadership (Edgar Schein)
nodes.rationale: Referenced for the insight that processes are a critical part of the unspoken culture of an organization. Processes enforce 'this is what matters most to us.' They emerge from hundreds of small decisions and belong to the company.

nodes.label: "If you can't describe what you are doing as a process, then you don't know what you are doing."
Intensive Care Medicine Origin (Dwight Harken)
nodes.rationale: In 1952, surgical pioneer Dwight Harken noted that patients were surviving complex surgeries but dying in post-op recovery because no set of processes ensured fragile patients received the array of interventions needed. 'How is it that everyone's doing what they're supposed to be doing, all the hospital's existing processes are functioning as designed — yet patients are dying?' He pioneered the intensive care unit, demonstrating that the critical-care job had no owner within any one hospital function. This illustrates why organizations need a 'job steward' — someone responsible for the customer's job across all functions.

nodes.label: Bernoulli's Principle / Flight Analogy
nodes.rationale: Early flight researchers observed correlation between feathers/wings and flight, so they tried to replicate bird anatomy. Bernoulli's principle revealed the causal mechanism: lift (air flowing across an airfoil). Bird wings function as airfoils, but you don't need feathers — you need airfoils. Modern aviation refined this through trial and error, defining precise rules for every circumstance (weather, air pressure, etc.). Similarly, innovation needs to identify the causal mechanism (the job) rather than replicating surface features (product attributes). The circumstances always matter.

nodes.label: "We're lost, but we're making good time!"
Sick Care vs. Health Care
nodes.rationale: Christensen observes that the US health care system is really 'sick care' — providers make money when people get sick, not when they stay healthy. This is a jobs misalignment: providers' job (treat sickness for revenue) conflicts with consumers' job (stay healthy and never think about health). Organizations like Kaiser Permanente, Intermountain, and Geisinger align jobs by assuming responsibility for cost of care, creating incentive to keep consumers healthy. The same misalignment happens in business when managers respond only to active data (problems) rather than maintaining focus on the ongoing job.

nodes.label: Feature Chase
nodes.rationale: When companies ask customers what features they want and build them without understanding the job, they end up in a 'feature chase' — 'navigating without a compass.' Intuit's TurboTax team fell into this trap, adding 150 customer-requested features without knowing which ones actually solved the job. The solution: understand the job first, then let the job dictate which features matter. 'We got into feature chase. Too often we'd go look at what customers were asking for and build it.'

nodes.label: Surface Growth
nodes.rationale: Growth achieved by selling more products to existing customers (low marginal cost, alluring profit) without understanding the underlying job. Examples: V8's proliferation of variants, DeWalt's expansion into other power tools after Black & Decker acquisition. Surface growth makes companies vulnerable to disruptors focused on a single job. Contrast with deep growth: solving the core job better and expanding from that foundation.

nodes.label: Confirmation Bias in Innovation
nodes.rationale: Described through the parent-teacher conference analogy: Christensen and his wife would leave with completely different interpretations because each heard what confirmed their expectations. In business, sales, marketing, R&D, and business unit heads each bring data conforming to their functional responsibilities and incentives. None reflects the customer's job. 'Decisions don't get made. They happen,' quoting Gerald Zaltman — by the time a decision reaches the leader, the data has been increasingly skewed through layers of confirmation bias.

nodes.label: Hire and Fire as Construct Terms
nodes.rationale: The terms 'hire' and 'fire' are not simply cute metaphors — they are constructs that help visualize how the processes of buying and selling actually work. They represent the dynamic, ongoing nature of customer choice rather than static, one-time transactions. This language helps managers see products from the customer's perspective and understand the competitive dynamics at play in the customer's life.

nodes.label: Job Steward
nodes.rationale: Most organizations have managers in charge of every function and product line, but nobody is in charge of understanding — and ensuring the company delivers on — the customer's job. A 'job steward' is needed: someone responsible for the end-to-end experience and for keeping the organization focused on the job. This role integrates across silos and ensures the job has a voice in decision-making.

nodes.label: The Parable of Google Flu Trends
nodes.rationale: Referenced as a cautionary tale about big data hubris. Google Flu Trends matched 50 million search terms against 1,152 data points to predict flu outbreaks but wildly overestimated cases for 2+ years. The errors came from 'algorithmic dynamics' and 'big data hubris' — the engineers' decisions about what to include in their models. Just because data can be computed doesn't mean it deserves the status of objective data. 'Is it directionally helpful? Yes. Is it objective reality? No.'

nodes.label: Museum of False Precision
nodes.rationale: A humorous concept from Tom Monahan (CEO of CEB): a proposed museum showcasing the superficial precision of quantitative data that masks its underlying bias. Illustrates the point that numerical data's apparent objectivity is often misleading — all data is created by people who decide what to measure, how to categorize, and how to interpret.

nodes.label: Inoculation Against Disruption
nodes.rationale: Creating the right set of experiences around a clearly defined job — and then organizing the company around delivering those experiences — 'almost inoculates you against disruption.' Disruptive competitors almost never come with a better sense of the job. They don't see beyond the product. This is why companies like American Girl and IKEA have remained dominant despite competitive attempts: they own the job, not just the product.

nodes.label: Akio Morita's Principle: Observe, Don't Survey
nodes.rationale: Sony founder Akio Morita advised against market research, urging instead to 'carefully watch how people live, get an intuitive sense as to what they might want and then go with it.' The Walkman succeeded despite market research saying consumers would never buy a tape player without recording capability. This aligns with Jobs Theory's emphasis on observation over asking customers what they want.

nodes.label: Positioning (definition)
nodes.rationale: Positioning is the act of deliberately defining how you are the best at something that a defined market cares a lot about.
quotes: Positioning is the act of deliberately defining how you are the best at something that a defined market cares a lot about.

nodes.label: Positioning as Context Setting
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: Positioning as Context
nodes.rationale: Positioning is 'context setting' for products. When customers encounter a new product, they look for contextual clues (messaging, pricing, features, branding, partners, customers) to figure out what it is, who it's for, and why they should care. Context can completely transform how a product is perceived — a world-class product poorly positioned can fail.
quotes: I like to describe positioning as 'context setting' for products.
Even a world-class product, poorly positioned, can fail.
Context can completely transform the way we think about a product.
Most products are exceptional only when we understand them within their best frame of reference.

nodes.label: Joshua Bell Subway Experiment
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: Positioning as Context
nodes.rationale: Washington Post experiment: Joshua Bell, internationally acclaimed violinist who sells out concert halls at $300+/ticket, played outside a DC subway station during morning commute. Of 1,070 people who passed, 27 gave money, 7 paused to listen. Total earnings: $32.17. Same world-class product, different context — the subway context (street performer, garbage can, tips) devalued the same music that was priceless in a concert hall.

nodes.label: The Two Positioning Traps
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: Positioning as Context
nodes.rationale: Two common ways companies fail at deliberate positioning. Trap 1: You are stuck on the idea of what you intended to build, and don't realize your product has become something else (the cake-becomes-muffins trap). Trap 2: You carefully designed your product for a market, but that market has changed (the diet-muffin vs. gluten-free-paleo-snack trap).

nodes.label: Trap 1: Default Positioning / The Product Changed
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: Positioning as Context
nodes.rationale: Product creators fall into the trap of thinking there is only one way to position their offering. The product was conceived as X, but through iteration became Y — yet the company still positions it as X. The road from idea to market-ready product is rarely a straight line; the product that customers love is often not what you started out to build. Because the transformation happens gradually, the creators don't notice it, but customers are left confused.
quotes: The road from an idea to a market-ready product is rarely a straight line.
Frequently, the product we end up with is not what we started out to build.
We stick with a 'default' positioning, even when the product changes or the market changes.
This transformation happens so gradually that we, the product creators, often don't notice it.

nodes.label: Trap 2: The Market Changed Around You
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: Positioning as Context
nodes.rationale: A product that was well-positioned in a market suddenly becomes poorly positioned, not because the product itself changed, but because markets around it shifted. Competitors evolve, technology shifts, customer preferences change, economic conditions and regulatory requirements transform. Your once-relevant positioning becomes outdated (e.g., 'diet muffin' vs. 'gluten-free paleo snack').

nodes.label: Default vs. Deliberate Positioning
nodes.rationale: Most products can be positioned in multiple different markets. The common failure is not deliberately positioning the product — settling for the 'default' positioning without considering alternatives. The cake-on-a-stick vs. cake-pop example shows how the same product can be a confusing 'Frankencake' or an intuitive 'lollipop for grownups' depending on the frame of reference.
quotes: Your dessert might be better positioned as a snack, and your email solution might make more sense if it were positioned as chat.
We generally fail to consider other—potentially better—ways to position our products because we simply aren't positioning them deliberately.
Any product can be positioned in multiple markets.

nodes.label: Four Signs of Weak Positioning
nodes.rationale: Four observable symptoms that indicate positioning problems: (1) Current customers love you, but new prospects can't figure out what you're selling. (2) Long sales cycles, low close rates, losing to competition at the last minute. (3) High customer churn shortly after purchase; customers constantly asking for features you have no plan to deliver. (4) Under price pressure — customers complain prices are too high because they see you as indistinguishable from alternatives.

nodes.label: The Five (Plus One) Components of Effective Positioning
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: The Five (Plus One) Components
nodes.rationale: The complete breakdown of positioning into components: (1) Competitive Alternatives — what customers would do/use if your solution didn't exist. (2) Unique Attributes — features/capabilities you have that alternatives lack. (3) Value (and proof) — the benefit those features enable for customers, backed by objective proof. (4) Target Market Characteristics — characteristics of buyers who care a lot about that value. (5) Market Category — the frame of reference that triggers assumptions about competitors, features, and pricing. (6) Relevant Trends (bonus) — trends that make the product more relevant right now.

nodes.label: Competitive Alternatives
nodes.rationale: What customers would do or use if your product didn't exist. This is broader than direct competitors — it can include 'hire an intern,' 'use a spreadsheet,' 'do nothing,' or 'suffer along with the problem.' This is the yardstick customers use to define 'better.' In business software, the most common competitive alternative is a combination of general-purpose business software (spreadsheets, documents, presentations) and manual processes. Understanding true alternatives reveals how customers categorize your solution.
quotes: The competitive alternative is what your target customers would 'use' or 'do' if your product didn't exist.
It's important to really understand what customers compare your solution with, because that's the yardstick they use to define 'better.'

nodes.label: Unique Attributes
nodes.rationale: Capabilities or features that your offering has that the competitive alternatives do not have. These are your 'secret sauce.' They can be technical features, delivery models (SaaS vs. on-premise), business models (leasing vs. selling), or specific expertise/experience. The key is to ensure they are truly different when compared against the real competitive alternatives from a customer's perspective, not just compared against products you consider competitors.
quotes: Your unique attributes are your secret sauce, the things you can do that the alternatives can't.

nodes.label: Value and Proof
nodes.rationale: Value is the benefit you can deliver to customers because of your unique attributes — the reason why someone might care about your secret sauce. Value should be as fact-based as possible: qualitative claims like 'people enjoy well-designed user interfaces' are too subjective. Your opinion of your value does not count as proof; the opinion of customers, reviewers, and experts does. Data or third-party opinions are difficult to refute.
quotes: If unique attributes are your secret sauce, then value is the reason why someone might care about your secret sauce.
Your value needs to be provable in an objective and demonstrable way.

nodes.label: Target Market Characteristics
nodes.rationale: The characteristics of a group of buyers that cause them to really care a lot about the value you deliver. Your target market is the customers who buy quickly, rarely ask for discounts, and tell their friends about your offerings. Big-picture characteristics (industry, location, company size) are rarely enough — you need to identify specific traits that make certain customers love your product more than others.
quotes: Your target market is the customers who buy quickly, rarely ask for discounts and tell their friends about your offerings.

nodes.label: Market Category
nodes.rationale: The market you describe yourself as being part of, acting as a frame of reference that triggers a powerful set of assumptions in customers' minds about competitors, features, and pricing. Choosing the right category means those assumptions work for you; choosing poorly means you spend effort battling wrong assumptions. Declaring a market category is like giving customers the answer to 'What are you?' A well-chosen market category makes your unique value more obvious to target customers.
quotes: Declaring that your product exists in a market category triggers a set of powerful assumptions.
Your market category can work for you or against you.
Market categories help customers use what they know to figure out what they don't.
A well-chosen market category will help make your unique value more obvious to your target customers.

nodes.label: Relevant Trends
nodes.rationale: An optional bonus component. Trends help customers understand why a product is important to them right now, not just what it is. Trends can make a product a more strategic and urgent purchase by aligning it with overall company priorities. Market categories and trends are not the same thing: market categories are collections of similar products; trends are characteristics that are very new and noteworthy at a given point in time. AI is a trend, not a market category; 'AI-empowered CRM' tells what the product is (CRM) and what makes it special right now (AI).
quotes: Market categories help customers understand what your offering is all about and why they should care. Trends help buyers understand why this product is important to them right now.
It's easy to confuse market categories and trends, but they are not the same thing.
Position your product in a market category that puts your offering's strengths in their best context, and then look for relevant trends.

nodes.label: The 10-Step Positioning Process
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part II: The 10-Step Positioning Process
nodes.rationale: A systematic, field-tested methodology for positioning any product. Steps must be performed in order because each component flows into the next: Alternatives → Attributes → Value → Target Market → Market Category → Trends.

nodes.label: Step 1: Understand the Customers Who Love Your Product
nodes.rationale: Make a short list of your best-fit customers — those who understood the product quickly, bought quickly, became raving fans, referred others, and acted as references. They represent the perfect type of customer you want in the short term. Looking at all customers gives muddy results; filtering to only ecstatic fans reveals clear patterns. If you don't have super-happy customers yet, keep your positioning loose — like a fishing net — until patterns emerge. Do NOT start with product features; that unconsciously compares you to competitors the way you see them, not how customers see them.
quotes: Your best-fit customers hold the key to understanding what your product is.
Think of your product as a fishing net. You have a theory that your net is good for catching grouper, but you haven't fished with it yet so you aren't certain what you might catch.
If you had positioned your tuna net as a grouper net in the beginning, you might never have figured out your best positioning.

nodes.label: Step 2: Form a Positioning Team
nodes.rationale: Positioning must be a team effort driven by the business leader (CEO/founder in startups; division/business unit leader in larger companies). Marketing cannot 'own' positioning — it's too broad and too important. The team should include representation from marketing, sales, customer success, and product development, each bringing a unique perspective. 3-12 people is the sweet spot. An external facilitator is highly recommended to challenge long-held assumptions and ensure all voices are heard.
quotes: A positioning exercise that is not a team effort driven by the business leader will fail.
Marketing can't 'own' positioning, in the same way marketing can't 'own' the overall business strategy.

nodes.label: Step 3: Align Your Positioning Vocabulary and Let Go of Your Positioning Baggage
nodes.rationale: The team needs a common understanding of what positioning is, the components, and how market maturity affects positioning style. Most critically, the team must agree to let go of 'positioning baggage' — the old ways of thinking about the product tied to its origin story. The product was created with a certain market and audience in mind, but may no longer be best positioned that way. Customers don't share the product creators' baggage; they know nothing about the product's history when they first encounter it.
quotes: The starting point of this growth was Arm & Hammer's willingness to let go of their old way of thinking about the product.
Market confusion starts with our disconnect between understanding the product as product creators, and understanding the product as customers first perceive it.

nodes.label: Step 4: List Your True Competitive Alternatives
nodes.rationale: Answer the question: What would our best customers do if we didn't exist? Focus on best-fit customers only, rank from most common to least common, and ignore edge cases and tiny competitors with zero customer mindshare. Understanding the customer's problem isn't enough — you need to understand the alternatives they compare you to. Alternatives often cluster into 2-5 groups. The foundation of positioning is the problem customers are trying to solve and how they perceive your offering relative to alternatives.
quotes: Customers don't always see competitors the same way we do, and their opinion is the only one that matters for positioning.
The best way to understand competitive alternatives is to answer the question, What would our best customers do if we didn't exist?
Many companies have weak positioning precisely because they don't clearly understand their true competitive alternatives in the minds of customers.

nodes.label: Step 5: Isolate Your Unique Attributes or Features
nodes.rationale: List all capabilities you have that the alternatives do not. Stay focused on features/capabilities (what the product has or does), not the value they drive. List every attribute, even if it seems negative to some. Start with patented features, look at customer feedback on why they chose you, include non-technical attributes like expertise, partnerships, delivery models. List broadly now; group later.
quotes: Strong positioning is centered on what a product does best.
Your opinion of your own strengths is irrelevant without proof.
Concentrate on 'consideration' rather than 'retention' attributes.

nodes.label: Consideration vs. Retention Attributes
nodes.rationale: Consideration attributes are things customers care about when evaluating a purchase — features directly connected to a goal they want to accomplish now. Retention attributes (ease of doing business, quality of customer support) aren't as important in the initial purchase decision but matter at renewal. Customers who don't see enough value to consider buying will never stick around long enough to experience retention attributes.

nodes.label: Step 6: Map the Attributes to Value Themes
nodes.rationale: Translate features → benefits → value. Features enable benefits; benefits put into the context of a customer's goal become value. Then cluster value points into 1-4 themes by taking the customer's perspective — group attributes that deliver similar value. The goal is not to highlight every feature, but to bring the most critical unique value front and center.
quotes: Attributes or features are a starting point, but what customers care about is what those features can do for them.
This positioning exercise is not about highlighting every little feature and attribute that our customers love.
In positioning a product, we're taking the most critical things that make us special and worth considering, and bringing the resulting unique value to the front and center.

nodes.label: Step 7: Determine Who Cares a Lot
nodes.rationale: A segmentation exercise focused on value affinity — which customers really care a lot about the specific value you deliver. An actionable segmentation captures easily identifiable characteristics that make prospects care, going beyond demographics/firmographics. Target as narrowly as you can to meet near-term sales objectives; you can broaden later. Best-fit customers are easiest to sell to and retain: they understand the product immediately, buy quickly, don't ask for discounts, tell friends.
quotes: An actionable segmentation captures a list of a person's or company's easily identifiable characteristics that make them really care about what you do.
Target as narrowly as you can to meet your near-term sales objectives. You can broaden the targets later.
If I wanted to increase my chances of landing a customer, wouldn't I want to target as broad a market as possible? The reality is the exact opposite.

nodes.label: Step 8: Find a Market Frame of Reference That Puts Your Strengths at the Center
nodes.rationale: Deliberately choose a market frame that makes your value obvious to the folks who care most. Methods: (1) Abductive reasoning — what types of products typically have those features/value? (2) Examine adjacent (growing) markets — overlaps or blurry lines between markets. (3) Ask customers, but be cautious — customers aren't positioning experts and will default to the most obvious market. Introduces the three positioning styles: Head to Head, Big Fish Small Pond, and Create a New Game.
quotes: Picking a market is like giving customers an answer to the question, What are you?
Position yourself in a market that makes your strengths obvious to the folks you want to sell to.

nodes.label: Head to Head: Positioning to Win an Existing Market
nodes.rationale: Aiming to be the leader in an existing market by convincing customers you're the best at delivering the solution using the currently established criteria. Use when you're already the market leader and the status quo suits you, OR when a defined market exists but no strong leader has been established yet. For small companies launching new products, this style is rarely a good choice — it's like trying to out-cola Coke. Advantage: you don't have to teach buyers about the category. Disadvantage: you must fit within their existing definition.
quotes: As a startup, we could not credibly compete in this market, let alone win it.
Trying to beat an established market leader at their own game is a bit like trying to out-cola Coke.

nodes.label: Big Fish, Small Pond: Positioning to Win a Subsegment of an Existing Market
nodes.rationale: Dominate a piece of an existing market by targeting buyers with different requirements not met by the overall market leader. You leverage what buyers know about the broader category but focus on specific unmet needs of the subsegment. The subsegment must be easily identifiable and large enough to meet business goals. Advantages: easier targeting, value proposition can be highly specific, word-of-mouth accelerates within tight communities, you can expand adjacently later. The Bic 'For Her' failure illustrates the danger when the subsegment doesn't have a real unmet need.
quotes: Dominating a small piece of the market is generally much easier than attempting to directly take on a larger leader.
Word-of-mouth marketing happens most naturally in tight market subsegments.
You are simply unselecting the part of the market that was never going to buy your product anyway.

nodes.label: Create a New Game: Positioning to Win a Market You Create
nodes.rationale: Creating a new market category from scratch. First prove to customers that the new category deserves to exist, then define its parameters, then position yourself as leader. Use only when no existing category puts focus on your true differentiators, or when your company is large/powerful enough to capture attention. Requires teaching customers about the problem first, rather than the solution. Very difficult to execute but rewards are massive — you can tailor the market boundaries exactly to your strengths. Startups commonly fail by building the market then losing leadership to a larger fast-follower. Typically requires deep-pocketed, patient investors for smaller companies.
quotes: Category creation is about selling the market on the problem first, rather than on your solution.
This style is very, very difficult to execute, but if you manage to pull it off, the rewards are massive.
The most common way startups fail at this style is by working to build the market and then losing out on establishing themselves as its leader.
Timing is also important in creating a new category. To help customers make sense of why this category hasn't emerged sooner, there should be a very strong answer to the question, Why now?

nodes.label: Step 9: Layer On a Trend (but Be Careful)
nodes.rationale: Optional but potentially powerful. The ideal overlap is the intersection of: product strengths + market context + relevant trend. Trends can turn 'boring' products into urgent purchases by aligning with customer priorities. Dangers: (1) Describing a trend without declaring a market makes your product cool but baffling. (2) Focusing on the trend and market but not showing the link to your solution. (3) Using hype without a real connection (Long Blockchain example). If no trend fits naturally, it's better to be successful and boring than fashionable and bewildering.
quotes: It's always better to be a little boring than completely baffling.
Trends can only be used when they have a clear link to your product. Start by making the connection between your product and the market obvious.
Don't layer on a trend just for the sake of being trendy—it's better to be successful and boring, rather than fashionable and bewildering.

nodes.label: Step 10: Capture Your Positioning so It Can Be Shared
nodes.rationale: Positioning on its own isn't useful — it must be shared across the organization to inform branding, marketing campaigns, sales strategy, product decisions, and customer-success strategy. Rejects the traditional positioning statement as too brief and too awkward. Recommends: (1) a detailed positioning document breaking down each component and their interactions, (2) a single-page concise version, and (3) a positioning canvas that lines up competitive alternatives, attributes, value, and customer segments in relationship to each other.

nodes.label: Positioning Canvas
nodes.rationale: A structured template for capturing positioning. Includes: product name and one-line description, market category (and subcategory), competitive alternatives, unique attributes, value, and 'who cares a lot' (target customer characteristics). Structured to show the relationship between components in a columnar layout where alternatives map to attributes which map to value which maps to target segments.

nodes.label: Critique of the Traditional Positioning Statement
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: The Five (Plus One) Components
nodes.rationale: Dunford's five-part critique of the FOR target buyers / IS A market category / WHICH PROVIDES / UNLIKE primary competitor / WHICH PROVIDES template: (1) It assumes you know the answers — you just plug in current assumptions without testing them. (2) It reinforces the status quo rather than encouraging creative thinking about alternatives. (3) It doesn't give hints about what to do next — nobody uses the output. (4) It's hard to remember or parse. (5) The output is awkward mumbo jumbo. The worst part: it assumes you already know the answers rather than helping you discover them.
quotes: The worst part of a positioning statement exercise is that it assumes you know the answers.
What do you call an exercise that produces something that never gets used? A big waste of time, that's what you call it.
This statement might pass for a way to capture aspects of your positioning, but great positioning is more than just articulating your assumptions.

nodes.label: Sales Story
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part III: After Positioning
nodes.rationale: The narrative arc for translating positioning into a sales pitch: (1) Define the problem your solution was designed to solve. (2) Describe how customers attempt to solve it today and where current solutions fall short. (3) Describe 'the perfect world' — what an ideal solution would look like given the problem and limitations. (4) Introduce the product positioned in the relevant market category. (5) Detail each value theme with how the solution enables that value. (6) Handle common objections, add case studies or customer lists. (7) Call to action — what you want the prospect to do next. The sales story should be crafted before messaging.

nodes.label: Master Messaging Document
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part III: After Positioning
nodes.rationale: A single source of truth for baseline messaging. Without it, messaging starts to creep as new campaigns build on modified versions of previous messages, and eventually the language deviates from the agreed positioning. Every campaign or material starts from this document as the baseline.

nodes.label: Best-Fit Customer
nodes.rationale: Customers who understood your product immediately, bought quickly, became raving fans, referred others, and act as references. They don't just like your product — they love it. Marketing and selling to them doesn't take much effort once found — they are 'buying' as much as you are 'selling.' The broader your focus, the more difficult it is to connect with any prospects.
quotes: Your best-fit customers hold the key to understanding what your product is.
Once we narrowed in on the happiest customers, we went looking for the reasons they were so satisfied while others were not.

nodes.label: Customer Positioning vs. Investor Positioning
nodes.rationale: Must be kept separate. Investors want vision — what the company will be in 5-10 years, how you'll lead a large, fast-growing market. Customers want immediate value and immediate pain relief. Investor pitch decks and sales decks should look dramatically different. The word 'disruption' means opportunity to investors but can mean 'replacement of things we've invested in' to customers.
quotes: Investors are investing in what your company will be in the future; customers are buying a solution to a problem they have right now.
Absolutely not! Investors are investing in what your company will be in the future; customers are buying a solution to a problem they have right now.

nodes.label: Product Positioning vs. Company Positioning
nodes.rationale: Single-product companies should position product and company as the same thing — don't make customers figure out two things. Multi-product companies need separate but linked positioning for each product and for the company umbrella. If most customers first encounter the company through a particular product, position that product first. If the company mainly sells products together, position the company first.

nodes.label: Case Study: Cake Pop (Frankencake vs. Deliberate Positioning)
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: Positioning as Context
nodes.rationale: The bakery thought experiment. Positioning the exact same product as 'cake on a stick' (Frankencake — confusing, wrong) vs. 'cake pop' (lollipop for grownups — intuitive, the stick and ball shape belong, fits the 'snack' frame). Demonstrates how the same product can be positioned in completely different ways with dramatically different business implications for target buyers, competitive alternatives, pricing, and features.

nodes.label: Case Study: Database Repositioned as Data Warehouse
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I and Part II, Step 8
nodes.rationale: Dunford's first startup: a specialized database with a patented query algorithm for fast analysis of large data. Positioned as 'database,' prospects instantly rejected they didn't need a new database. A customer suggested they were more like a data warehouse. Repositioning as a data warehouse made prospects stop and listen — they intuitively understood the value. The repositioning also changed the company's product roadmap and self-perception. They could raise prices because data warehouses (fewer options) commanded premium pricing vs. commodity databases.

nodes.label: Case Study: Email-for-Lawyers Repositioned as Team Collaboration
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: Market Category
nodes.rationale: A startup built 'better email for lawyers' but the product lacked expected email features (no calendar) and its key unique feature (secure, context-aware file sharing) wasn't expected or valued in the email market. Happy customers loved it for what was essentially team collaboration. Repositioning as team collaboration software made the value intuitive and avoided customers looking for features it didn't have.

nodes.label: Case Study: Sampler — From Online Product Sampling to Direct-to-Consumer Sampling
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I, Chapter: Relevant Trends
nodes.rationale: Marie Chevrier Schwartz's Sampler initially positioned as 'online product sampling' confused brands who weren't sure they could handle physical samples. By aligning with the direct-to-consumer trend and repositioning as 'direct-to-consumer sampling,' they tied into brands' most important strategic initiatives. Deals closed faster and they now work with L'Oréal, Kimberly-Clark, and Unilever.

nodes.label: Case Study: Wave — From Accounting Software to Financial Services
nodes.rationale: Wave launched as free cloud accounting for small businesses (2010), grew to 500K users by 2013. As customers adopted digital invoicing with online payments and asked for payroll and other financial products, Wave shifted company positioning from 'accounting' to 'financial software/services.' Dropped 'Accounting' from their name, shifted business model to transaction fees. Grew to 4M+ customers by 2019, tracking $200B+ in income/expenses.

nodes.label: Case Study: Clearpath Robotics — From Robots to Self-Driving Industrial Vehicles
nodes.rationale: Clearpath's industrial robot could autonomously navigate, map, and manage fleets — capabilities not associated with traditional stationary manufacturing robots. Every sales conversation started with 'robot' then spent time explaining why they were unlike robots. Repositioned as self-driving vehicles for industrial uses, created OTTO Motors division with headlights and taillights. Sales accelerated; raised $30M; won Silver at Edison Awards.

nodes.label: Case Study: Arm & Hammer — From Baking Soda to Deodorizer
nodes.rationale: Arm & Hammer baking soda (invented 1846) faced declining sales in the 1970s as packaged food rose and home baking declined. The company let go of its original positioning and repositioned the same product as a refrigerator deodorizer. Revenue grew from $16M (1969) to $318M+ (1987). The repositioning enabled natural brand extension into deodorizing everything from cat litter to underarms. Demonstrates the power of letting go of positioning baggage.

nodes.label: Case Study: Wattpad — From Social Storytelling Platform to Entertainment Company
nodes.rationale: Founded 2006 as a platform for reading/writing on mobile phones. After explosive user growth, founders realized their platform generated incredible, well-loved stories that could be adapted for TV and movies. Launched Wattpad Studios in 2016, repositioning as a global multi-platform entertainment company. Their data and machine learning now help media companies (NBC, Sony, Hulu) find IP, build audiences, and guide adaptation decisions. First test was a TV series in the Philippines that increased station viewership by 30%.

nodes.label: Case Study: Janna Systems — From Enterprise CRM to CRM for Investment Banks
nodes.rationale: Dunford's startup in enterprise CRM, completely dominated by Siebel Systems ($2B+ revenue). Their unique relationship-modeling feature was uninteresting to general prospects. One investment bank loved it — it solved a critical revenue-driving problem. Repositioned as 'CRM for investment banks,' focused sales/marketing on that narrow segment, used banking terminology and demo data. Revenue grew from $2M to $70M in 18 months. Acquired for $1.7B — the largest Canadian software acquisition at the time. Classic Big Fish Small Pond.

nodes.label: Case Study: Eloqua — From Demand Generation Automation to Marketing Automation
nodes.rationale: Mark Organ's Eloqua (founded 2000) originally combined website tracking with email marketing for 'demand generators.' Positioned as 'demand generation automation' — resonated with target buyers but confused investors. As demand gen folks proliferated, revenue grew fast. Shifted to 'marketing automation' around 2006 to differentiate from simple email automation. Grew from $12M revenue (2006) to $96M (2012), went public, acquired by Oracle for $870M. Demonstrates category creation through deep understanding of a narrow customer set.

nodes.label: Case Study: Redgate Software — Database DevOps
nodes.rationale: Redgate, leader in database tools market with 800K+ users, found customers didn't see their products as urgent or strategic. Rather than changing market category, they layered the DevOps trend onto their positioning, creating 'database DevOps' content and training sales on consulting about DevOps transformation. Result: 100% increase in inbound leads, dramatic increase in multi-product purchases.

nodes.label: Case Study: Long Blockchain (Anti-Pattern)
nodes.rationale: Long Island Iced Tea renamed to Long Blockchain without any real blockchain strategy, partnerships, or connection to their business. Stock briefly soared on hype, then plummeted when investors realized the disconnect. Delisted from Nasdaq within months. Demonstrates the danger of using trends without a genuine link to your product.

nodes.label: Case Study: Bic For Her (Anti-Pattern)
nodes.rationale: Bic launched a pink 'Bic for Her' pen that was essentially the same as regular Bic pens but targeted at women. The subsegment had no real unmet need — the product was ignored and mocked into oblivion. Demonstrates that a subsegment must have truly distinct, important unmet needs, not just a demographic label.

nodes.label: Positioning Impact on Product and Pricing
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part III: After Positioning
nodes.rationale: A change in positioning impacts more than marketing and sales. Product roadmap must adjust — 'accounting software' and 'financial services' have different feature priorities. Pricing must align with market category expectations — CRM for investment banks could charge more than general-purpose CRM. Great positioning is strengthened over time with adjustments in features and pricing that reinforce the chosen market context.

nodes.label: Positioning Health Check Cycle
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part III: After Positioning
nodes.rationale: Revisit positioning every six months or when a major event impacts the competitive landscape or customer perceptions. Triggers include: a credible established competitor entering the market, new government regulations, economic shifts, new enabling technology, or shifts in customer attitudes and preferences. When revisiting, start at Step 4 (competitive alternatives) and work forward.

nodes.label: Component Interdependency and Order
nodes.rationale: Each positioning component flows from the previous one: Attributes are only unique when compared with competitive alternatives. Those attributes drive value. Value determines who the best target customers are. Target customers highlight which market frame best showcases the value. Trends must be relevant to target customers and used with market category. Starting in the wrong order (e.g., starting with features without understanding alternatives) produces positioning that doesn't connect with how customers evaluate solutions.
quotes: Because these relationships flow from one another, the order in which you define the components is very important.

nodes.label: Feature → Benefit → Value Chain
nodes.rationale: Features (what the product does/has) enable Benefits (what the feature enables for customers). Value puts benefits into the context of a customer's goal. Example: '15-megapixel camera' (feature) → 'sharp photo images' (benefit) → 'images can be zoomed/printed in large format and still look sharp' (value). For most B2B products, customers won't translate features to value on their own — you must be explicit.

nodes.label: Abductive Reasoning for Market Selection
nodes.rationale: A method for finding the right market category: isolate key features and their value, then ask 'What types of products typically have those features? What category of products typically delivers that value?' If it looks like a duck, swims like a duck, and quacks like a duck, it's probably a duck — the same applies to new products and their natural market frame.

nodes.label: Third-Party Validation as Proof
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part I and Part II, Step 5
nodes.rationale: Your opinion of your own value does not count as proof. Independent reviewers, analysts, customer quotes, and objective data count. If an independent reviewer states your product is easier to use, that's a fact. If a customer says in an approved quote that your service is much better, that's proof. Qualitative claims like 'people enjoy well-designed interfaces' are too subjective and customers won't believe them without evidence.

nodes.label: Continuous Positioning Evolution
nodes.source_file: Obviously Awesome, Part II and Part III
nodes.rationale: Product positioning constantly evolves. There is no need to ensure positioning fits where you'll be in 10, 5, or even 2 years. Great positioning resonates with best-fit customers right now and will evolve with them over time. Start narrow, broaden as you grow. Positioning should be revisited every 6 months.

nodes.label: The Mom Test
nodes.rationale: A set of 3 simple rules for crafting good customer conversation questions that even your mom can't lie to you about. The rules are: (1) Talk about their life instead of your idea, (2) Ask about specifics in the past instead of generics or opinions about the future, (3) Talk less and listen more. The core insight is that you shouldn't ask anyone whether your business is a good idea — it's your responsibility to find the truth by asking good questions.
key_quotes: The Mom Test is a set of simple rules for crafting good questions that even your mom can't lie to you about.
It's not anyone else's responsibility to show us the truth. It's our responsibility to find it. We do that by asking good questions.
You shouldn't ask anyone whether your business is a good idea. At least not in those words.
The truth is our goal and questions are our tools.

nodes.label: Talk About Their Life Instead of Your Idea
nodes.rationale: Rule 1 of The Mom Test. The big mistake is almost always to mention your idea too soon rather than too late. By talking about the customer's life — their problems, cares, constraints, and goals — you avoid biasing them and get real data. If you just avoid mentioning your idea, you automatically start asking better questions. When done right, they won't even know you have an idea.
key_quotes: Mom was unable to lie to us because we never talked about our idea.
If you just avoid mentioning your idea, you automatically start asking better questions.
They own the problem, you own the solution.

nodes.label: Ask About Specifics in the Past Instead of Generics or Opinions About the Future
nodes.rationale: Rule 2 of The Mom Test. People are wildly optimistic about what they would do in the future. They describe themselves as who they want to be, not who they actually are. By asking about concrete past examples, you get real, actionable data. Get specific about examples in the past to get real, concrete data.
key_quotes: Anything involving the future is an over-optimistic lie.
While using generics, people describe themselves as who they want to be, not who they actually are. You need to get specific to bring out the edge cases.
Folks can't be wishy-washy when you're watching them do the task in question.

nodes.label: Talk Less and Listen More
nodes.rationale: Rule 3 of The Mom Test. You can't learn anything useful unless you're willing to spend a few minutes shutting up. The more you're talking, the worse you're doing. When you interrupt to correct someone's understanding or pitch features, you lose a privileged glimpse into their mental model of the world.
key_quotes: You can't learn anything useful unless you're willing to spend a few minutes shutting up (even if you have something really smart to say).
The more you're talking, the worse you're doing.

nodes.label: Customer Conversations Are Bad By Default
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Customer conversations are bad by default. It's your job to fix them. Bad customer conversations aren't just useless — worse, they convince you that you're on the right path, giving you false positives that cause you to over-invest cash, time, and team.
key_quotes: Customer conversations are bad by default. It's your job to fix them.
Bad customer conversations aren't just useless. Worse, they convince you that you're on the right path.

nodes.label: Opinions Are Worthless
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Only the market can tell if your idea is good. Everything else is just opinion. Unless you're talking to a deep industry expert, asking for opinions is self-indulgent noise with a high risk of false positives.
key_quotes: Opinions are worthless.
Only the market can tell if your idea is good. Everything else is just opinion.

nodes.label: People Will Lie If They Think It's What You Want to Hear
