nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: People will lie to you if they think it's what you want to hear. This is the fundamental problem that The Mom Test solves — by asking about their life and specific past, they can't easily lie.
key_quotes: People will lie to you if they think it's what you want to hear.

nodes.label: They Own the Problem, You Own the Solution
nodes.rationale: A core distribution of responsibility in customer conversations. You aren't allowed to tell them what their problem is, and in return, they aren't allowed to tell you what to build. Deciding what to build is your job. You gather information about customers' lives and then take your own visionary leap to a solution.
key_quotes: You aren't allowed to tell them what their problem is, and in return, they aren't allowed to tell you what to build.
They own the problem, you own the solution.
Deciding what to build is your job.

nodes.label: People Know Their Problems, Not How to Solve Them
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: People know what their problems are, but they don't know how to solve those problems. This is why building by committee (collecting feature requests) fails. You need to understand the motivations behind requests, not just collect them.
key_quotes: People know what their problems are, but they don't know how to solve those problems.

nodes.label: Show, Not Tell
nodes.rationale: Whenever possible, learn through your customers' actions instead of their opinions. Watching someone do a task will show you where the problems and inefficiencies really are, not where the customer thinks they are. If you can't be there in person, asking them to talk you through the last time it happened still offers many of the benefits.
key_quotes: Watching someone do a task will show you where the problems and inefficiencies really are, not where the customer thinks they are.

nodes.label: If They Haven't Looked, They Won't Buy
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: If they haven't looked for ways of solving it already, they're not going to look for (or buy) yours. If someone claims a problem is important but hasn't even Googled for a solution, the problem doesn't actually matter to them.
key_quotes: If they haven't looked for ways of solving it already, they're not going to look for (or buy) yours.

nodes.label: You're Shooting Blind Until You Understand Their Goals
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: You're shooting blind until you understand their goals. Questions like 'why do you bother' point toward customer motivations and reveal the real problem beneath the perceived one.
key_quotes: You're shooting blind until you understand their goals.

nodes.label: Some Problems Don't Actually Matter
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Some problems have big, costly implications. Others exist but don't actually matter. Asking 'what are the implications of that?' distinguishes between I-will-pay-to-solve-that problems and thats-kind-of-annoying problems.
key_quotes: Some problems don't actually matter.

nodes.label: They'll Show You What It's Worth
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: While it's rare for someone to tell you precisely what they'll pay you, they'll often show you what it's worth to them. Ask how they currently solve the problem, how much it costs them, and how much time it takes.
key_quotes: While it's rare for someone to tell you precisely what they'll pay you, they'll often show you what it's worth to them.

nodes.label: People Want to Help If Given an Excuse
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: People want to help you, but will rarely do so unless you give them an excuse to do so. End every conversation with 'Is there anything else I should have asked?' and 'Who else should I talk to?'
key_quotes: People want to help you, but will rarely do so unless you give them an excuse to do so.

nodes.label: Measure of a Useful Conversation
nodes.rationale: The measure of usefulness of an early customer conversation is whether it gives us concrete facts about our customers' lives and world views. These facts, in turn, allow us to improve our business. A good conversation provides actionable insight, not validation.
key_quotes: The measure of usefulness of an early customer conversation is whether it gives us concrete facts about our customers' lives and world views.

nodes.label: The Three Types of Bad Data
nodes.rationale: There are three types of bad data in customer conversations: (1) Compliments — vacuous nice things people say to protect your feelings, (2) Fluff — generics, hypotheticals, and future-tense promises that have no grounding in reality, (3) Ideas — feature requests and suggestions that distract from understanding the real problem. Each type requires a different countermeasure: deflect, anchor, and dig.
key_quotes: There are three types of bad data: 1. Compliments, 2. Fluff (generics, hypotheticals, and the future), 3. Ideas.
They say that to bankrupt a fool, give him information.

nodes.label: Compliments Are the Fool's Gold of Customer Learning
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Compliments are the fool's gold of customer learning: shiny, distracting, and entirely worthless. They feel good but carry zero data. With the exception of industry experts who have built very similar businesses, opinions are worthless. You want facts and commitments, not compliments.
key_quotes: Compliments are the fool's gold of customer learning: shiny, distracting, and entirely worthless.
You want facts and commitments, not compliments.

nodes.label: Deflecting Compliments
nodes.rationale: The technique for neutralizing compliments. When someone says 'That's cool, I love it,' deflect by apologizing for slipping into pitch mode and redirecting to their life: 'How are you dealing with this stuff at the moment?' The best way to avoid compliments completely is to not mention your idea. If they happen anyway, deflect and get back to gathering facts.
key_quotes: The best way to escape the misinformation of compliments is to avoid them completely by not mentioning your idea.
If they happen anyway, you need to deflect the compliment and get on with the business of gathering facts and commitments.

nodes.label: The Three Shapes of Fluff
nodes.rationale: Fluff comes in 3 shapes: (1) Generic claims ('I usually', 'I always', 'I never'), (2) Future-tense promises ('I would', 'I will'), (3) Hypothetical maybes ('I might', 'I could'). The world's most deadly fluff is: 'I would definitely buy that.' It sounds concrete but is wildly optimistic and carries zero commitment.
key_quotes: Fluff comes in 3 cuddly shapes: Generic claims, Future-tense promises, Hypothetical maybes.
The world's most deadly fluff is: 'I would definitely buy that.'
Folks are wildly optimistic about what they would do in the future.

nodes.label: Anchoring Fluff
nodes.rationale: The technique for converting fluffy statements into concrete data. When someone makes a generic or hypothetical claim, anchor it by asking when it last happened, for them to talk you through it, how they solved it, and what else they tried. The key transition: from a fluffy question ('Do you ever X?') to an anchoring question ('When's the last time that happened?').
key_quotes: When someone starts talking about what they 'always' or 'usually' or 'never' or 'would' do, they are giving you generic and hypothetical fluff.
Follow The Mom Test and bring them back to specifics in the past.

nodes.label: The 'Would You Ever' Trap
nodes.rationale: The worst type of fluff-inducing question is 'Would you ever?' Of course they might. Someday. That doesn't mean they will. Other fluff-inducing questions include: 'Do you ever…', 'What do you usually…', 'Do you think you…', 'Might you…', 'Could you see yourself…'. These questions aren't toxic, but the responses are useless. The mistake is in valuing the answers, not asking the questions.

nodes.label: Digging Beneath Ideas and Feature Requests
nodes.rationale: When someone proposes an idea or feature request, don't just write it on your todo list. Dig into the motivations behind the request. Ask: 'Why do you want that?', 'What would that let you do?', 'How are you coping without it?', 'How would that fit into your day?' Ideas and feature requests should be understood, but not obeyed. Startups are about focusing on a single, scalable idea, not jumping on every good one.
key_quotes: Ideas and feature requests should be understood, but not obeyed.
When you hear a request, it's your job to understand the motivations which led to it.
Write them down, but don't rush to add them to your todo list.

nodes.label: Digging Into Emotional Signals
nodes.rationale: Any strong emotion is worth exploring. Is someone angry? Dig. Embarrassed? Dig. Overjoyed? Dig! Use nudges like 'Tell me more about that', 'That seems to really bug you — I bet there's a story here', 'What makes it so awful?', 'Why haven't you been able to fix this already?', 'You seem pretty excited about that — it's a big deal?' Emotions reveal the real depth of a problem or desire.
key_quotes: Just like feature requests, any strong emotion is worth exploring.
It's the worst part of his day and you're not going to figure out why? That's insane. You've got to dig.

nodes.label: Stop Seeking Approval
nodes.rationale: The main source of compliment-creation is seeking approval, either intentionally (fishing for compliments) or inadvertently (The Pathos Problem). Keep your idea and your ego out of the conversation until you're ready to ask for commitments. People's approval doesn't make your business better.
key_quotes: The main source of compliment-creation is seeking approval, either intentionally or inadvertently.
Keep your idea and your ego out of the conversation until you're ready to ask for commitments.

nodes.label: Fishing for Compliments
nodes.rationale: Intentionally seeking approval. You aren't looking for contradictory information. You've already made up your mind but need someone's blessing to take the leap. Symptoms: 'I'm thinking of starting a business... so, do you think it will work?' 'I had an awesome idea for an app — do you like it?'

nodes.label: The Pathos Problem
nodes.rationale: Accidental approval-seeking that happens when you expose your ego, leading people to feel they ought to protect you by saying nice things. Even if you give folks permission to be honest and ask for criticism, they'll still pull their punches. Symptoms: 'So here's that top-secret project I quit my job for... what do you think?' To deal with it, keep the conversation focused on the other person and ask about specific, concrete cases.
key_quotes: Accidental approval-seeking is what I call 'The Pathos Problem.' It happens when you expose your ego, leading people to feel they ought to protect you by saying nice things.
Once someone detects that your ego is on the line, they'll give you fluffy mis-truths and extra compliments.

nodes.label: If You've Mentioned Your Idea, People Will Protect Your Feelings
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: If you've mentioned your idea, people will try to protect your feelings. This is why you should keep your idea out of the conversation as long as possible. Famous entrepreneurs like Elon Musk can seek negative feedback because nobody worries about hurting their feelings — you and I must be more circumspect.
key_quotes: If you've mentioned your idea, people will try to protect your feelings.

nodes.label: Cut Off Pitches
nodes.rationale: Being pitchy is the dark side of seeking approval — demanding compliments by being annoying. If you slip into pitch mode, just apologize: 'Whoops—I just slipped into pitch mode. I'm really sorry about that—I get excited about these things. Can we jump back to what you were just saying?' If they insist on hearing about your idea, promise to tell them at the end of the meeting.
key_quotes: Anyone will say your idea is great if you're annoying enough about it.
Once you start talking about your idea, they stop talking about their problems.

nodes.label: Anyone Will Say Your Idea Is Great If You're Annoying Enough
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Anyone will say your idea is great if you're annoying enough about it. 'Won't-take-no-for-an-answer' is generally a good quality for a founder, but when it creeps into a learning conversation, it works against you.
key_quotes: Anyone will say your idea is great if you're annoying enough about it.

nodes.label: The More You're Talking, The Worse You're Doing
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: The more you're talking, the worse you're doing. When someone begins a sentence with 'So it's similar to…' or 'I like it but…', resist the urge to interrupt and correct them. They're about to give you a privileged glimpse into their mental model of the world.
key_quotes: The more you're talking, the worse you're doing.

nodes.label: You Should Be Terrified of at Least One Question
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: You should be terrified of at least one of the questions you're asking in every conversation. Every time you talk to someone, you should be asking a question which has the potential to completely destroy your currently imagined business. If you get an unexpected answer and it doesn't affect what you're doing, it wasn't a terribly important question.
key_quotes: You should be terrified of at least one of the questions you're asking in every conversation.
Every time you talk to someone, you should be asking a question which has the potential to completely destroy your currently imagined business.

nodes.label: Love Bad News
nodes.rationale: Learn to love bad news. It's solid learning and is getting you closer to the truth. If you have $50k and spend $5k to learn you're running down a dead end, that's awesome — you can use the rest to find a viable path. You're searching for the truth, not trying to be right. The worst thing you can do is ignore the bad news while searching for some tiny grain of validation to celebrate.
key_quotes: Learning that your beliefs are wrong is frustrating, but it's progress. It's bringing you ever closer to the truth of a real problem and a good market.
You're searching for the truth, not trying to be right.
The worst thing you can do is ignore the bad news while searching for some tiny grain of validation to celebrate.

nodes.label: A 'Meh' Is Better Than a 'Wow'
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: There's more reliable information in a 'meh' than a 'Wow!' You can't build a business on a lukewarm response. Lukewarm responses ('Umm, I'm not so sure about that', 'That's pretty neat') are perfectly reliable information that this person does not care. The classic error is to 'up your game' and pitch them until they say something nice — that only creates false positives.
key_quotes: There's more reliable information in a 'meh' than a 'Wow!' You can't build a business on a lukewarm response.
Lukewarm is a terrific response. It gives you a crystal clear signal that this person does not care.

nodes.label: The Premature Zoom
nodes.rationale: Zooming in on a specific problem before confirming that the person actually cares about the problem category. If you ask someone 'What's your biggest problem with going to the gym?' when they don't care about fitness at all, any response is dangerously misleading. The premature zoom is a big source of false positives because it leads to data that seems like validation but is actually worthless. Start broad and don't zoom in until you've found a strong signal.
key_quotes: Start broad and don't zoom in until you've found a strong signal, both with your whole business and with every conversation.
The premature zoom is a real problem because it leads to data which seems like validation, but is actually worthless.
Before you have solid evidence that you're fixing a meaningful problem for your customer segment, you can really mess yourself up by zooming in too quickly.

nodes.label: Start Broad, Zoom In Only on Strong Signal
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Start broad and don't zoom in until you've found a strong signal, both with your whole business and with every conversation. If you aren't sure it's a must-solve problem, start with 'What are your big problems right now?' rather than 'What are your big problems with X?' Only when you're 100% certain it's a must-solve problem can you safely zoom in immediately.
key_quotes: Start broad and don't zoom in until you've found a strong signal, both with your whole business and with every conversation.

nodes.label: Does-This-Problem-Matter Questions
nodes.rationale: When it's not clear whether a problem is a must-solve-right-now (painkiller) or a nice-to-have (vitamin), use cost/value questions to get clarity: 'How seriously do you take X?', 'Do you make money from it?', 'How much time do you spend on it each week?', 'What are you already doing to improve this?', 'What are the 3 big things you're trying to fix or improve right now?'

nodes.label: Look at the Elephant (in the Room)
nodes.rationale: Don't ignore the big, scary, lurking questions that could kill your business. Successful startups depend on multiple failure points. It's tempting to obsess over the most interesting failure point and ignore the others. Overlooking product risks is just as deadly as overlooking customer goals and constraints. Example: validating that teachers need a tool while ignoring that the poorest schools have no budget.
key_quotes: Sometimes we comfort ourselves by asking questions which don't actually de-risk the business or resolve those critical, big, scary, lurking questions. We ignore the elephant in the room.

nodes.label: Product Risk vs. Market Risk
nodes.rationale: Two fundamental types of risk in startups. Product risk: Can I build it? Can I grow it? Will they keep using it? Market risk: Do they want it? Will they pay? Are there enough of them? If you've got heavy product risk (as opposed to pure market risk), you're not going to be able to prove as much through conversations alone. Conversations give you a starting point, but you'll have to start building product earlier and with less certainty.
key_quotes: Product risk — Can I build it? Can I grow it? Will they keep using it?
Market risk — Do they want it? Will they pay? Are there enough of them?
If you've got heavy product risk (as opposed to pure market risk), then you're not going to be able to prove as much of your business through conversations alone.

nodes.label: Your List of 3 Big Questions
nodes.rationale: Always pre-plan the 3 most important things you want to learn from any given type of person. Your 3 questions will be different for each type of person. Pre-planning makes it easier to ask questions that pass The Mom Test and face the questions that hurt. Don't stress about choosing the 'right' ones — they will change. Just choose the 3 that seem murkiest or most important right now. Knowing your list allows you to take better advantage of serendipitous encounters.
key_quotes: You always need a list of your 3 big questions.
Always pre-plan the 3 most important things you want to learn from any given type of person.

nodes.label: Keeping It Casual
nodes.rationale: Strip the formality from customer conversations. Instead of scheduling formal meetings, have quick, casual chats. When you strip all the formality, you end up with no meetings, no interview questions, and a much easier time. The conversations become so fast and lightweight that you can go to an industry meet-up and leave with a dozen conversations, each providing as much value as a formal meeting. Learning about a customer and their problems works better as a quick and casual chat than a long, formal meeting.
key_quotes: Learning about a customer and their problems works better as a quick and casual chat than a long, formal meeting.
When you strip all the formality from the process, you end up with no meetings, no interview questions, and a much easier time.

nodes.label: The Meeting Anti-Pattern
nodes.rationale: The tendency to relegate every opportunity for customer conversation into a calendar block. Beyond being a bad use of time and setting expectations that you're going to show a product, over-reliance on formal meetings can cause you to overlook perfectly good chances for serendipitous learning. Being too formal is a crutch used to deal with an ambiguous and awkward situation.
key_quotes: The Meeting Anti-Pattern is the tendency to relegate every opportunity for customer conversation into a calendar block.
If it feels like they're doing you a favour by talking to you, it's probably too formal.

nodes.label: If It Feels Like a Favor, It's Too Formal
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: If it feels like they're doing you a favour by talking to you, it's probably too formal. Symptoms of formality include: 'So, first off, thanks for agreeing to this interview…', 'On a scale of 1 to 5, how much would you say you…'
key_quotes: If it feels like they're doing you a favour by talking to you, it's probably too formal.

nodes.label: Give as Little Information as Possible About Your Idea
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Give as little information as possible about your idea while still nudging the discussion in a useful direction. Keep your idea out of it for as long as possible. Even explaining that you're starting a company can take 5-10 minutes and biases the conversation.
key_quotes: Give as little information as possible about your idea while still nudging the discussion in a useful direction.

nodes.label: Five-Minute Learning
nodes.rationale: It only takes 5 minutes to learn whether a problem exists and is important. Learning how someone currently achieves a goal or solves a problem is also quick. Early conversations are very fast. The chats grow longer as you move from early broad questions toward more specific product and industry issues.

nodes.label: Critique of Steve Blank's 3-Meeting Structure
nodes.rationale: Fitzpatrick critiques Steve Blank's recommendation of 3 separate meetings (problem, solution, sale) as both difficult and inefficient to set up due to the time cost (1-hour meeting ≈ 4 hours with calendar dance, commuting, reviewing). The 3-meeting structure is critical for avoiding bias, but the first one doesn't actually need to be a formal meeting — it can be a 5-minute chat.
key_quotes: The structure of separate problem/solution/sales conversations is critical for avoiding bias, but it's important to realise that the first one doesn't actually need to be a meeting.

nodes.label: Commitment and Advancement
nodes.rationale: Once you've learned the key facts and have a product, you cut through false positives by asking for commitments. Commitment: They are showing they're serious by giving up something they value such as time, reputation, or money. Advancement: They are moving to the next step of your real-world funnel and getting closer to a sale. If you fail to push for advancement, you end up with zombie leads who keep taking meetings and saying nice things but never cut a check.
key_quotes: Commitment — They are showing they're serious by giving up something they value such as time, reputation, or money.
Advancement — They are moving to the next step of your real-world funnel and getting closer to a sale.
When you fail to push for advancement, you end up with zombie leads.

nodes.label: Zombie Leads
nodes.rationale: Potential customers (or investors) who keep taking meetings with you and saying nice things, but who never seem to cut a check. It's like your startup has been friend-zoned. Caused by being clingy and fearing rejection. Fix it by giving them a clear chance to either commit or reject.
key_quotes: When you fail to push for advancement, you end up with zombie leads: potential customers (or investors) who keep taking meetings with you and saying nice things, but who never seem to cut a check.
It's like your startup has been friend-zoned.

nodes.label: The Currencies of Conversation
nodes.rationale: Commitment can be measured in three currencies: (1) Time — clear next meeting with known goals, sitting down to give feedback on wireframes, using a trial for a non-trivial period; (2) Reputation risk — intro to peers or team, intro to a decision maker, giving a public testimonial or case study; (3) Cash — letter of intent, pre-order, deposit. A compliment costs nothing, so it's worth nothing and carries no data. The more they're giving up, the more seriously you can take their kind words.
key_quotes: A compliment costs them nothing, so it's worth nothing and carries no data.
The more they're giving up, the more seriously you can take their kind words.

nodes.label: Every Meeting Either Succeeds or Fails
nodes.rationale: There's no such thing as a meeting which just 'went well.' Every meeting either succeeds or fails. You've lost the meeting when you leave with a compliment or a stalling tactic. A meeting has succeeded when it ends with a commitment to advance to the next step. You have to force this resolution or the meetings drift along wasting everyone's time.
key_quotes: There's no such thing as a meeting which just 'went well'. Every meeting either succeeds or fails.
A meeting has succeeded when it ends with a commitment to advance to the next step.

nodes.label: It's Not a Real Lead Until They Can Reject You
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: It's not a real lead until you've given them a concrete chance to reject you. I never consider rejection to be a real failure. But not asking certainly is. A lost meeting can often be saved by just pushing for a commitment at the end while being brushed off with a compliment.
key_quotes: It's not a real lead until you've given them a concrete chance to reject you.
I never consider rejection to be a real failure. But not asking certainly is.

nodes.label: If You Don't Know What Happens Next, the Meeting Was Pointless
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: If you don't know what happens next after a product or sales meeting, the meeting was pointless. If you have to say 'Let me have a think about that and get back to you,' then you've ruined a perfectly good meeting. Know your next steps to benefit from good meetings.
key_quotes: If you don't know what happens next after a product or sales meeting, the meeting was pointless.

nodes.label: Don't Pitch Blind
nodes.rationale: Even in product-focused sales meetings, start with open-ended learning to get your bearings. Hard pitching gives binary feedback: you either nailed it or you didn't. That's okay for fine adjustments but bad for bigger questions. Ask learning questions that pass The Mom Test, then confirm by selling. This happens over both the life of your company and during a single meeting.

nodes.label: Earlyvangelists (Early Evangelists)
nodes.rationale: Steve Blank's term for first customers. They are the people who: have the problem, know they have the problem, have the budget to solve the problem, and have already cobbled together their own makeshift solution. They're the company who will commit way before it makes rational sense. In the consumer space, it's the fan who will front you money as a pre-order when all you've got is a duct-tape prototype. Whenever you see deep emotion, keep that person close — they are the rare, precious fan who will become your first sale.
key_quotes: First customers are crazy. Crazy in a good way. They really, really want what you're making.
Steve Blank calls them earlyvangelists (early evangelists).
Whenever you see the deep emotion, do your utmost to keep that person close.

nodes.label: In Early Stage Sales, the Real Goal Is Learning
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: In early stage sales, the real goal is learning. Revenue is just a side-effect. You're not trying to convince every person to like what you're doing. When you've got the information you came for (even if it's that they don't care), you can leave.
key_quotes: In early stage sales, the real goal is learning. Revenue is just a side-effect.

nodes.label: Cold Conversations: The Goal Is to Stop Having Them
nodes.rationale: Drumming up good conversations from cold leads is hard but doable. The goal of cold conversations is to stop having them. You hustle together the first one or two from wherever you can, and then, if you treat people's time respectfully, those cold conversations start turning into warm intros. The snowball is rolling.
key_quotes: The goal of cold conversations is to stop having them.
If you reach out to 100 people and 98 of them hang up on you? Well, nothing, except that you've now got 2 conversations in play.

nodes.label: Seizing Serendipity
nodes.rationale: Stay open to unexpected opportunities for customer conversations. The only thing people love talking about more than themselves is their problems. By taking an interest in the problems and minutia of their day, you're already being more interesting than 99% of the people they've ever met. If it's not a formal meeting, you don't need to make excuses about why you're there or even mention that you're starting a business.
key_quotes: If it's not a formal meeting, you don't need to make excuses about why you're there or even mention that you're starting a business. Just have a good conversation.

nodes.label: Find a Good Excuse
nodes.rationale: If it's a topic you both care about, find an excuse to talk about it. Your idea never needs to enter the equation and you'll both enjoy the chat. Examples: complimenting the coffee and asking about the story behind the beans; having a PhD student on the team who can claim dissertation research.
key_quotes: If it's a topic you both care about, find an excuse to talk about it. Your idea never needs to enter the equation and you'll both enjoy the chat.

nodes.label: Immerse Yourself in Where They Are
nodes.rationale: Go to where your customers are. Fitzpatrick wanted to build tools for public speakers, so he hit the conference circuit and gave free talks everywhere. By immersing himself in the community, he met loads of people and soon had all the connections and conversations he could handle.

nodes.label: Landing Pages for Conversations (Not Metrics)
nodes.rationale: Joel Gascoigne's Buffer landing page test: it wasn't the metrics or conversion rate that convinced him to move forward, but the conversations that resulted from emailing every single person who signed up and saying hello. Landing pages are great for collecting emails of qualified leads to reach out to and strike up conversations with.

nodes.label: Bring Them to You
nodes.rationale: Instead of always being on the back foot approaching people, find ways for customers to come to you. When you make the approach, they are suspicious. When they find you, they take you more seriously. Strategies: organize meetups, speak and teach, industry blogging, and getting clever with creative approaches.

nodes.label: Organize Meetups as Customer Learning
nodes.rationale: For marginally more effort than attending an event, you can organize your own and benefit from being the centre of attention. People will assume you're credible just because you happen to be the person who sent the invite emails. Fitzpatrick calls this 'the most unfair trick I know for rapid customer learning' — it also instantly bootstraps industry credibility.
key_quotes: It's literally the most unfair trick I know for rapid customer learning.

nodes.label: Speaking and Teaching as Customer Learning
nodes.rationale: Teaching is under-valued as both a learning and selling tool. If you're building better project management software, you probably have both expertise and a strongly held opinion. Spend time teaching at conferences, workshops, through videos, blogging, or free consulting. You'll refine your message, get in touch with potential customers who take you seriously, and learn which parts of your offering resonate — before you've even built it.

nodes.label: 7 Degrees of Bacon
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Kevin Bacon's 7 degrees of separation applies to customer conversations. You can find anyone you need if you ask for it a couple times. Everyone knows someone. Fitzpatrick stood on a chair in a coworking space and yelled for intros to McKinsey — 3 beers and 3 chats later, they had a diary full of intros.
key_quotes: Kevin Bacon's 7 degrees of separation applies to customer conversations. You can find anyone you need if you ask for it a couple times.

nodes.label: Framing the Meeting: Vision / Framing / Weakness / Pedestal / Ask
nodes.rationale: A 5-element format for framing meeting requests: (1) Vision — half-sentence version of how you're making the world better; (2) Framing — where you're at and that you don't have anything to sell; (3) Weakness — show your specific problem you're looking for answers on; (4) Pedestal — show how they, in particular, can help; (5) Ask — ask for help. Mnemonic: 'Very Few Wizards Properly Ask [for help].' Don't mention your idea. Frame expectations by mentioning your stage.
key_quotes: Vision / Framing / Weakness / Pedestal / Ask
The mnemonic is 'Very Few Wizards Properly Ask [for help].'
People like to help entrepreneurs. But they also hate wasting their time.

nodes.label: In-Person vs. Phone Calls
nodes.rationale: Fitzpatrick strongly recommends in-person meetings over phone calls. Phone calls damage the delicate power dynamic: people try to squeeze calls in between other activities and want to hang up quickly. Phone calls end up sounding more like scripted interviews than natural conversations. Plus, nobody becomes friends over the phone, so you won't get warm intros and future meetings. Start in person — it's too easy to use phone calls as an excuse to skip awkwardness.

nodes.label: The Advisory Flip
nodes.rationale: A mindset switch: don't go into discussions looking for customers. Instead, go in search of industry and customer advisors. This creates a helpful internal narrative: you're evaluating them as potential advisors rather than needing them as customers. It puts you firmly in control of the meeting and avoids a needy vibe. Willpower is finite — change your circumstances to require less willpower rather than trying to power through.
key_quotes: Don't go into these discussions looking for customers. It creates a needy vibe and forfeits the position of power.
Willpower is a finite resource. The way to overcome difficult situations isn't to power through, but rather to change your circumstances to require less willpower.

nodes.label: Keep Talking Until You Stop Hearing New Information
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Keep having conversations until you stop hearing new stuff. The UX community says you should keep talking to people until you stop hearing new information. This could take as few as 3-5 conversations for a simple industry and focused segment. If you've run more than 10 and are still getting results all over the map, your customer segment is too fuzzy.
key_quotes: Keep having conversations until you stop hearing new stuff.

nodes.label: Startups Don't Starve, They Drown
nodes.rationale: You never have too few options, too few leads, or too few ideas. You have too many. You get overwhelmed. You do a little bit of everything. Good customer segmentation is your best friend for avoiding drowning and making faster progress.
key_quotes: They say that startups don't starve, they drown.

nodes.label: Before You Can Serve Everyone, Serve Someone
nodes.rationale: If you start too generic, everything is watered down. Your marketing message is generic. You suffer feature creep. Google helped PhD students find code. eBay helped collectors buy and sell Pez dispensers. Evernote helped moms save and share recipes. By getting specific about who your ideal customers are, you can filter out all the noise from everyone else.
key_quotes: Before we can serve everyone, we have to serve someone.
If you start too generic, everything is watered down.

nodes.label: Customer Slicing
nodes.rationale: Drilling down into ever more specific groups until you've got a tangible sense of who you can go talk to and where you can find them. Start with a broad segment and keep asking: 'Within this group, which type would want it most?' 'Why do they want it?' 'What are they already doing to solve it?' 'Where can we find them?' Good customer segments are a who-where pair. If you don't know where to go to find your customers, keep slicing until you do.
key_quotes: Good customer segments are a who-where pair. If you don't know where to go to find your customers, keep slicing your segment into smaller pieces until you do.
This drilling down into ever more specific groups is called Customer Slicing.

nodes.label: Customer Segment as a Who-Where Pair
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Good customer segments are a who-where pair. If you don't know where to go to find your customers, keep slicing your segment into smaller pieces until you do. A customer segment isn't very useful if there's no way you can get in touch.
key_quotes: Good customer segments are a who-where pair. If you don't know where to go to find your customers, keep slicing your segment into smaller pieces until you do.

nodes.label: Segment Selection: Profitable, Reachable, Rewarding
nodes.rationale: When choosing which customer segment to start with, decide based on who seems most: (1) Profitable, (2) Easy to reach, (3) Rewarding for you to build a business around. Since you'll spend a lot of time talking to your customers, it's worth choosing someone who is both accessible and fun for you to be around.

nodes.label: If Feedback Is Inconsistent, Your Segment Isn't Specific Enough
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: If you aren't finding consistent problems and goals, you don't yet have a specific enough customer segment. When the feedback is all over the map, you aren't having 20 conversations with your customers — you're having one conversation each with 20 different types of customers.
key_quotes: If you aren't finding consistent problems and goals, you don't yet have a specific enough customer segment.
They weren't having 20 conversations with their customers. They were having one conversation each with 20 different types of customers.

nodes.label: Talking to the Wrong People
nodes.rationale: Three ways to end up talking to the wrong people: (1) Too-broad of a segment — talking to everyone; (2) Multiple customer segments — missed some of them (e.g. multi-sided marketplaces, apps for kids that need to sell to both kids and parents); (3) Complicated B2B buying process — overlooked stakeholders. Don't just talk to the most senior people; talk to representative customers.

nodes.label: The Learning Bottleneck
nodes.rationale: When all the customer learning is stuck in one person's head instead of being disseminated to the rest of the team. Owning the customer conversations creates a de-facto dictator with 'The customer said so' as the ultimate trump card. Symptoms: 'You just worry about the product. I'll learn what we need to know.' 'Because the customers told me so!' Avoid being the bottleneck by sharing learning with the entire founding team promptly and faithfully through good notes and team reviews.
key_quotes: When all the customer learning is stuck in someone's head instead of being disseminated to the rest of the team, you've got a learning bottleneck.
Owning the customer conversations creates a de-facto dictator with 'The customer said so' as the ultimate trump card.

nodes.label: Pre-Meeting Prep
nodes.rationale: Before conversations: (1) Know your current list of 3 big questions; (2) Know what commitment and next steps you'll push for; (3) Spend up to an hour writing down your best guesses about what the person cares about (creates a skeleton to keep the discussion on track); (4) If a question could be answered with desk research, do it first; (5) Do basic due diligence on LinkedIn/company website. Sit down with your whole founding team when you prep.
key_quotes: If you don't know what you're trying to learn, you shouldn't bother having the conversation.

nodes.label: If You Don't Know What to Learn, Don't Bother
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: If you don't know what you're trying to learn, you shouldn't bother having the conversation. Without figuring out what actually matters to your company and how to deal with it effectively, you're just going through the motions.
key_quotes: If you don't know what you're trying to learn, you shouldn't bother having the conversation.

nodes.label: Post-Meeting Review
nodes.rationale: After conversations: Review your notes with your team, update beliefs and 3 big questions, talk through key quotes and main takeaways. Also discuss the meta-level: which questions worked and which didn't? How can we do better next time? Were there any important signals or questions missed? This stuff is more craft than science — you have to actively practice to get better. Disseminate learnings as quickly and directly as possible using notes and exact quotes.

nodes.label: Who Should Attend Customer Conversations
nodes.rationale: Everyone on the team making big decisions (including tech decisions) needs to go to at least some of the meetings. Meetings go best with two people: one taking notes, one talking. Don't send more than two unless there's a good reason. Going solo is fine once you're good at taking concise notes, but it's harder to catch yourself going off-track. You can't outsource or hire someone to do customer learning — founders need to be in the meetings themselves.
key_quotes: You can't outsource or hire someone to do customer learning.
The founders need to be in the meetings themselves.

nodes.label: Note-Taking Symbol System
nodes.rationale: A shorthand system for customer conversation notes using symbols. Emotions: :) Excited, :( Angry, :| Embarrassed. Life: ☇ Pain/problem, ⨅ Goal/job-to-be-done, ☐ Obstacle, ⤴ Workaround, ^ Background/context. Specifics: ☑ Feature request/purchasing criteria, ＄ Money/budgets/purchasing process, ♀ Specific person/company, ☆ Follow-up task. Write down exact quotes in quotation marks. Combine emotion symbols with life symbols where appropriate.
key_quotes: When possible, write down exact quotes. Wrap them in quotation marks so you know it's verbatim.
Notes are useless if you don't look at them.

nodes.label: Index Cards for Customer Notes
nodes.rationale: Fitzpatrick's preferred note-taking medium: index cards or Post-it notes. One quote or learning per card with a signal icon. After the meeting, jot down date and name. Cards are great for random access and sorting — if you learn the problem doesn't matter, pull out all cards marked with a lightning bolt and find a new one that's already confirmed.

nodes.label: Notes Are Useless If You Don't Look at Them
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb: Notes are useless if you don't look at them. If you won't look at your notes, they aren't much good. The most important thing is that notes are lightweight enough that you'll actually review them yourself and with your team.
key_quotes: Notes are useless if you don't look at them.

nodes.label: Prep Questions to Unearth Hidden Risks
nodes.rationale: Two questions to unearth hidden product and market risks during prep: (1) 'If this company were to fail, why would it have happened?' (2) 'What would have to be true for this to be a huge success?' (from Lafley/Martin's strategy book). These aren't long strategy discussions — your gut reaction is enough. All you're trying to figure out is: 'What do we want to learn from these guys?'
key_quotes: If this company were to fail, why would it have happened?
What would have to be true for this to be a huge success?

nodes.label: Signs You're Just Going Through the Motions
nodes.rationale: Warning signs that customer conversations are performative rather than productive: You're talking more than they are; They are complimenting you or your idea; You told them about your idea and don't know what's happening next; You don't have notes; You haven't looked through your notes with your team; You got an unexpected answer and it didn't change your idea; You weren't scared of any of the questions you asked; You aren't sure which big question you're trying to answer.
key_quotes: Talking to customers is a tool, not an obligation.
Without figuring out what actually matters to your company and how to deal with it effectively, you're just going through the motions.

nodes.label: The Full Conversation Process
nodes.rationale: Before: Choose a focused, findable segment; decide big 3 learning goals; decide ideal next steps/commitments; figure out who to talk to; create best guesses; do desk research first. During: Frame the conversation; keep it casual; ask good questions that pass The Mom Test; deflect compliments, anchor fluff, dig beneath signals; take good notes; press for commitment and next steps if relevant. After: Review notes and key quotes with team; transfer notes into permanent storage; update beliefs and plans; decide next 3 big questions.

nodes.label: This Stuff Is Fast
nodes.rationale: The point of customer conversations is to make your business move faster, not slower. Don't spend a week prepping for meetings — spend an hour and then go talk to people. Don't spend months doing full-time customer conversations before moving on a product — spend a week, maybe two. Get your bearings and then give them something to commit to. Then go build your dang company already.
key_quotes: Don't spend a week prepping for meetings; spend an hour and then go talk to people. Anything more is stalling.
Don't spend months doing full-time customer conversations before beginning to move on a product. Spend a week, maybe two.
Go build your dang company already.

nodes.label: Hack the Gordian Knot
nodes.rationale: Having a process is valuable, but don't get stuck in it. Sometimes you can just pick up the phone and hack through the knot. Alexander the Great drew his sword and cut the Gordian Knot in half with a single stroke rather than struggling to untie it the 'proper' way. Don't over-intellectualize customer learning — just go talk to people.
key_quotes: Having a process is valuable, but don't get stuck in it. Sometimes you can just pick up the phone and hack through the knot.

nodes.label: Eternity Will Forgive
nodes.rationale: A recurring theme: it's going to be okay. You're going to make mistakes in conversations. Everyone does. Review with your team, don't beat yourself up. Most bad conversations can be fixed. People love startups and want to forgive entrepreneurs when they screw up. Go to the people who believed in you, thank them, apologize if relevant — they'll say 'don't even worry about it.'
key_quotes: Eternity will forgive our earthly blunders.
It's going to be okay.

nodes.label: The B2B Startup Pyramid
nodes.rationale: A layered validation framework moving from Vision → Market → Jury → Problem → Solution. Each layer must be solidified before the next can be addressed. The pyramid structure ensures entrepreneurs validate market and stakeholder assumptions before building any solution.
quotes: Selling to businesses without going through this validation is as foolish as our protagonist David has been.
components: Vision — founding team's early agreement, tracks and evaluates progress
Market — possible exemplifications of the vision; what markets could be affected
Jury — group of stakeholders with influence over purchase decisions (decision makers, influencers, early adopters)
Problem — the pain to be solved for the jury; root of any solution
Solution — product, usage, or benefits provided to solve the pain

nodes.label: Lean B2B Canvas
nodes.rationale: A lightweight complement to the Lean Canvas for tracking progress and assessing likelihood of solution purchase/implementation. Six sections: Market, Early Adopter Profile, Problem/Opportunity, Solution, Solution Risks, Solution Benefits. The difference between Solution Risks and Benefits visualizes the Implementation Score.

nodes.label: Three Critical Differences Between B2B and B2C
nodes.rationale: ROI, Client Relationships, and Decision-Making Process are the three areas where B2B customer development fundamentally differs from B2C.
quotes: Consumers love novelty; businesses just call it risk. — Ben Yoskovitz and Alistair Croll
Clients should be perceived as coworkers and not just customers. — Don Charlton
Companies don't buy, people buy. This is why salespeople often refer to B2B as P2P (Person-to-Person).

components.name: Return on Investment (ROI)
components.description: B2B transactions are investments in future profitability, cost reduction, timesaving, productivity or customer satisfaction. Three reasons businesses buy: to increase revenue, to decrease costs, to increase customer satisfaction. 'Consumers love novelty; businesses just call it risk.' — Ben Yoskovitz and Alistair Croll

components.name: Client Relationships
components.description: B2B markets are smaller, requiring deep relationships with few companies. Trust and stability are essential. Companies can't change product overnight. 'Clients should be perceived as coworkers and not just customers.' — Don Charlton

components.name: Decision-Making Process
components.description: Large B2B purchases require approval of 4-6 stakeholders. Three buyer types: Economic, Technical, and User Buyer. 'Companies don't buy, people buy... B2B is P2P (Person-to-Person).' Win results are the real personal benefits buyers expect.

nodes.label: Three Buyer Types (Strategic Selling)
nodes.rationale: From Robert Miller and Stephen Heiman's Strategic Selling: the Economic Buyer, User Buyer, and Technical Buyer form the 'jury' that must be won over in B2B sales.

components.name: Economic Buyer
components.description: Concerned with ROI. Acts as gatekeeper of the budget through veto power. Cares about long-term and risk-mitigation. 'Bring your economic buying influence information that serves as Windex for their clouded crystal balls.'

components.name: User Buyer
components.description: Concerned with user experience and day-to-day impact. Uses or supervises the product. Consults peers in user groups. 'There's a direct relationship between their success and your solution's success.'

components.name: Technical Buyer
components.description: Concerned with security and feasibility. Doesn't have final approval but can reject proposals. Approach is skepticism. Asks about specifications, implementation, and transition challenges.

nodes.label: Jury Roles Beyond Buyers
nodes.rationale: In addition to the three buyer types, two additional roles exist in the B2B sales jury.

components.name: Coach (Recommender)
components.description: Desires your success. Convinced your solution will help the company. Typically early adopters playing user or economic buyer roles. Has credibility with other buying influencers and strong personal relationships. 'It's essential to get a coach.'

components.name: Saboteur
components.description: Actively attempts to block a sale. Individual or team whose job feels threatened by new software or processes. Often from IT or the CIO's department. 'IT people are notorious for playing the role of saboteurs — try to avoid selling to IT if you can.'

nodes.label: B2B Startup Risk Scale
nodes.rationale: Three-scale model for assessing business opportunity risk. Domain expertise + solution expertise reduce risk significantly.
quotes: Startup founders with domain expertise and the skills to create quality solutions can capitalize on business opportunities much faster than entrepreneurs with only the ability to execute on a solution.

nodes.label: Minimum Viable Vision (MVV)
nodes.rationale: Coined by Alistair Croll. The minimum criteria a business idea should meet. A shared MVV helps a founding team focus business research around what really matters and close additional doors.
quotes: Your vision should rarely change. Your strategy should occasionally change. Your tactics should regularly change. — Aaron Levie

nodes.label: Founder Institute's Ten Rules for a Great Startup Idea
nodes.rationale: A checklist by Adeo Ressi of base criteria to test a business idea, used as a foundation for MVV creation.

nodes.label: Money Map
nodes.rationale: A visual representation of a prospect's expenditures to track spend flow. Objectives: understand key problems, motivations for spending, the prize/reward available if budget is transferred, and perceived alternatives/competitive landscape.

nodes.label: Value Proposition Exploration Table
nodes.rationale: A framework exploring problems around three core value propositions: increase revenue, reduce costs, increase customer satisfaction. Used to write compelling value propositions.

nodes.label: Hard vs. Soft Benefits
nodes.rationale: Classification of value proposition benefits in B2B.

components.name: Soft benefits
components.description: Cannot be quantified. Harder to sell. Examples: increasing employee happiness, improving user experience, providing better service. Can be turned into hard benefits if quantified.

components.name: Hard benefits
components.description: Can be quantified and lead to clear ROI. Easiest to sell. Examples: increased conversion, increased sales, cost reduction. Focus of the Lean B2B methodology.

nodes.label: Crossing the Chasm — Five Customer Groups
nodes.rationale: Geoffrey Moore's technology adoption lifecycle: Innovators, Early Adopters, Early Majority, Late Majority, Laggards. The chasm exists between Early Adopters and Early Majority. Beachhead strategy is critical.
quotes: A lot of technology startups fail to address the needs of these different groups and, as a result, are never able to cross the chasm between the Early and Mainstream markets.

nodes.label: Six Signs of Early Adopters in B2B
nodes.rationale: Characteristics to recognize early adopters in enterprise contexts.

nodes.label: Earlyvangelist (Early Advocate)
nodes.rationale: Steve Blank's term for early adopters who are also advocates/champions. They want to be first, like to brag about discoveries, are positioned to benefit from innovation, and have visibility and influence to bring solutions to attention.
quotes: Working with the right early advocates can substantially reduce the effort needed to sign your first customers, get case studies and convince other companies to follow.

nodes.label: Early Adopter Influence Grading System
nodes.rationale: Two-axis grading of early adopters: Personal influence (how many people can this EA influence?) and Company influence (how many companies can be influenced by a case study from their employer?). Graded 1-5.

nodes.label: Ideal Customer Profile for Early Adopters
nodes.rationale: A psychographic profile combining demographic data with interests, opinions, attitudes, values, and behaviors. Created from the 5-10 most influential early adopters. If insufficient EAs exist for the profile, revise the profile or reconsider the market.

nodes.label: Five Elements of Domain Credibility
nodes.rationale: What entrepreneurs must demonstrate to be perceived as credible by business prospects.

nodes.label: Five Dimensions of Contact Influence
nodes.rationale: What makes an introduction effective: Fame, Power, Longevity, Proximity, Trust.

nodes.label: Top Ten Things to Offer Early Adopters
nodes.rationale: Motivations to convince busy early adopters to meet with startup founders.

nodes.label: Five Principles for Contacting Early Adopters
nodes.rationale: Key principles for successful cold outreach to B2B prospects.

nodes.label: Cold Email Outreach Script Structure
nodes.rationale: Template for initial outreach to B2B early adopters.
components: Personalization — this message is from a real person
Relationship — who can vouch for us
Broad problem + Shared passion — creating common ground
Ego + Request for help — why we want their perspective
Social proof + Offer — what we can do together
Low commitment + Call to action — possible next steps

nodes.label: B2B Sales Funnel Qualification
nodes.rationale: Prospects become 'qualified' leads when they see value, have budget, you have access to a decision maker, and there's a timeline. Unready leads are nurtured for future conversion.

nodes.label: Double Diamond Design Process (Applied to B2B Customer Development)
nodes.rationale: UK Design Council's innovation process applied to B2B customer development. Alternates between divergent and convergent phases.

nodes.label: Three Parts of a Problem That Matters
nodes.rationale: The essential components a problem must have to be worth addressing.
components: The Pain — the problem or pain experienced
The Jury — the people to whom this problem matters
The Reward — the prize available for solving that pain

nodes.label: Hair-on-Fire Problem
nodes.rationale: A problem a businessperson is aware of without being told. They want and are actively looking for a solution, and are willing to overlook switching costs to pay for it. 'You're looking for the pain of a buyer, not just the pain of any user.'

nodes.label: Explicit vs. Implicit Problems
nodes.rationale: Explicit problems are those you know you have. Implicit problems are those you don't know you have (often disguised as workarounds like shared Excel spreadsheets). Explicit problems drive towards consulting; implicit problems can lead to disruptions.

nodes.label: Problem Interview Code of Conduct (16 Rules)
nodes.rationale: Rules for conducting effective problem interviews in B2B customer development.

nodes.label: Three Common Interviewer Biases
nodes.rationale: Biases entrepreneurs must avoid during problem interviews.

nodes.label: Problem Interview Meeting Structure (5 Steps)
nodes.rationale: The recommended structure for a 30-minute problem interview.

nodes.label: Core vs. Secondary Interview Questions
nodes.rationale: Prioritization of interview questions. Core questions address business drivers, problems, pain intensity, problem ownership, decision-making units, and buying processes. Secondary questions cover business processes and problem space details.

nodes.label: Techniques to Make Prospects Talk (Cindy Alvarez Method)
nodes.rationale: Methods to elicit deeper information from interviewees. Abstract problems by one level, use five-why sequences, and leverage silence.

nodes.label: Five Ways to Score Problems
nodes.rationale: Methodology for prioritizing discovered problems after problem interviews.

nodes.label: Startup Death Zone
nodes.rationale: The quadrant where solving a problem requires high market education AND high task/time investment. High risk area.
quotes: Unless you have something really hard to replicate, competitors will always copy.

nodes.label: Budget Displacement Grid
nodes.rationale: Grid for mapping problems against budget availability. Problems with large committed budgets indicate real pain and business priorities.

nodes.label: Solution Impact Grid
nodes.rationale: Grid for mapping problems against expected ROI and organizational impact.

nodes.label: Real vs. False Validation
nodes.rationale: The distinction between genuine market validation and misleading signals. Letters of intent, free pilots, and 'interesting' feedback are false validation. Only money changing hands constitutes real validation. 'The only true way to validate a revenue model is by asking a prospect to sign a contract and pay money for a solution.'
quotes: It's not a client if he's not paying. — François Lane
The only true way to validate a revenue model is by asking a prospect to sign a contract and pay money for a solution. There is no alternative to getting paid.

nodes.label: Lighthouse Customer
nodes.rationale: A prominent, referenceable customer that opens the market. Vontu's first objective was to close one large, name-brand, referenceable account — Bank of America. Took 18 months from formation but validated the model and established credibility. Sold for $350M to Symantec.

nodes.label: Endorsement Hierarchy (7 Levels)
nodes.rationale: Seven types of customer endorsements ranked by value, from displaying logos to presenting at conferences.

nodes.label: Problem Owner Profile (Buyer Persona)
nodes.rationale: A laser-focused profile built around the common problem. Includes demographics, work patterns, decision-making influence, budget ownership, and ROI calculation methods. Tony Zambito's Buyer Persona Canvas referenced.

nodes.label: Kano Model of Feature Classification
nodes.rationale: Noriaki Kano's model for categorizing product features by perceived value.

nodes.label: Kano Analysis
nodes.rationale: Questionnaire categorizing features by perceived value using positive/negative question pairs. 20-30 respondents in homogeneous segments can determine ~90% of product requirements.

nodes.label: Concierge MVP
nodes.rationale: A minimum viable product where you charge customers for manually performing the service without product development. The client may or may not be aware of the work being handled manually. Short-term solution to learn how to solve customer problems and drastically reduce development.

nodes.label: Three Components of a Valuable MVP
nodes.rationale: What every MVP experiment must include.
components: The experiment — What are you trying to learn with this MVP?
The data — What data are you collecting about your experiment?
The success criteria — What determines success or failure of the experiment?

nodes.label: Three Things Your MVP Must Communicate
nodes.rationale: The credibility signals an MVP must send to prospects.

nodes.label: MVP Do's and Don'ts
nodes.rationale: Best practices for creating effective MVPs.

nodes.label: Four Company Situations for Purchasing
nodes.rationale: The four situations a prospect company can be in, determining likelihood to buy.
quotes: People buy when, and only when, they perceive a discrepancy between reality and their desired results. — Robert Miller and Stephen Heiman

nodes.label: The 2x Solution Rule (Status Quo Coefficient)
nodes.rationale: To overcome the Status Quo Coefficient and mitigate adoption risk, your solution should be at least two times faster, two times better, and two times cheaper than known alternatives.

nodes.label: Implementation Score
nodes.rationale: Value (Actual or Perceived) – Risk (Perceived) = Implementation Score. Solutions that don't disrupt business operations are easiest to implement. Risk reduces perceived value.

nodes.label: Zero Overhead Principle
nodes.rationale: Coined by DJ Patil, VP of Product at RelateIQ. No feature may add training costs to the user. Solutions respecting this principle are easier to implement.

nodes.label: Alternate B2B Revenue Models
nodes.rationale: Classification of revenue models available to B2B startups.

nodes.label: Signing Authority in Large Companies
nodes.rationale: The lowest level of signing authority in a company is typically $500-$1,000. Pricing $1 above this threshold requires next-level management signoff, complicating and prolonging sales cycles. Selling above prospect's signing authority complicates deals.

nodes.label: Discount Alternatives (Carrots)
nodes.rationale: Better approaches than price discounting for sweetening deals. Discounts blur validation and are hard to increase later.
quotes: I'm more prone to give services away than recurring revenue discounts. The way I look at it is, if I give services away, it's like I'm paying tuition (for learning). — Steve Smith

nodes.label: Conditional Purchase Agreement
nodes.rationale: Built-in acceptance criteria for pilot projects using a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU). If core metrics are hit or solution functions as promised, company buys. Start the clock on billing from day one — risk-free (not free) pilot.

nodes.label: Solution Interview Meeting Structure (5 Steps)
nodes.rationale: The recommended structure for solution interviews.

nodes.label: Sales Readiness Checklist (12 Items)
nodes.rationale: Checklist to determine if you're ready to close a B2B sale.

nodes.label: Reasons Prospects Don't Buy (13 Objections)
nodes.rationale: Common rejection reasons and how to counter them.

nodes.label: Steve Blank's Price Sensitivity Technique
nodes.rationale: Push to extremes to find maximum willingness to pay. Ask 'If it were free, how many would you get?' then 'It's one million dollars.' Prospect reveals true max: 'I would never pay more than $X.'

nodes.label: Product-Market Fit: Three Engines
nodes.rationale: P-M fit is a mix of Revenue, Engagement, and Growth. Revenue is first validation; retention (engagement) is ultimate validation; growth/demand proves sustainability.

nodes.label: Sean Ellis Test for Product-Market Fit
nodes.rationale: If more than 40% of users would be 'very disappointed' if the product disappeared, the product has P-M fit. Survey available at survey.io.

nodes.label: AARRR Model (Startup Metrics for Pirates)
nodes.rationale: Dave McClure's customer lifecycle model: Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue. Activation and retention are the engagement components critical to P-M fit.

nodes.label: Pilot/Product Validation Metrics (10 Metrics)
nodes.rationale: Metrics to validate whether pilots demonstrate product-market fit.

nodes.label: Toyota's Five Whys for Purchase Root Cause
nodes.rationale: Applied to understanding why customers bought. Ask 'why' five times to reach root cause of purchase. Challenge clients to justify their purchase. Use negative selling to try to convince them the solution is not good for them.

nodes.label: Startup Pivot Pyramid
nodes.rationale: Pivots should be evaluated in reverse order of assumptions: Solution → Problem → Jury → Market → Vision. Keep one foot firmly in place. Change too many things at once loses track of what had impact.

nodes.label: Eight Pivot Types (Eric Ries)
nodes.rationale: Types of structured course corrections from The Lean Startup, applied to B2B.

nodes.label: Entrepreneur Success Odds (HBS Study)
nodes.rationale: Paul Gompers et al., Harvard Business School 2008: Serial entrepreneurs with IPOs = 30% success chance next venture; First-time entrepreneurs = 18%; Failed entrepreneurs = 20%.

nodes.label: 11 Common B2B Entrepreneur Challenges
nodes.rationale: The most frequent obstacles in B2B product-market validation.

nodes.label: Eight Techniques to Speed Up Product-Market Validation
nodes.rationale: Methods successful entrepreneurs use to accelerate B2B validation.

nodes.label: The Consultant vs. The Inventor Dilemma
nodes.rationale: B2B is market-first. Acting like an inventor (building a solution in isolation) gambles on odds of success. To succeed, entrepreneurs must be consultants regardless of previous occupation — solve a problem before creating a product. 'Very few people really are visionary entrepreneurs.'
quotes: B2B is market-first. To succeed in B2B, you have to be a consultant regardless of what your previous occupation was.
By acting like inventors in B2B, they gamble on their odds of success. It's hit or miss (and miss is the most common outcome).

nodes.label: Ignorance + Intent = Innovation
nodes.rationale: Eric Picard's formula. Not being an expert is not a bad thing. Outsiders see the industry differently and bring in completely new ideas. Experts can be shackled by old ideas, creating opportunities for strategic surprise (Taleo vs. Resumix example).
quotes: Ignorance + Intent = Innovation. Not being an expert is not a bad thing. — Eric Picard

nodes.label: Unfair Advantages (Jason Cohen)
nodes.rationale: The only real competitive advantage is that which cannot be copied and cannot be bought.

nodes.label: The Lean Startup Gap in B2B
nodes.rationale: While Lean Startup principles apply to B2B, techniques differ significantly. B2B validation is more relational: fewer relationships, more proximity to customers needed, analytics play a lesser role early on. Eric Ries's book focused on consumer startups, leading B2B entrepreneurs to misinterpret lessons.
quotes: The Lean Startup, at its core, is about innovation, not relationships. It does not capture the intricacies of creating mutually beneficial partnerships with business stakeholders.
In B2B, you're building fewer relationships and thus can't change the product overnight.

nodes.label: B2B Entrepreneur Self-Assessment Quiz
nodes.rationale: 8-question quiz to determine if B2B entrepreneurship is the right fit. Choosing 'A' 5+ times = B2B fit. Dimensions: relationships vs. mass users, solving pain vs. creating need, selling big vs. fast, small feedback groups vs. large, thought leadership vs. client expertise, salespeople vs. marketing, demos/whitepapers vs. SEO/SEM, ROI vs. UX.

nodes.label: Seven Deadly B2B Sins
nodes.rationale: Reasons why 9 out of 10 B2B startups fail to find a profitable product niche.

nodes.label: Consulting-to-Product Strategy
nodes.rationale: Starting as a consulting firm to pay bills while exploring business opportunities. Gives visibility into problems, builds proximity with customers, develops domain expertise. HootSuite example: started as Invoke doing web development and social marketing, then created productized solution. 'Starting off as a consulting firm can be a great way to reduce the risks of starting up, but to get out of consulting, your solution must be able to scale.'

nodes.label: Missionary Salesperson
nodes.rationale: A type of salesperson who helps define product features, finds a market, and creates a compelling value proposition — as opposed to selling a clearly defined product following an established process. At least one founding team member should have this skillset. Never surrender early customer development to hired salespeople.
quotes: The best salesperson is the founder. Others won't have the passion for it. — Bill Aulet

nodes.label: Prospect Reengagement Script
nodes.rationale: Template for re-contacting prospects for solution interviews. Frame as seeking advice/coaching. Offer market intelligence as WIIFM.
components: Personalization
Where you're coming from — what came out of last discussion
Asking for advice — 'We'd like to hear your perspective and advice'
WIIFM — 'We collected market intelligence useful in your work'
Call to action — 'Quick 20-minute chat?'

nodes.label: Perceived Comparables vs. Real Competitors
nodes.rationale: Perceived comparables and perceived value matter more than real competitors and real value. If prospects think your solution is just like Dropbox, you'll have a hard time charging more. Substitute products and the status quo are typically more dangerous competitors than large incumbents.

nodes.label: Fred Lalonde's List of Things That Don't Matter Early On
nodes.rationale: Only P-M fit and not running out of money matter in the first 12-18 months. Vanity metrics to ignore: analytics, responsive design, domain name, branding, press, etc. 'P-M fit is when you have five passionate customers.'
quotes: The only things that matter early on are P-M fit and not running out of money. — Fred Lalonde

nodes.label: Problem-Finding Orientation (Getzels & Csikszentmihalyi)
nodes.rationale: University of Chicago research demonstrating that problem-finders are more successful in creative endeavors than problem-solvers. Art students who spent more time considering composition (problem-finders) ranked higher in creativity and later became professional artists at much higher rates.
quotes: Much like in art, a problem-finding orientation in business can be the difference between long-term success and failure.

nodes.label: Thou Shalt Not Fall in Love with Thy Solution
nodes.rationale: Everything not validated is a risk requiring a testable hypothesis. Rank hypotheses from high risk (can kill solution) to low risk (improvement opportunity). Include riskiest assumptions in the first product demo.

nodes.label: Keep Your Day Job During Early Customer Development
nodes.rationale: Customer development can be done on the cheap with very little burn. Quitting too early fills schedule with low-value activities and creates pressure to build or sell too early. Finding P-M fit in enterprise is lengthy with dependencies on busy people.

nodes.label: Five Founding Insights from HireVoice Post-Mortem
nodes.rationale: Key lessons learned from the author's failed B2B startup.

nodes.label: When to Stick and When to Quit
nodes.rationale: Three questions before quitting: Am I still learning? Am I still moving forward? Will I regret walking away from this opportunity? Go as far as possible with validation until near certainty.
quotes: When you stop failing you stop being a startup. — Fred Lalonde

nodes.label: Rule Zero of Startups
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The fundamental principle guiding all startup decisions: 'Don't die.' Applies both physically (avoid burnout, suicide) and financially (avoid personal bankruptcy, don't bet everything). Even if the startup fails, the founder survives to try again.
quotes: "Don't give up. Don't die."
"Even if your startup fails, you didn't fail."

nodes.label: F*ckup Definition
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A f*ckup is a neglect of action resulting in a significant and preventable mistake. Something you neglected to do which has a significant negative impact that could have been prevented had you handled the situation differently. For most startups, multiple f*ckups in combination lead to downfall.
quotes: NEGLECT OF ACTION RESULTING IN A SIGNIFICANT AND PREVENTABLE MISTAKE.

nodes.label: Cult of Success
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The harmful mindset where people only present their best selves, connected to mental health issues, depression, loneliness, and low self-esteem. Works against founders by discouraging transparency about struggles and failures. Contrasted with learning from failure.

nodes.label: Survivorship Bias
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The logical error of concentrating on people or things that survive and succeed while ignoring those that didn't. Illustrated by Abraham Wald's WWII aircraft analysis—reinforcing planes where returning planes showed bullet holes was wrong; the planes that didn't return (hit in engines/cockpit) showed the true vulnerabilities. Making decisions based on outlier success stories like college-dropout billionaires is survivorship bias.
quotes: Making decisions based on the successes of outliers is a logical error called survivorship bias.

nodes.label: Dunning-Kruger Effect
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Cognitive bias where unskilled people unaware of their deficits are more confident than people with skills and experience. The journey from Mount Stupid (high confidence, low wisdom) through the Valley of Despair to the Slope of Enlightenment (high wisdom, high confidence).

nodes.label: Loss Aversion
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The behavioral economics principle that humans have a stronger tendency to prefer avoiding loss over acquiring gains. Losing $5 hurts more than finding $5 pleases. In business, fear of losing in one area overshadows potential gains elsewhere, stopping forward movement. Also explains why founders avoid starting (not starting due to fear of losing steady income).

nodes.label: Availability Heuristics
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The mistaken belief that whatever is at the top of our minds is more relevant or common than things we're not hearing about. Illustrated by 'Summer of the Shark' (2001) where media coverage made shark attacks seem more prevalent despite being on the decline. In business, used to influence decisions by making specific data points 'available' to decision-makers.

nodes.label: Anchoring
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: An arbitrary bias that becomes the focal point for all discussions. Once an anchor value is established, decisions are made in relation to it. Examples: three beer sizes where 'very small' (actually normal size) anchors people to buy 'normal' (large); 'only two items per customer' signs anchor people to buy exactly two; precise pricing like $493 instead of $500 anchors negotiation range.

nodes.label: Confirmation Bias
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The tendency to search for knowledge, facts, and information that support existing beliefs. In decision-making, it prevents smarter outcomes because it only confirms what is already believed rather than challenging assumptions.

nodes.label: The Toilet Test
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A quick decision filter for startup ideas: Does the idea cross your mind at least as often as you go to the toilet each day? If yes, it's worth pursuing. If it's just a fleeting idea that doesn't come to mind daily, flush it and move on.

nodes.label: Not Starting
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The most basic attitude failure: never launching due to fear, excuses (age, income, family), or loss aversion. Age is not a barrier—successful founders span from teens to sixties. Fear of failure keeps talented people stagnant. The fix is to act, tell people your plans, and treat failure as brave rather than shameful.
quotes: You can't win the race you don't start.
warning_signs: Endless planning without execution
Rationalizing delay with 'good reasons'
Waiting for the perfect time
Buying into age-as-barrier myths

nodes.label: Impostor Syndrome
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Letting doubt consume you to the point of inaction. If you don't believe you can do it, you can't. Doubt driving decisions is dangerous—investors won't invest in founders who doubt themselves. Related to Dunning-Kruger: skilled people undervalue their abilities. The fix is 'Fake it till you make it'—identify AS a founder/runner/musician vs. someone who 'just' does the activity.
warning_signs: Questioning own qualifications constantly
Attributing success to luck
Fear of being 'found out'
Saying 'Maybe I'm just not cut out for this'

nodes.label: Plate-Spinning
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Pursuing too many things at once, causing the most important project to suffer. A common offshoot of the passion pinch. The author's father called his ventures 'plate-spinning startups.' Manifested in the author's own life when he landed in jail due to spreading himself too thin across multiple businesses. The fix: do less, prioritize with a Not-To-Do list.
warning_signs: Running multiple startups simultaneously
Constant context-switching
Missing critical details across ventures
Inability to say no to new ideas

nodes.label: Lone-Wolf Syndrome
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The belief that you can be successful entirely on your own. Solo founders have full control and all profits, but also all problems, losses, hardship, and an increased risk of failure. Without partners, you lack industry knowledge, accountability, and complementary skills. The fix is to find mentors, get industry experience before launching, and build a pack.
warning_signs: Refusing co-founders or partners
No industry experience before launching
Underpricing services due to ignorance
No mentorship or external feedback

nodes.label: A Poor Business Plan
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Failing to produce a meaningful business plan. The number one mistake startups make. Without a plan, you work with your feet, not your head—all brawn and no brains.
