The only bad business plan is the one that exists solely in your head. The fix uses the Business Model Canvas and an iterative planning approach.
warning_signs: No written business plan
Pricing that doesn't account for intermediaries taking margins
No repeat-sales strategy
Revenue model that relies on hope

nodes.label: Idiot Industry
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Choosing a fundamentally unprofitable or unattractive industry. It's better to be an idiot in an intelligent industry than intelligent in an idiot industry. Uses Porter's Five Forces to assess industry attractiveness: threat of new entrants, threat of substitutes, bargaining power of customers, bargaining power of suppliers, and competitive rivalry.
quotes: 'It's better to be an idiot in an intelligent industry, than intelligent in an idiot industry.'
warning_signs: Industry with terrible EBIDA scores
Low entry barriers (easy for competitors)
High buyer or supplier power
Industry content with status quo, resistant to change

nodes.label: The Problem-Less Solution
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Starting with the solution instead of the actual problem. If you're trying to change a problem that most people don't think of as a problem, you're in for an uphill battle. The fix: spend 55 minutes thinking about the problem and only 5 on solutions (per Einstein). Focus on Tier 1 problems (painful enough for the target audience to care)—think headache pills, not vitamin pills.
quotes: 'If you're ever approached to invest in a startup that requires educating consumers on its necessity, keep your money and run.' —Tim Frank Andersen
warning_signs: Product that requires 'educating' consumers on its necessity
Digitizing something better done by hand
Wi-Fi/Bluetooth added to products that work fine without it
Solving problems no one actually has

nodes.label: Co-Dependency Collapse
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Relying on a single industry, client, or platform for survival. A Single Point of Failure (SPOF) that, if it fails, halts the entire system. Examples include relying on one platform (PlayStation, Facebook), one client, one industry, or one distribution channel. The fix is diversification while avoiding over-customization.
warning_signs: Relying on a single client for >30% of revenue
Product tied to a single platform
Sub-supplier to a single industry
No direct access to end customers

nodes.label: Terrible Timing
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Launching too early (before market/infrastructure is ready) or too late (after market has moved on). Understanding your industry gives timing insight. The fix involves building If-Then statements into the business plan and being ready to pivot.
warning_signs: Product requires infrastructure not yet widely available
Product launching into regulatory uncertainty
Product arriving just as market conditions shift adversely
Competitor with similar idea succeeded years later

nodes.label: Mini-Mindset
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Thinking too small too soon. Entrepreneurs tend to think 'too big' in early stages, but many fail because they think too small too soon. A small profit margin leaves no buffer for when things go wrong. The fix: apply the 10x Rule (Antifragile Rule)—multiply goals by ten, develop improvements that increase revenue tenfold.
quotes: 'If you think small from the get-go, when challenges arise, you'll be dead, end of story.'
warning_signs: 'All I need is a cozy little shop that pays my bills'
Planning for modest profits with no buffer
Dismissing scaling opportunities as unrealistic
Pigeonholing as a 'small business' out of fear

nodes.label: Doppelganger Danger
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Cloning an existing successful company without meaningful differentiation. Copy all you want, and you'll always be one step behind. The fix: differentiate in at least one (preferably more) aspects, using Porter's generic strategies of cost leadership, differentiation, or focus.
quotes: 'Ideas are a dime a dozen. People who implement them are priceless.' —Mary Kay Ash
warning_signs: Pitch described as 'X for Y' (Pinterest for men, Uber for dogs, etc.)
Identical feature set to existing competitor
No unique selling proposition beyond price

nodes.label: Unnecessarily Original
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Creating something so unique that there's no market for it. If your market seems empty, there's probably a reason—thousands of entrepreneurs have already investigated and found it unprofitable. The fix: formulate from familiarity—look at similar ventures and find ways to reinvent, streamline, or remarket.
warning_signs: No comparable products in the market
Creating a product for a problem no one has
Assuming originality guarantees success
Lack of repeated revenue stream

nodes.label: Competing with the Network Effect
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Attempting to compete against products whose value increases with user numbers (network effects). Very hard to build, extremely hard to break. Four types: Direct, Indirect, Two-Sided, and Local Network Effects. The fix: convert your liability to an asset by finding a complementary niche, focusing efforts, using the right business model, and ensuring compatibility with market leaders.
warning_signs: Launching a social network to compete with Facebook
No critical mass strategy
Underestimating switching costs for users
Competing on features alone against an entrenched network

nodes.label: Customers—What Customers?
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Neglecting to consult actual customers. Founders forget the customer is the true boss who can fire everybody. Making assumptions instead of gathering data. The '1% market share' fallacy: assuming capturing 1% of a market is easy when it's actually enormous. The fix: survey customers, use split/A/B testing, include data-based predictions.
warning_signs: Skipped focus groups and surveys
Assuming '1% market share' is easy
Business plan built on wishful thinking
No customer validation before launch

nodes.label: Messed-Up MVP
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Either doing the Minimum Viable Product wrong or skipping it altogether. Without an MVP, a startup is as likely to succeed as a team without an MVP player. The fix: use the MoSCoW method to prioritize MVP features.
warning_signs: Launching a full product without testing assumptions
Skipping prototype phase
No customer feedback before full release
Building features nobody asked for

nodes.label: Analysis Paralysis
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Getting bogged down in statistics, research, and testing to the point of never making a decision. Any decision is better than no decision. The fix: test like Tesla—release incremental features to real customers rather than waiting for 100% perfection.
warning_signs: Endless A/B testing of trivial elements
Never launching because 'more research needed'
Perfectionism blocking progress
Spending 5 minutes on creative, 1 hour on test setup

nodes.label: Cheating and Ripping People Off
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The temptation to cheat your way to success. Startups whose core value comes from stealing, defrauding investors, or ripping off customers. These always end badly and can result in jail time. The fix: go legit—building an empire takes time, patience, practice, and fortitude.
warning_signs: 'Get-rich-quick' business model
Doctored success metrics
Core value from exploiting others
Fudging numbers for investor buy-in

nodes.label: Getting Funded (Too Early / Wrong Focus)
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Focusing on funding before building a product the market wants. Early obsession with pitch decks, VCs, and valuations distracts from customers and product. Money doesn't solve failures—it can hide a crap product. The fix: prove your case first (get customers), then seek funding. The egg came before the chicken.
warning_signs: Founder's primary focus is pitch deck, not product
Funding sought before any customer validation
VC-pleasing mindset over customer-pleasing
High-reputation founders riding on past success rather than current product

nodes.label: Convoluting Capital
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Overcomplicating the funding process to the point of paralysis or complete avoidance. Either tying yourself in knots trying to figure out where money comes from, or ignoring capital needs entirely. The fix: know how to pitch—have a pitch deck with clear value proposition and key metrics; pitch like a king, not a beggar.
warning_signs: Assuming raising capital is impossibly complicated
Ignoring funding needs until crisis
Only pursuing VC when other sources exist
Avoiding funding conversations entirely

nodes.label: Bad Budgeting
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Miscalculating the cost of running company functions. Failing to assess burn rate accurately. Common mistake: hiring too many employees too quickly, taking on too large a lease. The fix: isolate fixed vs. variable costs for the next 12 months, understand CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost) and churn rate, run dynamic budget scenarios.
warning_signs: Rapid hiring without revenue growth
Taking on expensive facilities prematurely
Not tracking burn rate
Building own infrastructure instead of using cloud services

nodes.label: The Cap-Table Capsize
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Losing control of your company through equity dilution. One of the few mistakes that cannot be undone. Once you've sold too much equity, you can't easily buy it back. Malignant investors can use reverse stock splits, stock dilution, and down rounds to reduce 99% ownership to 1%. The fix: vet investors thoroughly, accept dilution strategically, always hire a contract attorney.
warning_signs: Not maintaining a proper cap table
Giving away too much equity too early
No legal counsel reviewing investment terms
Losing board control or voting power

nodes.label: Amateur Accounting
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Founders with zero accounting training handling their own finances, then outsourcing to cheap, unreliable services. The fix: hire a professional accountant early. Types include management accountants, financial advisors, project accountants, cost accountants, investment accountants, and staff accountants.
warning_signs: DIY bookkeeping by founder with no training
Outsourcing accounting to cheapest overseas provider
Bills paid twice or not at all
Tax issues or IRS problems

nodes.label: Inadequate Early Profitability
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Taking too long to reach profitability. The clock is ticking—typically 2-4 years as a rule of thumb. Don't assume another funding round will save you. The fix: don't be afraid to make concessions; launch faster rather than perfecting indefinitely.
warning_signs: No path to profitability in sight after 2+ years
Relying on 'future' market adoption that hasn't materialized
Product too complex for broad adoption
Burn rate as a KPI (dot-com era mentality)

nodes.label: Death by Overfunding
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Too much money too early puts undue pressure on a young company to grow rapidly. Coined by Sramana Mitra. Hyper-growth mentality promoted by VCs can destroy a company's core identity and lead to mismanagement. The fix: bootstrap initially; only seek outside capital when equity post-infusion will be greater than pre-investment.
quotes: 'Never eat more than you can lift.' —Miss Piggy
warning_signs: Accepting massive VC funding before organic growth is stable
Hyper-growth mentality replacing core mission
Losing company identity post-funding
VC pressure to scale faster than is healthy

nodes.label: The FNAC (Feature, Not A Company)
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A set of product features that doesn't add up to a stand-alone business. VC shorthand for a service component that isn't a full company. Three rules: (1) Your company isn't your product, (2) Your customer experience isn't your product, (3) Your marketing plan isn't your product. The fix: be crystal clear about what your product is and keep it simple.
warning_signs: Product is really just a feature of a larger platform
No clear path to independent profitability
Pitch confuses company identity with product
Serial acquisitions of unrelated companies to define identity

nodes.label: The Big Bang Release
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Releasing products with a massive, showy launch of multiple features at once. Dangerous and might end up burning your business. Leads to long development cycles, missed deadlines, and products that don't meet customer needs. The fix: parse offerings into smaller, more frequent releases (growth hacking approach).
warning_signs: Long development cycles without customer feedback
Tying release to rebranding or major event
Ignoring team concerns about timeline/resources
All-or-nothing launch mentality

nodes.label: The Mashup
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Mixing two good things does not necessarily make something great. Adding Wi-Fi, AI, or other technology to an existing product without solving a real problem. Common with intrapreneurs (old companies trying to infuse startup hipness). The fix: keep mashups for elevator pitches; create something genuinely problem-solving.
warning_signs: Product pitched as 'X meets Y'
Adding technology (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth/AI) without solving a new problem
Old company trying to be startup-cool
Features that remove the core convenience of the original product

nodes.label: Identity Crisis
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Obsessing over company name, logo, and branding to the detriment of product development. When a founder isn't comfortable with the company's identity, that ambivalence becomes contagious, seeping into every aspect of the business. The fix: the 80% = 100% rule—stop waiting to be 100% in love; 80% satisfied is good enough.
warning_signs: Endless debates over company name
No business cards made because identity isn't 'final'
Weekly meetings consumed by branding discussions
Employees feeling uneasy about company's future due to identity ambivalence

nodes.label: Overcorrecting Success and Failure
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The pattern where first-time success leads to overconfidence, causing second venture to underperform (sophomore slump). Research by Rasmus Toft-Kehler at Copenhagen Business School analyzing 65,390 startups found: second startup significantly underperforms first; third fares no better than first; fourth finally exceeds the debut. The fix: allow yourself to practice—failure breeds success.
warning_signs: Assuming next venture will be as easy as the first success
Crushing pressure on second product after first hit
Giving up after first failure instead of trying again
Not learning transferable lessons from each venture

nodes.label: Overzealous Outsourcing
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Outsourcing core functions too early or to the wrong parties. Increases cost to acquire clients and creates communication/quality problems. Without a technical founder, feedback-driven adjustments take more time and money. The fix: outsource only non-core functions after the business is on solid footing.
warning_signs: Outsourcing core product development
No technical founder on team
Outsourcing to cut costs on critical functions
Remote teams without clear communication structures

nodes.label: Not Stealing from Giants
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Refusing to learn from and build upon existing solutions, tools, and ideas. Some founders attempt to reinvent everything from scratch rather than using existing platforms and partnerships. The fix: mentor up—ask existing successful companies in mirror markets for advice; adapt existing solutions with at least 5% alteration.
quotes: 'If I have seen further, it's by standing on the shoulders of giants.' —Sir Isaac Newton
warning_signs: Reinventing wheels that already exist
Refusing to partner with complementary companies
Building everything in-house when off-the-shelf exists
Not learning from competitors in mirror markets

nodes.label: The Flawed Founding Team
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Assembling a founding team with mismatched skills, values, or goals. The best teams are complementary in nature rather than carbon copies. Two types: young/skilled/Ramen-eating vs. older/experienced/well-funded. Wrong type for your needs drains resources. The fix: take stock early and often—ensure everyone moves in the same direction with clearly outlined responsibilities and KPIs.
warning_signs: Team too senior/expensive for the venture's needs
No one with industry-specific experience
Founders with no shared work history
Financial responsibilities left to someone unqualified

nodes.label: The Missing Co-Founder
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Going solo without a co-founder. Creates lack of accountability, makes it too easy to let yourself off the hook, and leaves skill gaps unfilled. Most companies established by a single founder suffer from this fundamental handicap. The fix: board-up, partner-up, or both—write a job description for ideal partner, network to find them, look for complementary skills.
warning_signs: Solo founder with no accountability partner
No one to challenge decisions
Easily letting deadlines slip
Physical/mental/emotional overwhelm from doing everything alone

nodes.label: Friends and Family On Board
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Starting a business with friends, significant others, or family members. The 90% startup failure rate combined with relationship stress often destroys both the business and the relationship. When work partner = life partner, the line between work and home disappears. The fix: don't mix business with pleasure—ask friends for recommendations, not to join you.
warning_signs: Founding with spouse/partner
Hiring friends from school
Family members as co-founders with no business track record together
Work conversations dominating home life

nodes.label: Rush to the Altar
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Selecting the wrong co-founder(s)—worse than having none, and one of the most costly decisions in business. The co-founder relationship is like a marriage and shouldn't be entered into lightly. You need to 'date'—carefully vet prospects, get to know them well, refuse to rush. The fix: go slow—learn their work ethic, conflict style, common interests, and negotiate roles including who is CEO before committing.
warning_signs: Partnering quickly without working together first
No discussion of conflict resolution styles
No documentation of roles and responsibilities
Disagreement on major milestones and KPIs

nodes.label: Hiring Freeze
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Not hiring when you should. While hiring too quickly is fatal financially, not hiring is also a huge problem. Having at least one employee forces structure, accountability, and brings different perspectives. The fix: hire when ready—as soon as you have the basic framework, hire someone for a component you can't do yourself.
warning_signs: Founder doing everything alone
No employees despite available budget
Endless debate without execution
No external perspective on decisions

nodes.label: Horrible Hiring
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Not all hires are good hires. A single wrong hire can mess up everything—dampen morale, make colleagues uncomfortable, drop key tasks. Hire for attitude, not aptitude. The fix: use compressed interview format (15-20 minutes each, with 10 minutes between for notes) plus personality assessments like Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI).
warning_signs: Rushed interviews without proper vetting
No personality/culture-fit assessment
Hiring for skills only, ignoring attitude
Misleading candidates about their status in the hiring process

nodes.label: Hiring (White, Heterosexual) Men
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Lacking diversity is bad for business. 90% of capital raised by European tech startups went to teams without any female founders (per Louise Lachmann). Diverse teams outperform market benchmarks and provide stronger returns. Companies with more women in senior positions have significantly higher returns. The fix: diversification develops dividends—search out talented people from any walk of life, gender, age, or identity.
warning_signs: All-male founding team with no diverse perspectives
Product designed for only one gender's experience
No women in senior positions
Hiring from within narrow personal network only

nodes.label: Firing Phobia
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Fear of firing employees who aren't the right fit or are unaffordable. Founders drag their feet to avoid causing pain, but keeping a bad fit is bad business. Two reasons to fire: (1) not the right fit, (2) can't afford them. The fix: use the Ground Zero Exercise every 6-12 months to evaluate organizational structure from scratch.
warning_signs: Keeping underperforming employees out of guilt
Avoiding difficult conversations
Couching firing in too many compliments (leaves room for misinterpretation)
Team morale suffering from one bad apple

nodes.label: Awful Accountability
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Ignoring accountability in small companies. If you don't see the task as yours and aren't ready to take full responsibility, you aren't accountable. Especially dangerous with remote teams. The fix: what gets measured gets done—weekly video meetings, track commitments and performance, measure meeting cost (time × hourly rates).
warning_signs: Missed deadlines with no consequences
Remote workers without regular check-ins
Founder letting themselves 'off the hook' easily
No clear ownership of tasks

nodes.label: The Forever CEO
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Assuming the founder will be CEO forever. Even Bill Gates stepped down. Some founders excel at the sprint but can't handle the marathon. The fix: self-fire when the time is right—recognize when you're no longer the best person to lead. Three requirements for CEO per Ben Horowitz: (1) Leadership (articulate vision, right ambition, achieve vision), (2) Burning desire to build something great, (3) Emotional and physical resilience.
warning_signs: Company outgrowing founder's skills
Burnout affecting health and relationships
Board losing confidence but founder won't step aside
No succession plan

nodes.label: Lacking Sales Talent
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Having no sales department or assigning sales to non-salespeople (project managers, customer support, CEO). Getting a 'yes' mentality leads to over-customization, inflated CPO (Cost Per Order), and non-scalable processes. Sales should be at the core of the business. The fix: hire at least one trained salesperson.
warning_signs: No designated sales personnel
CEO/founder doing all sales
No formal sales training in the organization
Sales treated as an afterthought

nodes.label: Missing Sales Metrics
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Not measuring conversions in the sales funnel. What gets measured gets done. Measure everything: cold call conversion, in-person meetings, email campaigns, website visitors, session length, signup conversion. The fix: track your data—know what's happening now, this very moment.
warning_signs: Sales goals set too far in the future without interim tracking
No visibility into conversion rates at each funnel stage
Not measuring which channels produce results
Making decisions without sales data

nodes.label: Non-Structured Sales
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: No well-established sales structure—no CRM, no sales funnel, no pipeline. Leads to yo-yo sales (intense work punctuated by dry spells) and seasonal traps. Only ~50% of companies with <10 employees use a CRM. The fix: implement a CRM system and a structured sales pipeline with five stages.
warning_signs: No CRM system in use
Yo-yo sales cycles (feast or famine)
Same person doing cold calls, meetings, proposals, closing, and follow-up
Relying on memory or paper for customer relationships

nodes.label: The Flawed Product-Market Fit
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Selling to the wrong audience—either targeting mass-market with high-end product, or high-end with cheap offering. Not all buyers are created equal. Marc Andreessen calls this one of the top factors in startup investment decisions. A bad-fit customer won't give valuable feedback, wastes time on support, asks for discounts, and hurts through negative word-of-mouth. The fix: reinvent or retool—remake the product for a new audience, or enhance/re-price for the current one.
warning_signs: Selling to anyone who shows interest
High churn rate from bad-fit customers
Product requires extensive customization per customer
Rich customers wanting cheap product or vice versa

nodes.label: Product Pleasing
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Bending over backward to accommodate picky customers on a case-by-case basis. Taking money for customization creates short-term cash influx but loses 3x that amount in the big picture by preventing the company from perfecting its standard product. The fix: validate the business you have—sell what's on your shelves, not what each individual customer demands.
warning_signs: Customizing product for every new customer
Taking money to build features for one client
No standard product offering
Six markets with forty different customer types

nodes.label: Building Sales on a Weak Product
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Pushing sales for a product that isn't market-ready. Distinction between mosquito-bite problems (annoying but not painful enough to build a company around) and shark-bite problems (severe, customers desperate to solve). Think headache pills, not vitamin pills. The fix: don't jump the gun—test until market-ready, don't sell what you don't have on the shelf.
warning_signs: Product solving a mosquito-bite rather than shark-bite problem
Adding features to a fundamentally broken product
Pre-sales before product is ready
Doubling down on defective products rather than halting sales

nodes.label: Pricing Predicaments
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Pricing mistakes that eat into reputation or profits. Understanding whether your market uses cost-plus pricing (breakeven cost + margin percentage) or value-based pricing (set by market threshold, not production cost). The fix: know your market motivations, know your actual costs (breakeven), whether to offer payment options, and stay alert to market fluctuations.
warning_signs: Pricing that ignores decades of customer conditioning
Charging far above market threshold (Juicero)
No understanding of cost-plus vs. value-based pricing
Not knowing actual breakeven cost

nodes.label: Over-Promise, Under-Deliver
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Making promises to get people excited and gain buy-in, then failing to deliver. One of the fastest ways to ruin a startup. The fix: under-promise and over-deliver—drum up excitement but keep expectations reasonable and possible to exceed. Set proper expectations by knowing your data.
warning_signs: Hyped AI/technology claims that are actually basic features
Promising luxury experiences on startup budgets
Claims not backed by working product
Ethical shortcuts to close deals

nodes.label: Rejecting Revenue
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Ignoring revenue while focusing on building a 'potentially fantastic' product. The dot-com mentality of measuring business by burn rates. Profitability is a startup's ultimate goal. The fix: create a revenue model—a plan outlining all ways to bring profits into the business, regularly evaluated and revised.
warning_signs: 'Our whole strategy is to spend more than we make'
Measuring business by burn rate
No revenue model created
Assuming revenue will magically appear later

nodes.label: Poor Pivoting
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Pivoting is a double-edged sword. For young startups, pivoting is typically a f*ckup. But not pivoting (typically mature companies) can be equally bad. Pivoting should be based on solid market research, sticking to core competencies, and targeting the same audience—not abandoning everything. The fix: spin slowly—give the company time, test with MVP, keep communication open.
warning_signs: Pivoting away from what's working
Abandoning core competency for a 'hot' market
Refusing to pivot when market has clearly shifted
Pivoting without market research or MVP

nodes.label: Wrong-Work Distractions
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Not knowing where to focus time and energy. Four identified distraction types: (1) Clingy clients (high-maintenance, low-return), (2) Lost in learning land (analysis paralysis—always learning, never doing), (3) Neurotic logo/web design (perfecting minor details while product suffers), (4) Meaningless meetings (meetings to discuss meetings). The fix: provide instant gratification—focus only on jobs that matter.
warning_signs: Majority of time on clingy clients with little return
Endless learning without execution
Perfecting logos/websites instead of product
Meetings that could be emails

nodes.label: Bad Decisions
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Poor choices rank high on the list of reasons for failed startups. Even a correct decision is wrong when made at the wrong time. The fix: use mental models for better decision-making—Loss Aversion, Availability Heuristics, Anchoring, Confirmation Bias, Survivorship Bias.
warning_signs: Quibbling over price and killing acquisition deals
No sales capability in founding team
Making decisions based on hope rather than data
Cognitive biases driving strategy

nodes.label: Overcomplicating Operations
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Complexity is often in direct opposition to growth. Shoehorning corporate management tools into small startups alienates employees and wastes time. The fix: from super-complicated to Supercell—organize into independent cells/teams, management supports rather than micromanages, no counting hours or micro-measuring output.
warning_signs: Implementing corporate management frameworks in small startups
Micromanaging creative employees
Measuring things that don't need measuring
High employee turnover from stifling culture

nodes.label: Growing Too Fast
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Growing pains are real. Expanding too aggressively into new markets, over-hiring, and over-spending on infrastructure. Premature scaling signals: aggressive expansion, hiring before leaders mature, building own data centers, losing customer-first mentality. The fix: make haste slowly (festina lente)—focus on natural growth, keep core values and customer focus, don't let VC pressure drive premature scaling.
warning_signs: Aggressive geographic/store expansion sucking accounts dry
Hiring recent grads and promoting before they mature as leaders
Building own infrastructure instead of using cloud services
Workforce reduction of 18%+ after over-hiring
Tension, frustration, abandonment replacing startup buzz

nodes.label: Giving Up Too Soon
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Succumbing to mental fatigue and throwing in the towel before the company has a chance to prove itself. Startup life stress has led multiple founders to suicide. The fix: stay on target—great companies are built from sweat, tears, and perseverance. Research shows three years is the average time to show significant revenue.
warning_signs: Quitting within months due to stress/low pay
Not giving the business 'a fair shot'
Comparing early struggles to others' visible success
Mental health deteriorating without support

nodes.label: Not Knowing When to Quit
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A fate worse than failing: not quitting when you should. 'Stale startups' stuck in limbo between meager profit and bankruptcy drain years of life. Not quitting can be worse than bankruptcy. The fix: set goals and deadlines—if company repeatedly fails to meet them, quitting is the wise choice.
warning_signs: Years in limbo between meager profit and bankruptcy
Unable to scale due to fundamental business model issues
Eleven consecutive quarterly losses
Market has moved on but company hasn't

nodes.label: Not Believing the Future Is Predictable
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Failing to believe it's possible to predict the future. Companies that don't even try to look ahead miss massive shifts. The fix: use the Johari Window at the 3 Ps of the Future—Known/Knowns, Known/Unknowns, Unknown/Knowns, Unknown/Unknowns, plus Provable, Probable, and Possible futures.
warning_signs: Ignoring innovations invented in-house
Refusing to cannibalize own products for new technology
Missing obvious market shifts
No systematic future-planning process

nodes.label: The Future Is Certain but Unwritten
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Making basic future predictions but missing the target, failing to follow up, or never reevaluating. Future analysis should be as routine as any operating procedure. The fix: use VUCA framework (Volatility, Uncertainty, Complexity, Ambiguity) and PESTLE (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental) to stay on top of trends.
warning_signs: One-time future analysis never revisited
Expert predictions proven catastrophically wrong
Dismissing new technologies as irrelevant
Educated incapacity—detailed knowledge missing the bigger picture

nodes.label: Business Model Canvas
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A visual tool developed by Alexander Osterwalder to map out business concepts. Single-page document with nine parts: left side covers inner workings (partners, activities, resources, cost), right side covers external (value propositions, customer relationships, channels, customer segments, revenue streams). First draft in 15-30 minutes. Part of the iterative planning approach.

nodes.label: Porter's Five Forces
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Michael Porter's framework for analyzing industry attractiveness: (1) Threat of new entrants, (2) Threat of substitutes, (3) Bargaining power of customers, (4) Bargaining power of suppliers, (5) Competitive rivalry. Warren Buffett calls this a 'moat' surrounding a castle—well-performing companies need a deep and wide moat. Complemented by Brandenburger and Nalebuff's sixth force: Complementors.

nodes.label: Porter's Generic Strategies
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Three strategies for competing: (1) Cost Leadership—be the cheapest in the target market (or perceived as best price-to-value ratio). Problem: only one player can be cheapest. (2) Differentiation—stand out on design, features, usability, time-to-market, support. Example: Volvo (safety) vs. BMW (driving experience). (3) Focus—narrow to a particular market segment and service it better than anyone. Apply cost focus or differentiation focus within the segment.

nodes.label: Tier 1 Problem
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Coined by BigCommerce co-founder Mitchell Harper. A Tier 1 problem is painful enough for the target audience to care enough about solving. Think headache pills, not vitamin pills. Tier 1 problems affect the largest possible market share. Non-Tier-1 problems (mosquito-bite problems) are annoying but not worth building a company around.

nodes.label: Shark-Bite vs. Mosquito-Bite Problem
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A shark-bite problem is severe—customers are desperate to solve it. A mosquito-bite problem is annoying but not painful enough to build a company around. Think headache pills (shark-bite), not vitamin pills (mosquito-bite). Related to Tier 1 problems.

nodes.label: MoSCoW Method
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Prioritization method developed by Dai Clegg at Oracle in 1994 for Rapid Application Development. Four categories: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have. Used with timeboxing (fix deadline, adjust functionality). Ideal for developing MVP.

nodes.label: The 10x Rule / Antifragile Rule
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Inspired by Nassim Nicholas Taleb. Multiply all goals by ten. Once you've determined how much you need in sales to pay bills, multiply by ten. Develop improvements to increase revenue tenfold. Includes both effort (10x harder) and desired result. Like a runner's bones getting stronger under stress, your business thrives on chaos if properly braced. If you can't find a way to 10x revenue, don't start the business—it's too fragile.

nodes.label: 80% = 100% Rule
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Stop waiting to be 100% in love with a decision (brand identity, logo, etc.). 80% satisfied is as good as 100%. Any decision is better than no decision. Applies to identity decisions, hiring doubts (if in doubt whether to fire, then fire), and any decision-making process.

nodes.label: Ground Zero Exercise
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A 4-step organizational audit run every 6-12 months: (1) Imagine you fire everyone—identify what's left of value, (2) Define ideal but realistic roles on Post-it Notes, (3) Compare the fiction to current reality, (4) Implement—remove redundant resources, hire for missing vital roles. 'C'est tout. You now have a trimmed and talented startup.'

nodes.label: Not-To-Do List
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A list where all ideas that pop into your head daily go for safekeeping. Satisfying to get them on paper and acknowledge without acting. Only ~20% are worth returning to. The other 80% are good but not right to pursue.

nodes.label: Sales Pipeline (5 Steps)
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The five steps every sales pipeline boils down to: (1) Target a potential client, (2) Prospect their qualifications (including budget), (3) Entertain a sales meeting, (4) Send a proposal, (5) Close the deal. 'Coffee is for closers.' Used by sales team.

nodes.label: Sales Funnel (ToFu, MoFu, BoFu)
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Visual representation of customer journey from first introduction to purchase. Top-Funnel (ToFu) → Middle-Funnel (MoFu) → Bottom-Funnel (BoFu). Wide at top, narrow at bottom. Designed by marketing team. Shows conversion and drop-off at each stage. Activity needed at all levels at all times to avoid stop-go sales.

nodes.label: Iterative Planning Approach (5 Steps)
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Step-by-step planning process: (1) Develop Business Model Canvas (15-30 min), (2) Expand with one-page business plan (15-30 min), (3) Make a budget (2-4 hours), (4) Make a project plan (2-4 hours), (5) Wrap into a business plan (1 day). In this specific order, you tackle the most important items first, catching issues early before investing more energy.

nodes.label: Johari Window (Modified for Business)
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Originally by psychologists Joseph Luft and Harrington Ingham (1955). Applied to business blind spots: four quadrants—Known/Knowns (aware and understand), Known/Unknowns (aware but don't understand), Unknown/Knowns (understand but not aware—Black Swans), Unknown/Unknowns (neither aware nor understand). Only the fourth quadrant is truly unpredictable. Famous from Donald Rumsfeld's 'known unknowns.'

nodes.label: The 3 Ps of the Future
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Framework for future prediction: (1) Provable—events predictable with certainty (technology will improve), (2) Probable—likely based on current knowledge (self-driving cars from AI growth), (3) Possible—might happen based on what we know (flying self-driving cars). Start from the certain and build outward. Most people take the backward approach starting from uncertainty and spiral.

nodes.label: VUCA Framework
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Developed by US military, drawing on Warren Bennis and Burt Nanus. Stands for Volatility (exponential change, shorter cycles), Uncertainty (difficult to predict outcomes), Complexity (creates more options), Ambiguity (Unknown/Unknowns). Takes the opposite approach to the 3 Ps—starts with the most uncertain. Alternative: TUNA (Turbulent, Uncertain, Novel, Ambiguous) by Rafael Ramirez and Angela Wilkinson at Oxford.

nodes.label: PESTLE Framework
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Developed as ETPS by Francis J. Aguilar in 1965. Factors for future prediction: Political (current political climate affecting industry), Economic (exchange rates, inflation, interest rates, growth), Social (population, demographics, career attitudes, health consciousness), Technological (tech issues impeding market entry, R&D limitations), Legal (laws, employment law, consumer law, antitrust), Environmental (weather, climate, natural disasters). Download canvas at kimhvidkjaer.com.

nodes.label: Reasoning by Analogy
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Quick and simple decision-making tool. Compare your situation to a similar situation with similar parameters another company faced. Must be apples-to-apples comparison. Useful when relevant analogy cases exist.

nodes.label: Reasoning by First Principle
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Traced back to Aristotle, popularized by Elon Musk. Dig down to the smallest known true component of a situation and build up from there. Start with what is proven to be true. Works best for complicated frameworks where analogy cases may not exist or be relevant.

nodes.label: Wissner-Gross Intelligence Equation
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Dr. Alex Wissner-Gross's equation: F = T ∆ Sτ. Intelligence is a force (F) trying to maximize future freedom of action (keeping options open), with some strength (T), with the diversity of possible accessible futures (S), up to some future time horizon (τ). In short: intelligence doesn't like to get trapped. The goal for startups when predicting possible futures.

nodes.label: Ben Horowitz's CEO Requirements
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Two required characteristics for CEO per Ben Horowitz/Andreessen Horowitz: (1) Leadership—ability to articulate vision, have the right kind of ambition, and ability to achieve the vision. (2) Desire—burning, irrepressible urge to build something great and willingness to do whatever it takes. Hvidkjaer adds third: emotional and physical resilience.

nodes.label: Level Up Mindset
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Replace 'f*ck up' with 'level up.' Failure is not failure—it's progression. Every startup attempt develops skills: creating business models, developing products, getting funding, selling to customers, building teams. 'You did not fail; you progressed.' Next time, likelihood of going all the way is much greater.
quotes: 'What you've done is complete several levels.'
'Inhale the future, exhale the past.'

nodes.label: Skin in the Game
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: American investors' term for founders risking their own money. Danish equivalent: 'Put your hand on the stove'—proving willingness to take the heat. Bootstrapping demonstrates commitment. This will pay off when seeking outside capital later.

nodes.label: Smart Money
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Investment that comes not just with capital but also with a network, knowledge in the startup world, and easier access to more capital later. The goal when taking outside investment.

nodes.label: Mindless Acceptance Syndrome
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Named by David Grady. The tendency to accept meeting invites without thinking, because it's easier than thinking for oneself. Contributes to meaningless meetings.

nodes.label: Twinning
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Using CRM data to find similar customers to approach. Example: if LEGO was interested in 5 sales opportunities, the CRM suggests Disney and Epic as similar prospects with contact information. Good CRM systems look at the big picture, not just micromanagement.

nodes.label: Stale Startup
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A company stuck in limbo between making a meager profit and going bankrupt. Pouring all energy in but never really going anywhere. In this case, not quitting is worse than bankruptcy.

nodes.label: Yo-Yo Sales
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Unpredictable revenue cycles where periods of intense work (serving existing clients) alternate with dry spells (seeking new clients). Happens when sales efforts stop once work is secured. A vicious repeating cycle causing inconsistent revenue.

nodes.label: Stop-Go Sales
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Fits and starts of intense work punctuated by dry spells lasting months or years. Caused by not keeping the sales funnel fed at all levels at all times. Leads to stress on both sides—either overworked or fretting—and eventual bankruptcy.

nodes.label: Single Point of Failure (SPOF)
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: A part of a system that, if it fails, halts the entire system. In business: one client, one platform, one distribution channel, one employee with too much unsupervised power. Examples: Barings Bank (one rogue trader), Lookery (Facebook dependency), Epic/Fortnite (App Store dependency).

nodes.label: If-Then Statements for Business Planning
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Building conditional scenarios into the business plan. Example: 'If product launches and the internet dies, how will you get it in front of consumers?' Creates flexibility and preparation for unforeseeable futures. Companies using If-Then statements make margaritas and lemon squares, not just lemonade.

nodes.label: Meeting Cost Calculation
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Quick calculation to determine if a meeting justifies its expense: time spent × employees' hourly rates in attendance. If 10 people attend for 40 minutes, the meeting must produce value exceeding that cost. Helps fight Mindless Acceptance Syndrome and ensures only essential attendees are invited.

nodes.label: Compressed Interview Format
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: 15-20 minute interviews with 10 minutes between for notes. Candidates informed about company in advance and complete MBTI personality assessment before interview. Founder knows in first 10-15 minutes if candidate fits. Result: 10 candidates in 5 hours flat.

nodes.label: Dating Your Co-Founder
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: The process of thoroughly vetting a potential co-founder before committing. Like romantic dating: learn work ethic, conflict style, skills, common interests outside work. Explore whether you can stand how they chew their food. Only after personal fit is established do you negotiate and document roles including who is CEO.

nodes.label: Festina Lente (Make Haste Slowly)
nodes.source_file: How to F_ck Up Your Startup - Kim Hvidkjaer.pdf
nodes.rationale: Originally recorded in Greek, this Latin phrase means 'make haste slowly.' In startup growth: focus on natural growth, be thinking about expansion but wait for it to happen naturally. Don't rush scaling.

nodes.label: Technology Adoption Life Cycle
quotes: the original research that gave rise to this model was done on the adoption of new strains of seed potatoes among American farmers

nodes.label: Innovators (Technology Enthusiasts / Techies)
quotes: Real techies don't need whole products.
Enthusiasts are like kindling: They help start the fire.

nodes.label: Early Adopters (Visionaries)
quotes: Visionaries are that rare breed of people who have the insight to match an emerging technology to a strategic opportunity.
Visionaries are easy to sell but very hard to please. This is because they are buying a dream—which, to some degree, will always be a dream.
Visionaries are not looking for an improvement; they are looking for a fundamental breakthrough.

nodes.label: Early Majority (Pragmatists)
quotes: Pragmatists evaluate and buy whole products.
Pioneers are people with arrows in their backs.
Pragmatists want to buy from proven market leaders because they know that third parties will design supporting products around a market-leading product.
The word risk is a negative word in their vocabulary—it does not connote opportunity or excitement but rather the chance to waste money and time.

nodes.label: Late Majority (Conservatives)
quotes: Conservatives, in essence, are against discontinuous innovations. They believe far more in tradition than in progress.
They want high-tech products to be like refrigerators—you open the door, the light comes on automatically, your food stays cold, and you don't have to think about it.

nodes.label: Laggards (Skeptics)
quotes: Skeptics—the group that makes up the last one-sixth of the Technology Adoption Life Cycle—do not participate in the high-tech marketplace, except to block purchases.
Steamrolling over the skeptics may be a great sales tactic, but it is a poor marketing one.

nodes.label: The High-Tech Marketing Model
quotes: This, in essence, is the High-Tech Marketing Model—a vision of a smooth unfolding through all the stages of the Technology Adoption Life Cycle.

nodes.label: The Chasm
quotes: The point of greatest peril in the development of a high-tech market lies in making the transition from an early market dominated by a few visionary customers to a mainstream market dominated by a large block of customers who are predominantly pragmatists in orientation.

What we have here is a catch-22. The only suitable reference for an early majority customer, it turns out, is another member of the early majority, but no upstanding member of the early majority will buy without first having consulted with several suitable references.

nodes.label: First Crack in the Bell Curve (Innovators → Early Adopters)
quotes: If the marketing effort is unable to find that compelling application, then market development stalls with the innovators, and the future of the product falls through the crack.

nodes.label: Second Crack in the Bell Curve (Early Majority → Late Majority)
quotes: When a product reaches this point in the market development, it must be made increasingly easier to adopt in order to continue being successful.

nodes.label: Four Characteristics of Visionaries That Alienate Pragmatists
D-Day Analogy for Chasm Crossing
quotes: Replicate D Day, and win entry to the mainstream. Cross the chasm by targeting a very specific niche market where you can dominate from the outset, force your competitors out of that market niche, and then use it as a base for broader operations.
The D-Day strategy keeps everyone on point—if we don't take Normandy, we don't have to worry about how we're going to take Paris.

nodes.label: Beachhead Segment
quotes: Pick on somebody your own size.
When you do pick, you pick hard. That is, you go hard in the direction chosen, regardless of doubts.

nodes.label: The Bowling Alley Metaphor
quotes: The bowling pin model allows you both to focus on the immediate market, keeping the burn rate down and the market development effort targeted, while still keeping in view the larger win.

nodes.label: The Tornado Phase
quotes: When markets go mass, platforms have the advantage.

nodes.label: Main Street Phase
quotes: the best service, both from the point of view of convenience to the customer and low cost to the vendor, is no service at all.

nodes.label: The Whole Product Model
quotes: There is a gap between the marketing promise made to the customer—the compelling value proposition—and the ability of the shipped product to fulfill that promise.
Pragmatists evaluate and buy whole products.

nodes.label: Whole Product Commitment Principle
quotes: Failure to meet this promise in a business-to-business market has extremely serious consequences.

nodes.label: Compelling Reason to Buy
quotes: If pragmatists can live with the problem for another year, they will.

nodes.label: Never Attack a Fortified Hill
quotes: Never attack a fortified hill.

nodes.label: High-Risk, Low-Data Decision
quotes: informed intuition, rather than analytical reason, is the most trustworthy decision-making tool to use.
It will be a billion-dollar market in 1995. If we only get 5 percent of that market... When you hear that sort of stuff, exit gracefully, holding on to your wallet.

nodes.label: Informed Intuition
quotes: Only work with memorable images.

nodes.label: Creating the Competition
quotes: Competition, therefore, becomes a fundamental condition for purchase.
there is no competition, there is no market.

nodes.label: The Competitive-Positioning Compass
quotes: Crossing the chasm requires moving from an environment of support among the visionaries back into one of skepticism among the pragmatists.

nodes.label: Positioning (Four Principles and Four Stages)
quotes: Positioning is the single largest influence on the buying decision.
Companies focus on making products easier to sell because that is what they are worried about—selling. But the correct goal is to make them easier to buy.

nodes.label: Distribution-Oriented Pricing (for Chasm Crossing)
quotes: during the chasm period, the number-one concern of pricing is not to satisfy the customer or to satisfy the investors, but to motivate the channel.

nodes.label: High-Tech Distribution Channel Types
quotes: The number-one corporate objective, when crossing the chasm, is to secure a channel into the mainstream market with which the pragmatist customer will be comfortable. This objective comes before revenues, before profits, before press, even before customer satisfaction.

nodes.label: Direct Sales as Optimal Chasm-Crossing Channel
Retail Channel Limitations for Chasm Crossing
Hockey Stick Revenue Forecast (vs. Staircase Model)
quotes: the revenue development that actually occurs looks more like a staircase than a hockey stick.

nodes.label: Valley of Death (Venture Capital Term for Chasm)
Pioneers vs. Settlers (Organizational Transition)
quotes: They are the ones primarily responsible for dragging companies back into the chasm.
This is a time when you must perform impeccably.

nodes.label: Whole Product R&D
quotes: It prefers to assemble its creations from existing technologies and products rather than to invent new ones from scratch.
There's gold in them thar hills.

nodes.label: Word of Mouth as #1 Information Source
quotes: in the high-tech buying process, word of mouth is the number one source of information buyers reference

nodes.label: Market Definition (Self-Referencing Criterion)
quotes: No company can afford to pay for every marketing contact made. Every program must rely on some ongoing chain-reaction effects—what is usually called word of mouth.

nodes.label: Sales-Driven vs. Market-Driven (Chasm Fatal Error)
quotes: The consequences of being sales-driven during the chasm period are, to put it simply, fatal.
We do not have, nor are we willing to adopt, any discipline that would ever require us to stop pursuing any sale at any time for any reason.

nodes.label: The Zen Proverb of High-Tech Markets
Microsoft as the Exception (Not a Chasm-Crossing Model)
quotes: Microsoft from day one was operating in a context of being a de facto standard. It was born inside a tornado of demand that IBM created.
Great cases make bad law.

nodes.label: Applications vs. Platforms in Chasm Crossing
quotes: Platforms, by their very nature, pay off when they achieve ubiquity, and typically not before.
To accelerate the adoption of platforms, vendors must clothe them in applications clothing.

nodes.label: Marketing is Warfare, Not Wordfare
quotes: marketing is warfare—not wordfare.

nodes.label: KISS Philosophy (Keep It Simple, Stupid) — Whole Product
The Doughnut Diagram (Whole Product Communication Tool)
Early Market Dynamics (Good and Bad Scenarios)
Good Fences Make Good Neighbors (Niche Boundaries)
quotes: Good fences make good neighbors.

nodes.label: Crossing the Chasm vs. Lean Startup / Customer Development
The High-Tech Parable (Entrepreneurial Experience Gone Awry)
Post-Chasm Enterprise Bound by Pre-Chasm Commitments
When to Spend Capital (Chasm vs. Early Market)
Market as a Real Entity (Not an Illusion)
Whole Product Importance Increases Left to Right in TALC
quotes: Real techies don't need whole products.

nodes.label: The Gap: Marketing Promise vs. Shipped Product
Commitment to Focus as Critical Success Factor
Size of First Pin Is Not the Issue

hyperedges.label: Problem-First Validation Pipeline
hyperedges.relation: All converge on starting with real customer problems (not solutions), validating through direct conversation, and understanding the job the customer is hiring for.
All advocate starting with a narrow, deep market (few users who desperately want the product) before expanding — the fundamental shape of successful startup go-to-market.

All involve deliberately shaping how the market perceives a new solution — from creating entire new categories (Play Bigger), to positioning within existing ones (Dunford), to building complete solutions for pragmatists (Moore), to noticing gaps from the future (Graham).

hyperedges.label: PMF Convergence Zone
hyperedges.description: Andreessen's PMF definition, Seibel's overwhelmed-with-demand signals, Sean Ellis's 40% rule, and Superhuman's PMF engine converge on the same core concept: PMF is measurable (40%+ very disappointed), experiential (constraint is capacity not demand), and optimizable (segment, double down, iterate). These four frameworks represent the evolution of PMF from concept to operationalization.

hyperedges.label: Go-to-Market System (Bansal Synthesis)
hyperedges.description: Product-Market-Sales Fit integrates the sandwich strategy (bottoms-up + tops-down), a 3-phase sales learning curve, a 2/3–1/3 engineering allocation, 10–15% services sweet spot, and stage-specific pricing. Together these form a complete system where product features, sales motion, engineering investment, pricing, and services align.

hyperedges.label: Seibel Unified Early-Stage Framework
hyperedges.description: Seibel's Order of Operations, Pre-Launch Sequence, User Conversation Method, and Screwdriver Analogy form a coherent system: 10 steps from problem discovery to validation, 4 pre-launch goals, a structured conversation method, and the screwdriver metaphor for iteration. All anchor on the same principle: hold problem/customer tight, solution loose.
